gravitational_potential.how_it_works: Gravitational potential determines how bodies move and light propagates. In general relativity, a potential difference causes gravitational redshift: light loses energy as it climbs out of the "well", expressed mathematically as Δλ/λ = Δφ/c². This effect has been verified with high precision.
key_problems: The nonlocality of gravitational energy in GR makes it difficult to define potential for strong fields.
The potential does not explain galaxy rotation curves without introducing dark matter.
Singularities inside black holes indicate the limits of the classical description of potential.
gravitational_potential.fun_fact: Due to the difference in gravitational potential, time at sea level flows slightly slower than in the mountains. On GPS satellites, this must be constantly corrected, otherwise navigation would be off by tens of meters per day.

formulas.description: Newtonian gravitational potential of a point mass
formulas.meaning: φ — gravitational potential, G — gravitational constant, G ≈ 6.674×10⁻¹¹ m³/(kg·s²), M — mass of the body, r — distance from the center of the body

formulas.description: Poisson's equation for gravitational potential
formulas.meaning: Δ — Laplace operator, φ — gravitational potential, G — gravitational constant, G ≈ 6.674×10⁻¹¹ m³/(kg·s²), ρ — density of matter

critical_point.name: critical point
critical_point.description_popular: Imagine a kettle with boiling water. Normally, when water turns into steam, you can see bubbles and bubbling. But if you heat water under high pressure, you can reach a state where water and steam become completely indistinguishable — this is the critical point. At this point, liquid and gas merge into a single state, like a thick fog, but without a surface boundary. It's similar to how, in the morning, the air is so saturated with moisture that dew droplets and air merge into one whole.
critical_point.fun_fact_popular: Did you know that at the critical point, water can simultaneously wet and not wet the walls of the container? The difference between liquid and steam disappears, and surface tension vanishes, so the liquid stops forming droplets.
critical_point.description_simple: The critical point is a special temperature and pressure at which the difference between liquid and steam disappears. Think of air on a hot summer day: it can be so humid that you feel stuffiness, but there are no actual droplets yet. If you keep heating and compressing, eventually steam and liquid become indistinguishable. It's like a haze that neither drips nor rises. At the critical point, the substance becomes a supercritical fluid: it flows like a gas but dissolves substances like a liquid.
critical_point.history_simple: Thomas Andrews first discovered the critical point in 1861 while studying carbon dioxide. He noticed that above 31°C, the gas would not liquefy under any pressure. Later, Van der Waals developed the theory, linking it to molecular forces.
critical_point.how_it_works_simple: Under normal conditions, liquid molecules attract each other and stay together, while in a gas they fly apart. With increasing temperature and pressure, the kinetic energy of molecules rises, and intermolecular forces can no longer hold them in an ordered state. At the critical point, these forces become equal: the liquid ceases to be dense, and the gas ceases to be rarefied. It's like a crowd at a concert: when there are too many people and everyone is moving, you can't tell who is standing still and who is dancing—everyone blends into a homogeneous mass.
critical_point.description: The critical point is a thermodynamic state in which the distinctions between liquid and gas phases vanish. It is characterized by critical parameters: temperature T_c, pressure P_c, and volume V_c. Above the critical point, the substance exists as a supercritical fluid, possessing properties of both liquids and gases. The second-order phase transition at the critical point is accompanied by anomalies in heat capacity, compressibility, and correlation length, which are described by critical exponents and scaling theory. Near the critical point, critical opalescence and density fluctuations are observed, which are related to the universality of critical behavior of different systems.
critical_point.history: The critical point was discovered by Charles Cagniard de la Tour in 1822, but systematically studied by Thomas Andrews in 1869. Andrews showed that for each gas there is a temperature above which it cannot be liquefied. In 1873, Johannes van der Waals proposed an equation of state for real gas that for the first time explained the existence of a critical point based on molecular forces. In the 20th century, Landau developed the theory of second-order phase transitions, and Wilson applied renormalization group to describe universal critical phenomena.
critical_point.how_it_works: From a microscopic perspective, the critical point is the result of a balance between intermolecular attraction, which tends to condense the substance, and thermal motion, which tends to expand it. At the critical temperature, the attraction energy equals the thermal energy, and the phase boundary disappears. This is described by the van der Waals equation, which includes parameters a and b. At the critical point, compressibility tends to infinity, and the correlation length of fluctuations becomes macroscopic, leading to critical opalescence. In modern understanding, critical phenomena are universal and do not depend on system details, but are determined only by the dimensionality of space and the symmetry of the order parameter, as described by renormalization group theory.
key_problems: Complete description of critical exponents for all universality classes
Unification of the theory of critical phenomena with quantum effects at low temperatures
Prediction of critical points for complex liquids, such as ionic liquids or polymer solutions
Study of the critical point in nuclear matter (quark-gluon plasma phase)
critical_point.fun_fact: At the critical point, water becomes an excellent solvent for organic matter and can even dissolve glass. Supercritical water is used for eco-friendly dry cleaning and destruction of toxic waste.

formulas.description: Critical temperature in the van der Waals equation
formulas.meaning: T_c is the critical temperature; a is the van der Waals constant accounting for intermolecular attraction; b is the van der Waals constant accounting for the excluded volume of molecules; R is the universal gas constant (R ≈ 8.314 J/(mol·K))

formulas.description: Critical pressure in the van der Waals equation
formulas.meaning: P_c is the critical pressure; a and b are the van der Waals constants (a is a measure of attraction, b is a measure of repulsion/volume)

semiconductor.name: semiconductor
semiconductor.description_popular: A semiconductor is a material that can conduct electricity not always, but only when heated or illuminated. Imagine a drawbridge that is usually raised, but when a signal flag is raised (energy is given), it lowers and allows cars to pass. In a semiconductor, electrons sit in 'locked' places, but with a little heat or light, they gain enough strength to break free and turn the material into a conductor.
semiconductor.fun_fact_popular: Your smartphone and computer work thanks to billions of tiny semiconductor switches—transistors. If they were made of ordinary conductors, the phone would heat up like an iron and discharge in seconds!
semiconductor.description_simple: A semiconductor is a material with conductivity between a conductor and an insulator. It has a unique ability: its resistance drops sharply when heated or illuminated. Imagine a crowd of people on an escalator. In the normal state, everyone stands still—no current. But if given a little energy (a push), they start moving, and a flow arises. In a semiconductor, 'electrons' sit in the valence band, and to make them move, they need to be transferred to the conduction band across a forbidden band.
semiconductor.history_simple: The first experiments with semiconductors began in the 19th century: Faraday noticed that silver sulfide conducts current better when heated. In 1947, Bardeen, Brattain, and Shockley created the first germanium transistor. Since then, silicon has become the basis of electronics. Shuji Nakamura in the 1990s developed bright blue LEDs based on gallium nitride—a semiconductor—for which he received the Nobel Prize.
semiconductor.how_it_works_simple: In a semiconductor, electrons are in energy bands. There is a valence band, where electrons are tightly bound to atoms, and a conduction band, where they are free. Between them is a forbidden band. An electron can jump across it if it receives energy greater than the band gap width. This is like jumping over a ditch: without a running start (energy), it won't work. By adding impurities (doping), additional steps can be created to facilitate the jump—this is how transistors are made.
semiconductor.description: A semiconductor is a material whose resistivity at room temperature ranges from 10^-4 to 10^8 Ω·m, intermediate between conductors and dielectrics. The main property of semiconductors is the strong dependence of conductivity on temperature, illumination, electric field, and impurities. The electronic structure is described by band theory: the valence band and the conduction band are separated by a forbidden band of width E_g (for silicon ~1.1 eV). At zero temperature, a semiconductor is a dielectric. Conductivity appears when carriers (electrons and holes) are excited by heat or radiation. Doping with donor or acceptor impurities allows control of the type and concentration of carriers.
semiconductor.history: Semiconductor properties were first observed by Michael Faraday in 1833 in silver sulfide. In 1874, Karl Braun discovered the rectifying effect at a metal-semiconductor contact. The decisive breakthrough came in 1947 with the invention of the transistor at Bell Labs (Bardeen, Brattain, Shockley). Shuji Nakamura in 1993 made a breakthrough by creating a bright blue LED based on gallium nitride, opening the way to white LED lighting. For this achievement he was awarded the Nobel Prize.
semiconductor.how_it_works: The conductivity of a semiconductor is determined by the concentration and mobility of free charge carriers. In an intrinsic semiconductor at T>0, electrons transition from the valence band to the conduction band, leaving holes. The carrier concentration obeys the law of mass action: n_i^2 = N_c N_v exp(-E_g/kT). When doped, donors create levels near the conduction band, facilitating thermal ionization. The principle of operation of the p-n junction, the basis of diodes and transistors, relies on diffusion and drift of carriers in an electric field. An important role is played by Coulomb's law, which determines potential barriers, and the Schrödinger equation, which describes the wave functions of electrons in a periodic potential.
key_problems: Creation of semiconductors with high mobility at room temperature (e.g., based on graphene)
Control of defects in wide-bandgap semiconductors for UV optoelectronics
Development of semiconductors compatible with biological tissues for implantable devices
Search for materials for high-temperature electronics (silicon carbide, diamond)
semiconductor.fun_fact: Semiconductors are used not only in electronics but also in solar cells: photons of light knock electrons from the valence band into the conduction band, creating an electric current. The most common semiconductor—silicon—makes up almost a third of the Earth's crust, literally the sand under our feet!

formulas.description: Intrinsic carrier concentration in a semiconductor
formulas.meaning: n_i is the electron concentration (equal to the hole concentration) in an intrinsic semiconductor; N_c is the effective density of states in the conduction band; N_v is the effective density of states in the valence band; E_g is the band gap width; k is the Boltzmann constant (k ≈ 1.38×10^{-23} J/K); T is the temperature in kelvins

band_structure.name: band structure
band_structure.description_popular: Imagine that electrons in a solid are like bees in a large hive. They can only fly on certain 'floors' or 'combs'. These allowed levels are called bands. The band structure is a map showing which energy floors electrons can occupy, and where they are forbidden to appear—these are forbidden bands. It's like in a multi-story building, some floors are occupied, and others are completely empty.
band_structure.fun_fact_popular: Thanks to band structure, some materials shine like metals (they have no forbidden band), while others are transparent, like glass (the forbidden band is too large for visible light). That's why metals conduct electricity, and plastic doesn't.
band_structure.description_simple: Band structure is a graph of electron energy versus its momentum in a crystal. Due to the periodic arrangement of atoms, electrons can only occupy certain allowed bands, not arbitrary energies. Between them are forbidden bands. It's like a musical instrument: a string can only produce certain notes (fundamental tone and overtones), not any sound. So an electron in a crystal 'sounds' at certain energies.
band_structure.history_simple: The idea of band structure was first proposed by Felix Bloch in 1928, solving the Schrödinger equation for an electron in a periodic lattice field. Erwin Schrödinger, the creator of wave mechanics, thereby laid the foundations for this discovery.
band_structure.how_it_works_simple: In an isolated atom, electrons occupy discrete orbitals. When atoms come together to form a crystal, their orbitals overlap and split into many levels, forming almost continuous bands. The distance between atoms determines whether the bands will overlap or a forbidden band remains. It's like placing many identical tuning forks in a row: each gives one note, but together they create a whole chord with new frequencies.
band_structure.description: Band structure is the dispersion relation E(k) for electronic states in a periodic crystal potential. According to Bloch's theorem, the electron wave function has the form ψ_k(r)=e^{ik·r}u_k(r), where u_k is a periodic function. The energy spectrum consists of allowed bands separated by forbidden gaps. The band structure determines the electrical, optical, and thermal properties of solids. Metals have a partially filled conduction band, dielectrics have a completely filled valence band with a large band gap (E_g>3 eV), and semiconductors have a small band gap (E_g<3 eV). The complex behavior of E(k) near high-symmetry points in the Brillouin zone allows the introduction of the concept of effective mass.
band_structure.history: Felix Bloch in 1928, applying the Schrödinger equation to a periodic potential, derived the form of the wave function and laid the foundations of band theory. In the 1930s, Brillouin developed the concept of Brillouin zones. Later, W. Shockley, J. Bardeen, and others used band structure to explain semiconductor properties. Erwin Schrödinger, whose equation underlies the calculations, is a key figure.
band_structure.how_it_works: Band structure arises from solving the stationary Schrödinger equation for an electron in a periodic crystal potential V(r)=V(r+R). The energy spectrum E(k) is a multivalued function in reciprocal space. Near band extrema, the energy is often approximated by a quadratic dependence: E(k) ≈ (ħ^2/2m*)(k - k0)^2, where m* is the effective mass. According to the Pauli principle, electrons fill states from low energies up to the Fermi level. Optical properties are determined by direct and indirect transitions across the band gap with absorption or emission of photons, which is described by conservation of energy and momentum.
key_problems: Accurate calculation of the band structure of strongly correlated systems (e.g., high-temperature superconductors)
Prediction of the band structure of new materials (topological insulators) without complex calculations
Accounting for many-body interaction effects (excitons, polarons) in band calculations
Development of materials with a given band structure (band engineering) for photonics
band_structure.fun_fact: In graphene, the band structure has the shape of cones touching at a single point—this makes it a semimetal with unique properties, for the study of which the Nobel Prize was awarded in 2010.

formulas.description: Dispersion relation in the effective mass approximation (parabolic band)
formulas.meaning: E is the electron energy; k is the magnitude of the wave vector; ħ is the reduced Planck constant (ħ = h/(2π) ≈ 1.0546×10^{-34} J·s); m* is the effective mass of the electron in this band

cosmological_constant.name: cosmological constant
cosmological_constant.description_popular: Imagine an infinite balloon that slowly inflates by itself, without any help. The cosmological constant is that very 'spring' inside space that pushes galaxies farther and farther apart.
cosmological_constant.fun_fact_popular: Albert Einstein first introduced this concept to make the Universe static, and then called it his 'greatest blunder'—and today we know that the Universe is actually accelerating its expansion!
cosmological_constant.description_simple: It is a property of the vacuum itself: even in an absolutely empty place, where there are no particles or fields, there is some energy. It works like antigravity, causing space to expand.
cosmological_constant.history_simple: Einstein added the constant to his equations in 1917 so that the model of the Universe would not contract under gravity. After Hubble's discovery in 1929, it was considered unnecessary. But in 1998, observations of supernovae showed that the expansion of the Universe is accelerating—and the cosmological constant returned as the main candidate for dark energy.
cosmological_constant.how_it_works_simple: The cosmological constant is equivalent to negative pressure. While ordinary matter and radiation slow down expansion, vacuum energy 'pushes' it, and the more space appears, the stronger this effect becomes—like if from every new cubic centimeter a new spring appeared.
cosmological_constant.description: The cosmological constant Λ is a term in Einstein's general relativity equations, corresponding to the energy density of the vacuum. Modern observations indicate that it accounts for about 70% of the total energy of the Universe and is responsible for accelerated expansion.
cosmological_constant.history: Einstein introduced Λ in 1917 for a static model of the Universe. In the 1920s, Friedmann and Lemaître showed that solutions with and without Λ lead to expansion. After Hubble's discovery, Einstein abandoned it. At the end of the 20th century, two groups of astronomers (Perlmutter, Riess, Schmidt) discovered acceleration from distant supernovae, which resurrected Λ.
cosmological_constant.how_it_works: In Einstein's equations, the cosmological constant adds a term Λ g_{μν}. In a homogeneous and isotropic Universe, it manifests as an effective fluid with equation of state p = -ρ c^2, leading to repulsive gravity. In quantum field theory, the predicted vacuum energy is 120 orders of magnitude larger than observed—this is the cosmological constant problem.
key_problems: The cosmological constant problem: the enormous mismatch between the theoretical value of vacuum energy and observations (~10^120 times).
The coincidence of the current densities of dark energy and matter (the coincidence problem).
Is Λ a true constant or a dynamic field (quintessence)?
cosmological_constant.fun_fact: If the cosmological constant were slightly larger, galaxies could never have formed. Slightly smaller—the Universe would have collapsed back long ago. We live in an amazingly finely tuned cosmos.

formulas.description: Vacuum energy density associated with the cosmological constant
formulas.meaning: ρ_Λ is the vacuum energy density, Λ is the cosmological constant, c is the speed of light in vacuum, c ≈ 3×10⁸ m/s, G is the gravitational constant, G ≈ 6.674×10⁻¹¹ m³/(kg·s²)

uncertainty_principle.name: uncertainty principle
uncertainty_principle.description_popular: The uncertainty principle states that in the world of tiny particles, you cannot simultaneously know exactly where a particle is and where it is going. Imagine a blurry photograph of a bee: if you see its position, its wings are smeared, and if you capture the movement of the wings, the bee itself becomes blurry. So with quanta — the more precisely you know one thing, the fuzzier the other becomes.
uncertainty_principle.fun_fact_popular: Because of this principle, subatomic particles don't have a definite location like a billiard ball; they are more like a smeared cloud of probabilities. Even in absolute vacuum, pairs of particles are constantly being born and dying — this is quantum jitter.
uncertainty_principle.description_simple: Heisenberg's uncertainty principle states that it is impossible to simultaneously measure with high precision the position and momentum of a particle. It's like trying to measure the length of a spring that is constantly vibrating: the measurement itself disturbs the system. This principle fundamentally limits our knowledge of the microworld.
uncertainty_principle.history_simple: Discovered by Werner Heisenberg in 1927. It arose from the mathematical formalism of quantum mechanics developed by scientists such as Erwin Schrödinger, Paul Dirac, and others.
uncertainty_principle.how_it_works_simple: The wave describing a particle has a width in space and a frequency in momentum. Just as a musical chord cannot be compressed into a single instant and simultaneously have a pure tone, so a quantum particle cannot be perfectly localized without uncertainty in its speed.
uncertainty_principle.description: The uncertainty principle is a fundamental relation in quantum mechanics that sets a limit on the joint precision of determining canonically conjugate observables, such as position and momentum. Mathematically, it is expressed by an inequality relating the variances of operators. This principle stems not from imperfections of measurement but from the wave-like nature of matter and the non-commutativity of operators.
uncertainty_principle.history: Formulated by Werner Heisenberg in 1927 based on his matrix mechanics. Later that year, Jordan generalized it for arbitrary non-commuting observables. Robertson in 1929 derived the general inequality. Erwin Schrödinger introduced the concept of coherent states that minimize uncertainty. The principle became one of the cornerstones of quantum theory, sparking deep philosophical discussions (Einstein-Bohr).
uncertainty_principle.how_it_works: The wave function of a particle in the position representation is related to the wave function in the momentum representation by a Fourier transform. The product of the widths of these distributions is bounded from below. The position and momentum operators do not commute: [x̂, p̂] = iħ, leading to the inequality Δx Δp ≥ ħ/2. Similar relations exist for energy and time, phase and particle number. The principle manifests in quantum tunneling, zero-point oscillations of oscillators, and the absence of particle trajectories.
key_problems: Compatibility with gravity at Planck scales
Interpretation of quantum mechanics (role of the observer, wave function collapse)
Problem of quantum gravity (quantization of spacetime)
uncertainty_principle.fun_fact: The uncertainty principle is not related to instrument error — it is a property of nature itself. Moreover, it ensures the stability of atoms: without it, the electron would fall into the nucleus, and the Universe would be a compressed point.

formulas.description: Coordinate-momentum uncertainty relation
formulas.meaning: Δx — standard deviation of position, Δp — standard deviation of momentum, ħ = h/(2π) — reduced Planck constant, h ≈ 6.626×10⁻³⁴ J·s.

dark_energy.name: dark energy
dark_energy.description_popular: Dark energy is a mysterious force that pushes galaxies in the Universe apart faster and faster, like wind blowing balloons apart. We don't see it, but we know it exists because the Universe is expanding at an accelerating rate.
dark_energy.fun_fact_popular: Dark energy makes up about 68% of all energy in the Universe — it's as if in a huge pie, almost three-quarters were made of invisible, intangible jelly!
dark_energy.description_simple: Dark energy is a hypothetical form of energy that permeates all space and causes the accelerated expansion of the Universe. Imagine a rubber band that, instead of contracting under gravity, unexpectedly begins to stretch more and more, as if something inside is pushing it apart. That's exactly how our Universe behaves: distant galaxies are moving away from each other at ever-increasing speeds.
dark_energy.history_simple: In 1998, two independent groups of astronomers, observing type Ia supernovae, discovered that the Universe is expanding with acceleration. This came as a shock: it was expected that gravity slows down expansion. The discovery brought the Nobel Prize, and the phenomenon itself was called dark energy — in honor of its invisible and mysterious nature.
dark_energy.how_it_works_simple: Dark energy acts like antigravity: it creates negative pressure that pushes space apart. The simplest option is the cosmological constant, proposed by Einstein, which can be imagined as the energy of the vacuum itself. But where it comes from and why there is so much of it is still a mystery.
dark_energy.description: Dark energy is the dominant component (about 68%) of the total energy density of the modern Universe, responsible for the observed accelerated expansion. In general relativity, it is described by the cosmological constant Λ in Einstein's equations, or by more general dynamical fields (quintessence). The cosmological constant corresponds to vacuum energy with the equation of state w = -1, where w is the ratio of pressure to energy density. Observational evidence includes data from type Ia supernovae, baryon acoustic oscillations, the cosmic microwave background, and large-scale structure.
dark_energy.history: In 1917, Albert Einstein introduced the cosmological constant for a static Universe, but abandoned it after Edwin Hubble's discovery of expansion in 1929. Georges Lemaître also linked the cosmological constant with vacuum energy. In 1998, the Supernova Cosmology Project and High-Z Supernova Search Team discovered accelerated expansion, independently confirming the need for dark energy. Subsequent experiments, such as WMAP and Planck, refined its parameters.
dark_energy.how_it_works: In the framework of GR, dark energy manifests as a source in Einstein's equations, creating a repulsive gravitational effect under negative pressure. The Friedmann equation describing the evolution of the scale factor a(t) contains a term with the cosmological constant: (ȧ/a)² = (8πG/3)ρ - k/a² + Λ/3. Under the dominance of Λ, the scale factor grows exponentially. Hubble's law v = H₀ d is modified: the Hubble parameter changes with time, and data from distant supernovae show deviation from linearity, indicating acceleration.
key_problems: Nature of dark energy: is it a cosmological constant or a dynamic field?
Why is the density of dark energy so small compared to theoretical predictions (cosmological constant problem)?
Coincidence that now the densities of dark energy and matter are comparable (coincidence problem)?
Is there a connection with quantum gravity?
dark_energy.fun_fact: If dark energy were just a tiny bit stronger, it would tear apart galaxies, stars, and even atoms in a 'Big Rip' scenario.

formulas.description: Friedmann equation including the cosmological constant
formulas.meaning: a — scale factor; ȧ — its time derivative; G — gravitational constant, G ≈ 6.674×10⁻¹¹ m³/(kg·s²); ρ — density of matter and radiation; k — spatial curvature (0, ±1); c — speed of light, c ≈ 3×10⁸ m/s; Λ — cosmological constant

formulas.description: Equation of state for dark energy
formulas.meaning: w — parameter of the equation of state; p — pressure; ρ — energy density; c — speed of light. For the cosmological constant, w = -1.

antimatter.name: antimatter
antimatter.description_popular: Imagine that every object has an invisible twin. As soon as they meet, both disappear in a flash of pure energy.
antimatter.fun_fact_popular: The positron — the 'twin' of the electron — helps doctors look inside the body using PET scanners, like an X-ray but for metabolism.
antimatter.description_simple: Antimatter is the mirror reflection of ordinary matter: particles with the same mass but opposite charge. On contact, they annihilate, turning into radiation, just as a drop of water and a drop of oil in a fire flare up with light.
antimatter.history_simple: Antimatter was predicted by British physicist Paul Dirac in 1928, while solving an equation that united quantum mechanics and relativity. In 1932, Carl Anderson first saw the positron in cosmic rays.
antimatter.how_it_works_simple: When a particle collides with its antiparticle, their mass completely converts into energy according to E=mc². This principle works in a PET scanner: a patient is injected with antimatter, and it 'lights up' tumors.
antimatter.description: Antimatter consists of antiparticles, each having the same mass but opposite quantum numbers (charge, lepton or baryon number) compared to the corresponding particle. Annihilation of a particle-antiparticle pair results in the production of photons or lighter pairs, with conservation of energy, momentum, and quantum numbers.
antimatter.history: The concept of antiparticles arose from Dirac's equation (1928), which allowed solutions with negative energy. Interpreting such states as particles with positive energy and opposite charge led to the prediction of the positron, discovered by Anderson in 1932. Later, accelerators produced antiprotons, antineutrons, and even antihydrogen. Today, antimatter is studied as a tool to test fundamental symmetries of nature.
antimatter.how_it_works: In quantum field theory, each field describing particles corresponds to a conjugate field of antiparticles. The annihilation process is described with high probability by Feynman diagrams and obeys the Dirac equation. Near black holes, in Hawking radiation processes, pairs of particle-antiparticle can be born, one of which falls into the hole, and the other flies away.
key_problems: Why does the observable Universe have a predominance of matter (baryon asymmetry)?
How long can antimatter be stored and transported over long distances?
How does gravity act on antimatter: does it fall down or up? Experiments (AEGIS, ALPHA) are looking for the answer.
antimatter.fun_fact: The Universe around us is almost entirely composed of matter. Where the antimatter went after the Big Bang is one of the greatest mysteries of cosmology.

formulas.description: Energy released during annihilation of a particle-antiparticle pair
formulas.meaning: E — released energy, J; m — total mass of annihilating particles, kg; c — speed of light in vacuum, c ≈ 3×10⁸ m/s

tensor.name: tensor
tensor.description_popular: Imagine you want to describe the tension inside a piece of rubber: you pull it along, across, or diagonally – the force responds differently. A tensor is like a 'summary table' of all such interactions at once. It stores information about how a material responds to stress in any direction. Simply put, if a number is a point, a vector is an arrow, then a tensor is a whole system of interconnected arrows.
tensor.fun_fact_popular: Without tensors, we wouldn’t be able to accurately predict how a bridge deforms under the weight of a train. And it was tensors that helped Einstein describe how massive bodies warp spacetime – like a heavy ball on a stretched sheet.
tensor.description_simple: A tensor generalizes the concepts of vectors and matrices to multidimensional arrays of numbers. If a scalar is a zero-dimensional quantity (just a number), a vector is a one-dimensional sequence, then a tensor is a multidimensional lattice describing physical properties that depend on several directions. For example, to describe the stress inside a solid body, we need to know the forces acting on each face in all directions – this is the stress tensor.
tensor.history_simple: The idea of tensor calculus originated in the late 19th century. Italian mathematicians Gregorio Ricci-Curbastro and Tullio Levi-Civita created the formal apparatus that later became the language of general relativity. James Clerk Maxwell, without knowing it, had already used tensors in his equations of electromagnetism, and Hendrik Lorentz applied them for transformations of space and time.
tensor.how_it_works_simple: Tensors manifest themselves when we rotate the coordinate system. Ordinary numbers (scalars) do not change upon rotation, while vector components change according to a simple rule. A tensor, however, transforms more complexly, preserving internal geometry, much like the shadow of a rotating cube changes shape but always remains a projection of the same object.
tensor.description: A tensor is a geometric object whose components in a given basis transform according to a certain linear law under coordinate changes. Tensors of rank (p,q) are invariant under the group of basis transformations. They are ubiquitous in differential geometry, continuum mechanics, and field theory. For example, the metric tensor g_{μν} defines distance in curved spacetime, the stress-energy tensor T_{μν} describes the density and flux of energy, and the electromagnetic field tensor F_{μν} unites electric and magnetic fields into a single relativistic structure.
tensor.history: The history of tensors begins with works on differential geometry and mechanics. Gauss and Riemann laid the foundations, but the systematic calculus was developed by Ricci-Curbastro and Levi-Civita in 1884–1900. In 1915, Albert Einstein used their apparatus to formulate general relativity, where gravity manifests as the curvature of spacetime, described by the Ricci tensor and the Einstein tensor. James Clerk Maxwell had already in 1865 essentially written his equations in tensor form, though he didn't use the term. Hendrik Lorentz in 1904 derived transformations that became the basis for understanding the tensor nature of physical laws in special relativity.
tensor.how_it_works: The transformation law for tensor components when transitioning to new coordinates x'^μ = x'^μ(x^ν) is given by the product of Jacobian matrices. For example, for a tensor of rank (1,1): T'^μ_ν = (∂x'^μ/∂x^α) (∂x^β/∂x'^ν) T^α_β. Maxwell's equations in tensor form: ∂_ν F^{μν} = μ_0 J^μ, where F^{μν} is the electromagnetic field tensor, J^μ is the 4-current. Einstein field equations: G_{μν} + Λ g_{μν} = (8πG/c^4) T_{μν}, where G_{μν} is the Einstein tensor expressing spacetime curvature through the metric tensor and its derivatives. Tensors also describe stress and strain in mechanics: σ_{ij} = C_{ijkl} ε^{kl}, where σ is the stress tensor, ε is the strain tensor, and C is the fourth-rank elasticity tensor.
key_problems: Developing efficient numerical methods for tensor decompositions of multidimensional data under memory constraints
Finding an exact solution to Einstein's equations for two bodies including gravitational radiation remains a challenge
Mathematical classification of singularities of tensor fields in general relativity
tensor.fun_fact: In machine learning, tensors are the basic data type. Neural networks process huge multidimensional tensors: for instance, a color image is a three-dimensional tensor (width, height, color channels), and video is four-dimensional, adding time.

formulas.description: Einstein field equations
formulas.meaning: G_{μν} is the Einstein tensor (spacetime curvature), Λ is the cosmological constant, g_{μν} is the metric tensor, G is the gravitational constant, G ≈ 6.67430×10⁻¹¹ m³ kg⁻¹ s⁻², c is the speed of light, c ≈ 3×10⁸ m/s, T_{μν} is the stress-energy tensor of matter

formulas.description: Electromagnetic field tensor (Maxwell)
formulas.meaning: F_{μν} is the electromagnetic field tensor, ∂_μ is the partial derivative with respect to coordinate x^μ, A_μ is the electromagnetic four-potential

spin.name: spin
spin.description_popular: Spin is like a tiny top that every particle has, but it doesn't actually spin. It's just an intrinsic property, similar to rotation, but without motion.
spin.fun_fact_popular: An electron behaves as if it's spinning, but it really isn't – it's just its inherent property, like eye color.
spin.description_simple: Spin is the intrinsic angular momentum of a particle, similar to how Earth rotates about its axis and possesses angular momentum. But unlike Earth, spin is quantized and not related to actual rotation in space.
spin.history_simple: The idea of spin arose in the 1920s, when Otto Stern and Walther Gerlach discovered the splitting of a beam of silver atoms in a magnetic field. Later, Wolfgang Pauli and Paul Dirac developed its theoretical description.
spin.how_it_works_simple: Spin manifests in magnetic properties: for example, in permanent magnets, electrons align their spins in one direction, creating a net magnetic field. It's like many tiny magnets oriented the same way.
spin.description: Spin is a fundamental quantum number characterizing the intrinsic angular momentum of a particle. Unlike orbital angular momentum, spin is not associated with motion in space but is an internal property described by the SU(2) algebra. For fermions (electrons, protons), spin takes half-integer values (e.g., 1/2), for bosons (photons) – integer (0, 1). Spin determines particle statistics and their behavior in a magnetic field via the magnetic moment. Measurement of a spin component always yields discrete values, and its state is described by spinors.
spin.history: The experimental discovery of spin is linked to the Stern–Gerlach experiment (1922), where a beam of silver atoms split into two components in an inhomogeneous magnetic field, contradicting classical physics. The theoretical explanation was given by George Uhlenbeck and Samuel Goudsmit in 1925, proposing an electron’s own momentum. Later, Pauli introduced spin matrices, and Dirac in 1928 naturally incorporated spin into the relativistic equation for the electron. Subsequently, the concept of spin became key for all quantum field theory and particle physics.
spin.how_it_works: Spin is described by operators obeying the commutation relations [Ŝ_i, Ŝ_j] = iħ ε_{ijk} Ŝ_k. For spin-1/2 particles, the Pauli representation with matrices σ_x, σ_y, σ_z is often used. The Hamiltonian for interaction with a magnetic field: H = –μ·B, where the magnetic moment μ = g (e/2m) S. Quantum spin correlations (entanglement) are tested in Bell's inequalities, demonstrating the nonlocality of quantum mechanics. Spin is also responsible for the fine structure of spectra and plays a central role in spin electronics (spintronics).
key_problems: Nature of spin: is spin a fundamental or composite property?
Problem of quantum gravity: how to describe spin in curved spacetime?
Spin measurement: how does the wavefunction collapse occur when measuring a projection?
spin.fun_fact: Thanks to spin, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) works: hydrogen nuclei in our body have spin and, placed in a magnetic field, emit signals that the scanner reads.

formulas.description: Magnitude of the intrinsic angular momentum vector
formulas.meaning: |S| is the magnitude of spin angular momentum; s is the spin quantum number (for an electron s = 1/2); ħ is the reduced Planck constant, ħ = h/(2π) ≈ 1.054571817×10⁻³⁴ J·s.

quantum_tunneling.name: quantum tunneling
quantum_tunneling.description_popular: Imagine you're rolling a ball over a hill, but it miraculously appears on the other side without even climbing over the top. For microscopic particles, this is routine – it's called quantum tunneling.
quantum_tunneling.fun_fact_popular: Quantum tunneling is like walking through a wall without breaking it. Electrons do this constantly in microchips, seeping through barriers.
quantum_tunneling.description_simple: In the quantum world, particles can pass through energy barriers like ghosts through walls. It's like a wave hitting a pier: some water passes through gaps, even though most of it reflects.
quantum_tunneling.history_simple: The phenomenon was first described in 1928 when George Gamow explained alpha decay of radioactive nuclei. Later, tunneling became key to semiconductor electronics (tunnel diodes) and explaining nuclear fusion in stars.
quantum_tunneling.how_it_works_simple: An electron in a transistor encounters an energy barrier, but due to its wave nature, it can 'seep' through, creating a tunnel current. This resembles water seeping through sand, though the sand seems like a solid barrier.
quantum_tunneling.description: Quantum tunneling is a phenomenon where a particle overcomes a potential barrier whose height exceeds its total energy. From a classical standpoint, this is impossible, but in quantum mechanics, the particle's wavefunction decays exponentially inside the barrier and has a nonzero amplitude beyond it. This leads to a finite probability of passage. The effect is widespread: from radioactive decay and thermonuclear fusion to modern transistors and scanning tunneling microscopy (STM). In quantum cosmology, tunneling is considered a possible mechanism for the birth of the Universe from 'nothing'.
quantum_tunneling.history: The first theoretical explanation of tunneling was given in 1928 independently by George Gamow, and also by Ronald Gurney and Edward Condon for alpha decay of nuclei. Subsequently, the phenomenon was applied in solid-state physics: Leo Esaki in 1958 created the tunnel diode, for which he received the Nobel Prize. In astrophysics, tunneling explained the mechanism of stellar nucleosynthesis (Hans Bethe, 1938). Later, Stephen Hawking used the concept of tunneling to describe black hole radiation.
quantum_tunneling.how_it_works: Within nonrelativistic quantum mechanics, tunneling is described by solving the stationary Schrödinger equation for a potential with a barrier. The wavefunction inside the barrier (E < V₀) has the form ψ(x) ∝ e^{-κx}, where κ = √(2m(V₀−E))/ħ. The transmission coefficient (tunneling probability) for a rectangular barrier of width L: T ≈ e^{-2κL}. The Heisenberg uncertainty principle allows a temporary violation of energy conservation, making tunneling possible. This effect is also used to explain the cosmological birth of the Universe and Hawking radiation.
key_problems: Tunneling time: how much time does a particle spend in the classically forbidden region? The Hartman paradox points to the possibility of superluminal tunneling.
Macroscopic tunneling: is quantum tunneling possible for systems with a large number of particles?
Precise description of tunneling in complex systems with dissipation and interaction with the environment.
quantum_tunneling.fun_fact: Without quantum tunneling, the Sun wouldn't shine: hydrogen nuclei overcome mutual repulsion and fuse only thanks to the tunnel effect.

formulas.description: Approximate transmission coefficient through a rectangular potential barrier
formulas.meaning: T is the transmission coefficient (tunneling probability); κ is the decay constant of the wavefunction in the barrier; m is the particle mass; V₀ is the height of the potential barrier; E is the particle energy; L is the barrier width; ħ is the reduced Planck constant, ħ ≈ 1.054571817×10⁻³⁴ J·s.

density_matrix.name: density matrix
density_matrix.description_popular: If we don't know exactly what state a quantum system is in, we can describe it with a density matrix – it's like a surprise box: there could be one toy inside, but we're not sure which one, we only know the probabilities.
density_matrix.fun_fact_popular: A density matrix is like a recipe with probabilities: for example, there might be carrots or onions in the soup, but until you look, you don't know. In the quantum world, this is how mixed states are described.
density_matrix.description_simple: A density matrix is a mathematical tool for describing quantum systems when we lack complete information. It's like a weather forecast: we say rain is 30% likely, without knowing the exact position of every raindrop. The matrix contains both probabilities and quantum correlations (entanglement).
density_matrix.history_simple: The density matrix formalism was independently proposed by John von Neumann (1927) and Lev Landau (1927) to describe mixed quantum states that arise, for example, in thermodynamics.
density_matrix.how_it_works_simple: Imagine a shuffled deck of cards: you don't know which card is on top – only probabilities. The density matrix sets these probabilities and also contains hidden information about entanglement. For example, if two particles are linked (entangled), the density matrix for one of them will be mixed, even if the overall system is pure.
density_matrix.description: The density matrix (density operator) ρ is a self-adjoint, positive semi-definite operator with unit trace, describing a statistical ensemble of quantum states. For a pure state |ψ⟩: ρ = |ψ⟩⟨ψ|; for a mixed state: ρ = Σ_i p_i |ψ_i⟩⟨ψ_i|, where p_i are probabilities. The evolution of a closed system is given by the von Neumann equation: iħ dρ/dt = [H, ρ]. When interacting with an environment, off-diagonal elements decay (decoherence), formalizing the transition from quantum superpositions to classical probabilities. The density matrix is indispensable in quantum statistics, quantum optics, and quantum information.
density_matrix.history: The concept of the density operator was introduced by John von Neumann in his work 'Probabilistic Construction of Quantum Mechanics' (1927) and simultaneously by Lev Landau to describe damping in quantum systems. Subsequently, the formalism became the foundation of quantum statistical mechanics (Felix Bloch, Richard Feynman). In the 1960s-70s, with the development of quantum optics, the density matrix became a key tool for describing states of light and atoms. Alain Aspect's experiments testing Bell's inequalities (1980s) used the density matrix to analyze entangled photons.
density_matrix.how_it_works: In Hilbert space, the density matrix is written in a basis as ρ_{mn} = ⟨m|ρ|n⟩. Diagonal elements give populations of states, off-diagonal elements – coherences. For a subsystem obtained by taking the partial trace over the environment, ρ_red = Tr_env(ρ_total). This leads to the von Neumann entropy S = –Tr(ρ ln ρ), which characterizes the degree of mixedness. Quantum tomography allows reconstructing all elements of the density matrix through a series of measurements. The density matrix naturally generalizes to continuous variables (Wigner function) and is used in quantum computing for error correction.
key_problems: Measurement problem: how to objectively describe the transition from a pure state to a mixed one upon measurement?
Decoherence: the detailed mechanism of turning quantum correlations into classical ones upon interaction with the environment.
Quantum tomography: efficient methods for full reconstruction of the unknown density matrix of high-dimensional systems.
density_matrix.fun_fact: Thanks to density matrices, we are developing quantum computers: they are used to describe and correct errors in qubits, as well as to perform quantum tomography – a complete measurement of an unknown state.

formulas.description: Definition of the density operator for a mixed state
formulas.meaning: ρ is the density operator (density matrix); p_i is the probability of the system being in the pure state |ψ_i⟩; |ψ_i⟩ are state vectors in Hilbert space; the sum over i accounts for all possible states forming the ensemble.

quark.name: quark
quark.description_popular: Quarks are tiny bricks that make up everything around us. Like flour in dough, they come together to create protons and neutrons – the building blocks of atoms.
quark.fun_fact_popular: Quarks are real invisibles: they have never been seen alone, they always stick together in groups, like inseparable friends!
quark.description_simple: Imagine that protons and neutrons are M&M's candies, and quarks are the peanuts inside the chocolate coating. They are fundamental particles of matter, bound by a 'strong glue' of gluons.
quark.history_simple: The idea of quarks was born in 1964 when American physicist Murray Gell-Mann (and independently George Zweig) proposed that all nuclear particles consist of smaller objects with fractional electric charge.
quark.how_it_works_simple: Quarks interact via the strong force by exchanging gluons. It's like a team ping-pong game: quarks toss 'balls' – gluons – back and forth and thereby hold together inside hadrons.
quark.description: Quarks are elementary particles with half-integer spin (fermions), possessing color charge and participating in all fundamental interactions. In the Standard Model, quarks are grouped into three generations: (u, d), (c, s), (t, b). Their electric charges are fractional: +2/3 e for up-type, −1/3 e for down-type. Due to confinement, quarks are not observed free but exist only inside colorless hadrons – mesons (quark-antiquark) and baryons (three quarks).
quark.history: The quark model was proposed in 1964 by Murray Gell-Mann and George Zweig to classify hadrons. Initially, quarks were considered a mathematical abstraction, but deep inelastic scattering experiments of electrons on protons in 1968–1973 confirmed their reality. The discovery of the J/ψ meson in 1974 (charm quark) and the Υ meson in 1977 (bottom quark) strengthened the model. The last, the top quark, was discovered in 1995 at Fermilab. The development of quantum chromodynamics (QCD) explained the dynamics of quarks through gluon exchange and the phenomenon of asymptotic freedom.
quark.how_it_works: Quarks interact through the strong nuclear force, described by QCD. Each quark carries one of three color charges (red, green, blue), and the interaction is mediated by eight gluons, which themselves carry color. Due to asymptotic freedom, at short distances quarks behave almost like free particles, but as distance increases, the force field forms a 'string' with a linearly rising potential. This leads to confinement: an attempt to break the bond creates a quark-antiquark pair, and instead of a lone quark, new hadrons are born. The properties of quarks are described within Yang-Mills theory with the gauge group SU(3).
key_problems: Exact mathematical proof of confinement from first principles of QCD (Yang-Mills mass gap problem).
Nature of the quark mass hierarchy and the mechanism of their mass generation through interaction with the Higgs boson.
Behavior of quark-gluon plasma under extreme conditions (early Universe, heavy ion collisions).
quark.fun_fact: There are six 'flavors' of quarks: up, down, charm, strange, top, and bottom. The heaviest – the top quark – weighs as much as an entire gold atom!

carbon.name: carbon
carbon.description_popular: Carbon is what all living things are made of. If you burn a piece of wood, the black charcoal is almost pure carbon. It is like a magical constructor: from it you can build both the soft graphite of a pencil and a hard sparkling diamond.
carbon.fun_fact_popular: There is so much carbon in your body that you could make 9000 pencil leads from it!
carbon.description_simple: Carbon is a unique element capable of creating millions of compounds: from carbon dioxide in soda to DNA, which stores your heredity. It is like a universal glue holding together the molecules of life, and in its pure form it can be soft as graphite or sparkling as diamond.
carbon.history_simple: People have used coal and soot since ancient times, but the fact that diamond is the same carbon was proven only in 1772 when Lavoisier burned a diamond and obtained carbon dioxide.
carbon.how_it_works_simple: The secret of carbon is its ability to form four bonds, like four hands with which it grips other atoms. Thanks to this, it builds long chains and rings, creating all the diversity of organic chemistry — from gasoline to proteins.
carbon.description: Carbon (C) is an element with atomic number 6, the basis of organic chemistry and biochemistry. Its unique ability for catenation — formation of stable chains and rings through covalent bonds — generates an enormous variety of compounds. Carbon allotropes exhibit a striking contrast of properties: from electrically conductive graphite to superhard diamond and fullerenes. In astrophysics, carbon is synthesized in stars via the triple-alpha process, which requires fine-tuning of a resonance level predicted by Fred Hoyle — a vivid example of the anthropic principle.
carbon.history: The elemental nature of carbon was established at the end of the 18th century by Antoine Lavoisier, who obtained carbon dioxide by burning diamonds and proved that diamond, graphite, and coal are the same substance. Lavoisier introduced the term 'carbon' in 1789. The development of organic chemistry in the 19th century solidified the central role of carbon. Johann Balmer, by creating a formula for the spectral lines of hydrogen, opened the way for spectral analysis of carbon in flames and stars. Cecilia Payne-Gaposchkin in the 20th century confirmed that carbon is one of the main elements of stellar composition.
carbon.how_it_works: The chemical flexibility of carbon is provided by four valence electrons capable of orbital hybridization (sp, sp², sp³), which determines bond geometry and types of allotropes. In stars, carbon is formed in the triple-alpha process: three helium-4 nuclei fuse into carbon-12, which requires precise energy matching described by quantum mechanics (the Schrödinger equation). The stability of carbon nuclei is also dictated by the Pauli principle, which forbids fermions from occupying the same state, playing a role in stellar evolution and preventing the collapse of white dwarfs.
key_problems: Why is life on Earth based on carbon and not on silicon?
What new carbon allotropes with unique electronic and mechanical properties can be synthesized?
How to predict the long-term stability of carbon nanostructures under real conditions?
carbon.fun_fact: Diamond and graphite consist of the same atom, but the difference in their hardness is like between glass and steel. It all depends on how the atoms are packed in space: in diamond they are rigidly bound in a three-dimensional lattice, while in graphite they are arranged in layers that slide over each other.

methane.name: methane
methane.description_popular: Methane is an invisible gas that escapes from the ground in swamps and from the stomach of a cow as it digests grass. Imagine the methane molecule as a tiny tetrahedral hedgehog: a carbon atom in the center holds four hydrogen 'spines'. It is lighter than air, so like a balloon, it rises into the sky if set free.
methane.fun_fact_popular: Methane is called swamp gas because it bubbles up in bogs — that’s how bacteria finish off plant remains without oxygen, like tiny chefs making an invisible soup.
methane.description_simple: Methane is the simplest hydrocarbon, its molecule shaped like a perfect tetrahedron: a carbon atom in the center and four hydrogen atoms at equal distances, like clover leaves. In nature, it is born in anaerobic conditions — where there is no oxygen: in the intestines of animals (cows, termites), at the bottom of swamps, rice paddies, and even in permafrost.
methane.history_simple: Methane was first identified by the Italian scientist Alessandro Volta in 1776. He collected gas from the bottom of Lake Maggiore and ignited it, proving it was a combustible substance.
methane.how_it_works_simple: The greenhouse effect of methane is like a down duvet: sunlight easily penetrates through it to the Earth, but the planet’s thermal radiation gets trapped because methane molecules vibrate and absorb infrared rays, like a stretched spring resonating at a certain frequency.
methane.description: Methane (CH₄) is the simplest alkane, a saturated hydrocarbon with an sp³-hybridized carbon atom, forming a tetrahedral structure with an H-C-H angle of ≈ 109.5°. It is the main component of natural gas (70–90%), a powerful greenhouse gas with a global warming potential 28 times higher than CO₂ over 100 years, and an important player in atmospheric chemistry, including the formation of tropospheric ozone and stratospheric water vapor. In astrophysics, methane is a biomarker: its simultaneous presence with oxygen in exoplanet spectra could indicate life.
methane.history: The history of methane begins with alchemical observations of 'flammable air' in swamps. In 1776, Alessandro Volta scientifically described it, isolating it from bottom sediments. In the 19th century, Michael Faraday liquefied methane, and John Tyndall in 1859 proved its greenhouse properties. The modern era is associated with the study of methane hydrates on the ocean floor and its role in the climate system.
methane.how_it_works: Methane traps heat through vibrational-rotational transitions: its molecule has several normal modes (symmetric and asymmetric C-H stretching, H-C-H bending vibrations) that actively absorb infrared radiation at wavelengths of about 3.3 μm and 7.7 μm. In the atmosphere, it is oxidized by hydroxyl radicals (OH•) over ~9 years, turning into CO₂ and water vapor. According to Planck’s radiation law, the absorption intensity depends on temperature, and the Doppler effect broadens spectral lines in a turbulent atmosphere.
key_problems: Predicting methane emissions from thawing permafrost bogs and destabilization of marine hydrates under warming conditions
Developing cost-effective catalysts for direct decomposition of methane into hydrogen and carbon (turquoise hydrogen)
methane.fun_fact: Termites are champions of methane production: every year they emit nearly 20 million tons across the planet, building their methane-factory towers.

topology.name: topology
topology.description_popular: Topology is the mathematics of what remains unchanged when things bend, stretch, and squeeze, but do not tear. Imagine you are sculpting from plasticine: a cup with a handle and a donut are topological twins because both have exactly one hole. And a T-shirt is like a disk with three glued-on sleeve-tubes.
topology.fun_fact_popular: If you were an ant on a Möbius strip, you could crawl over the entire surface and return to the starting point... but upside down, because this strip has only one side!
topology.description_simple: Topology studies properties of space that are preserved under continuous deformations — imagine a rubber universe where you can crumple and twist objects, but cutting or gluing points is forbidden. It does not distinguish a square from a circle, but it distinguishes a sphere from a torus: on a sphere all loops shrink to a point, but on a torus they do not.
topology.history_simple: The birth of topology is associated with Henri Poincaré, who in 1895 published 'Analysis situs', laying the foundations of algebraic topology. He also formulated the famous Poincaré conjecture about the three-dimensional sphere, proved only in 2003.
topology.how_it_works_simple: Topology works through invariants — like passport data of a shape that do not change under stretching. For example, the number of holes (birational genus) or the Euler characteristic: for a cube it is 2 (V-E+F=8-12+6=2), same as for a sphere, while for a torus it is 0. If invariants differ, the objects are definitely not equivalent.
topology.description: Topology is a fundamental branch of mathematics studying properties of topological spaces that are invariant under homeomorphisms — continuous bijections with a continuous inverse. It includes general topology (limits, compactness, connectedness), algebraic topology (homotopies, homologies, fundamental groups), and differential topology (smooth manifolds, fiber bundles). The key idea: to classify spaces up to deformation using invariants such as homology groups or characteristic classes.
topology.history: The roots of topology go back to Euler's solution of the Königsberg bridge problem (1736) and his polyhedron theorem (1758). Henri Poincaré (included in the scientists list) laid the foundations in a series of works 'Analysis situs' (1895–1904), introducing the fundamental group and homologies. In the 20th century, the theory flourished thanks to Brouwer, Hurewicz, Whitehead, and others; the Poincaré conjecture was proved by Perelman in 2003.
topology.how_it_works: Topological invariants extract algebraic information from a space. For example, the fundamental group π₁(X) encodes all possible loops up to continuous deformation: for a sphere, π₁ = {0}; for a circle, π₁ = ℤ. Homologies Hₙ(X) count 'holes' of various dimensions. For manifolds, the de Rham theorem, linking differential forms and cohomology, is often used. In physics, topology manifests through quantum anomalies, solitons, and string theory: Calabi-Yau spaces have non-trivial topology affecting the compactification of extra dimensions.
key_problems: Classification of smooth structures on four-dimensional manifolds (the exotic R⁴ problem)
The Hodge conjecture on the relation between algebraic cycles and cohomologies (one of the Millennium Problems)
Computing homotopy groups of higher-dimensional spheres in general
topology.fun_fact: Topologists have proved that you can turn a sphere inside out without tearing, simply by passing the surface through itself — this is called Smale's sphere eversion.

formulas.description: Euler's formula for convex polyhedra — the simplest topological invariant
formulas.meaning: χ (chi) — Euler characteristic (topological invariant), V — number of vertices, E — number of edges, F — number of faces

lie_group.name: Lie group
lie_group.description_popular: A Lie group is like a round dance where the figures can change smoothly, and each figure is obtained from the previous one by a precise rule. Imagine a kaleidoscope: you turn the tube — the pattern shimmers, and all possible rotations live in a special world of symmetries that mathematicians called a Lie group.
lie_group.fun_fact_popular: Using Lie groups, physicists have described all particles in the universe: quarks and electrons live in symmetrical patterns, like in a magical kaleidoscope where every shift is a new transformation.
lie_group.description_simple: A Lie group is both a smooth manifold and a group where multiplication and inversion are smooth maps. Visually: imagine a rotating sphere — all possible rotations form a group, and each rotation can be smoothly transitioned to another, like a camera on a tripod slowly being turned.
lie_group.history_simple: Norwegian mathematician Sophus Lie created this theory in the 1870s, inspired by Galois's ideas and seeking to create for differential equations the same symmetry tool that group theory provided for algebraic equations.
lie_group.how_it_works_simple: Instead of studying the whole group at once, Lie looked at infinitesimal transformations near the identity — this is the tangent space with the Lie bracket operation. Like a conductor setting the tempo with a small wave of the baton, the tangent algebra determines the structure of the entire group.
lie_group.description: A Lie group is a differentiable manifold endowed with a group structure such that the group operations of multiplication (m: G×G→G) and inversion (i: G→G) are smooth maps. The tangent space at the identity forms a Lie algebra — a vector space with a bilinear antisymmetric Lie bracket satisfying the Jacobi identity. Lie groups classify continuous symmetries and are the foundation of modern physics: from rotations in quantum mechanics (SO(3), SU(2)) to gauge theories of the Standard Model (U(1)×SU(2)×SU(3)).
lie_group.history: The theory of Lie groups emerged in the works of Sophus Lie (1870s) while studying symmetries of differential equations. Later, Wilhelm Killing and Élie Cartan classified simple Lie groups. The connection to physics was established through the works of Hermann Weyl and Eugene Wigner on group representations in quantum mechanics. In the second half of the 20th century, Lie groups became the foundation of the gauge principle.
lie_group.how_it_works: Key fact: Lie groups are locally determined by their Lie algebra via the exponential map exp: g → G. For matrix groups, exp(X) = Σ_{k=0}^∞ X^k/k!. The structure constants of the algebra determine all local properties of the group. Representation theory describes how the group acts on vector spaces: for example, SO(3) on spherical harmonics. In physics, gauge fields are connections in principal bundles with a structure group, and their curvature is the field strength (Yang-Mills equations). According to Noether's theorem, every continuous symmetry described by a Lie group leads to the conservation of some quantity.
key_problems: Complete classification of infinite-dimensional Lie algebras
Construction of quantum groups (noncommutative deformations of universal enveloping algebras)
Integrability of nonlinear systems and Kac-Moody algebras
lie_group.fun_fact: The group SU(3) — the main symmetry of the strong interaction between quarks — has dimension 8 and generates eight gluons that glue protons and neutrons together.

manifold.name: manifold
manifold.description_popular: Imagine an ant crawling on the surface of a huge ball. To it, the ball seems flat, like an endless plain. But in fact, it lives on a curved surface that looks flat up close. So a manifold is a space that in each small region looks like ordinary Euclidean space, but as a whole may be more complex, like the surface of the Earth or a donut.
manifold.fun_fact_popular: If we lived on the surface of a donut, we could walk in any direction and eventually return to the starting point, unaware that our space has a hole! Manifolds allow us to study such unusual shapes of the universe.
manifold.description_simple: A manifold is a mathematical concept that generalizes the idea of a surface in any number of dimensions. Just as a sphere is a two-dimensional surface in three-dimensional space, a manifold can exist on its own, without being embedded in a higher-dimensional space. It's like a soap bubble film: it's curved, but up close it looks flat.
manifold.history_simple: The idea of manifolds originated in the works of Bernhard Riemann in the 19th century, but the modern language of topology was given to them by Henri Poincaré, who formulated the famous Poincaré conjecture about three-dimensional manifolds in the early 20th century.
manifold.how_it_works_simple: Manifolds appear in physics: according to general relativity, spacetime is a four-dimensional manifold curved by mass and energy. Just as a stretched fabric sags under a heavy ball, a star causes spacetime to curve, creating gravity.
manifold.description: A manifold is a topological space locally homeomorphic to Euclidean space ℝ^n. The topology of a manifold is given by an atlas of compatible charts. A smooth manifold is additionally endowed with a differentiable structure, allowing the use of calculus on it. Manifolds are classified by dimension, orientability, presence of boundaries, and other features. They serve as the main stage for many branches of mathematics and physics: from Riemannian geometry and Lie group theory to general relativity and gauge field theories.
manifold.history: The concept of a manifold arose from attempts to mathematically describe complex geometric shapes. Bernhard Riemann, in his 1854 lecture 'On the Hypotheses Which Lie at the Foundations of Geometry', introduced a generalized concept of multidimensional space. At the end of the 19th century, works by Felix Klein and Sophus Lie gave rise to the theory of Lie groups — smooth manifolds with a group structure. In the early 20th century, Henri Poincaré laid the foundations of topology, in particular, formulating the conjecture about the homotopy equivalence of the three-dimensional sphere. Later, the development of manifold topology led to the classification of surfaces, the creation of knot theory, and modern differential geometry.
manifold.how_it_works: In physics, spacetime is modeled as a four-dimensional pseudo-Riemannian manifold, whose metric is determined by Einstein's field equations: R_{μν} - ½Rg_{μν} = (8πG/c⁴) T_{μν}. Here, the manifold sets the geometric properties of the universe, and curvature describes gravitational interaction. In quantum mechanics, wave functions are defined on infinite-dimensional Hilbert spaces, but their configuration spaces are often finite-dimensional manifolds (e.g., the Bloch sphere for a single qubit). Gauge symmetry groups (U(1), SU(2), SU(3)) are Lie groups naturally acting on corresponding manifolds.
key_problems: Classification of four-dimensional topological manifolds, including the smooth structures conjecture
The problem of constructing a quantum theory of gravity on a dynamical manifold background
Study of symplectic manifolds in the context of mirror symmetry and string theory
manifold.fun_fact: The Poincaré conjecture, proved only in 2003 by Grigori Perelman, was one of the 'Millennium Problems'. Interestingly, Perelman himself declined the million-dollar prize and the Fields Medal, modestly noting that he simply completed Richard Hamilton's work.

eigenvalue.name: eigenvalue
eigenvalue.description_popular: Imagine stretching a rubber band. It lengthens but stays straight — that's the direction in which the band 'obeys' the stretch. The eigenvalue is exactly how much it stretched in that direction. In mathematics, eigenvalues show how a transformation (e.g., stretching or compression) affects an object along special 'compliant' directions.
eigenvalue.fun_fact_popular: Eigenvalues explain why bridges don't collapse from wind: engineers calculate at what frequency the bridge will start to oscillate and ensure that these 'eigenfrequencies' do not match the wind frequency.
eigenvalue.description_simple: In linear algebra, an eigenvalue is a number characterizing how many times a vector is stretched or compressed under a linear transformation if the vector remains on the same line. It's like the shadow of a rotating stick: there are directions where the shadow only changes length but not direction — those are eigendirections, and the length change coefficients are eigenvalues.
eigenvalue.history_simple: The idea of eigenvalues emerged in the 19th century in the works of Augustin Cauchy and Charles Hermite while studying quadratic forms. But true fame came to them from quantum mechanics, where the Schrödinger equation is an eigenvalue problem: the energy of a particle turns out to be an eigenvalue of the Hamiltonian operator.
eigenvalue.how_it_works_simple: In quantum mechanics, a particle's state is described by a wave function. When measuring energy, we apply an operator to it, like a template that 'reveals' specific values — eigenvalues. Just as a sieve sorts sand by particle size, the energy operator 'sifts' through possible states and yields the allowed energy levels of an atom.
eigenvalue.description: An eigenvalue of a linear operator A is a number λ such that there exists a nonzero vector v (eigenvector) satisfying the equation A v = λ v. The spectrum of an operator (the set of eigenvalues) plays a key role in functional analysis and quantum mechanics. For Hermitian operators, eigenvalues are real, which physically is interpreted as possible measurement results of an observable. Generalization to infinite-dimensional spaces leads to self-adjoint operators with a continuous spectrum.
eigenvalue.history: The roots of the concept go back to the 18th century: Euler studied the rotation of a rigid body, identifying the principal axes of inertia. In the 19th century, Cauchy developed the theory of eigenvalues of symmetric matrices. A decisive contribution was made by David Hilbert, who developed the spectral theory of operators, paving the way for quantum mechanics. In 1926, Erwin Schrödinger formulated his famous equation Ĥψ = Eψ, where Ĥ is the Hamiltonian operator and E is the energy eigenvalue. Since then, eigenvalue problems have become the main tool for calculating electronic structures, vibrations, and stability of systems.
eigenvalue.how_it_works: A quantum-mechanical observable is represented by a Hermitian operator Â. The eigenvalue equation is: Â ψ_n = a_n ψ_n, where ψ_n is an eigenfunction and a_n is an eigenvalue. Heisenberg's uncertainty principle limits the possibility of simultaneous precise measurement of non-commuting observables whose operators do not share common eigenvectors. In engineering, eigenvalues of the stress tensor determine principal stresses in materials, and in graph theory, eigenvalues of the adjacency matrix characterize network connectivity.
key_problems: Computing eigenvalues of large sparse matrices is a fundamental problem of numerical analysis
Complete description of the Dirac operator spectrum in curved spacetime remains mathematically challenging
Relationship between eigenvalue distributions of random matrices and quantum chaos and number theory
eigenvalue.fun_fact: Eigenvalue search is used by Google's search engine: the PageRank algorithm is based on computing the eigenvector of the web page link matrix. And facial recognition systems turn photos into eigenvectors, extracting key features.

formulas.description: Eigenvalue equation
formulas.meaning: A — linear operator (matrix), v — eigenvector, λ — eigenvalue

formulas.description: Time-independent Schrödinger equation
formulas.meaning: Ĥ — Hamiltonian operator (total energy of the system), ψ — wave function, E — energy eigenvalue

pulsar.name: pulsar
pulsar.description_popular: Imagine a space lighthouse that spins at a furious speed and beams radio waves like a giant searchlight. When the beam sweeps across Earth, we see regular flashes — like a blinking lightbulb.
pulsar.fun_fact_popular: Pulsars are so precise that you can set your watch by them! Some tick with better accuracy than atomic clocks.
pulsar.description_simple: A pulsar is a rapidly spinning neutron star left after a supernova explosion. It has a very strong magnetic field that channels radio emission into a narrow beam. When the beam, like a lighthouse beam, points toward Earth, we detect periodic pulses.
pulsar.history_simple: Pulsars were discovered in 1967 by graduate student Jocelyn Bell, who noticed regular signals on a radio telescope. At first, they were even thought to be signals from aliens! Later, it was realized they were rapidly spinning neutron stars.
pulsar.how_it_works_simple: A neutron star, the size of a city, is compressed to the limit and retains its rotation, like a figure skater pulling in her arms. Its magnetic field twists and generates radiation that emerges from the poles in narrow beams. If Earth lies in the path of such a beam, we see a pulsation.
pulsar.description: A pulsar is a rotating neutron star with a strong magnetic field (10^8–10^15 G), emitting electromagnetic radiation in the form of directional beams. Due to rotation, the beams create a lighthouse effect, and an observer records periodic pulses. Pulsars can emit from radio waves to gamma rays. Their rotation slows down over time due to energy loss. Some pulsars in binary systems speed up by accreting matter from a companion — these are millisecond pulsars.
pulsar.history: The idea of neutron stars was proposed by Fritz Zwicky and Walter Baade in 1934, but the discovery of pulsars occurred in 1967 by Jocelyn Bell Burnell under the supervision of Antony Hewish. At first, the signals were named LGM-1 (little green men). In 1968, Franco Pacini and Thomas Gold independently linked pulsars to rotating neutron stars. Since then, thousands of pulsars have been discovered, including binary ones, which allowed testing of the theory of gravity. Fritz Zwicky was a pioneer in predicting neutron stars.
pulsar.how_it_works: A pulsar works due to conservation of angular momentum. During collapse, the core shrinks to a radius of ~10 km, increasing the rotation speed. The magnetic field intensifies, and radiation is generated in the polar caps, where charged particles are accelerated along field lines, producing coherent radiation. Precession and spin-down are described by equations of electrodynamics and general relativity, for instance, energy loss to gravitational waves for rapidly rotating asymmetric pulsars.
key_problems: The exact mechanism of coherent radiation generation is unknown.
Why do pulsars exhibit glitches (rotation irregularities)?
Can pulsars be used to detect low-frequency gravitational waves?
What is the structure of matter in the core of a neutron star?
pulsar.fun_fact: The fastest pulsar spins 716 times per second — faster than a kitchen blender!

formulas.description: Estimation of a pulsar's magnetic field from its period and spin-down rate.
formulas.meaning: B — magnetic field strength (gauss), P — rotation period (seconds), \dot{P} — period derivative (s/s). The constant 3.2×10^19 is an empirical coefficient.

supernova.name: supernova
supernova.description_popular: A supernova is a grand fireworks display, when a star explodes and momentarily shines brighter than an entire galaxy. It's like a balloon that's been inflated for millions of years until it pops.
supernova.fun_fact_popular: The most powerful supernova explosion was seen back in 1006 – and it was as bright as day at night!
supernova.description_simple: A supernova is a stellar explosion that makes the star billions of times brighter than the Sun. It happens when a massive star runs out of fuel and its core collapses, or when a white dwarf accretes too much matter.
supernova.history_simple: Supernovae were first observed by Chinese astronomers as early as 185 AD. In the 20th century, Fritz Zwicky defined them as a separate class and predicted that they leave behind neutron stars.
supernova.how_it_works_simple: In a massive star, nuclear fusion creates iron. But iron does not burn, and the core stops pushing outward. Gravity crushes the core into a neutron star or black hole, while the outer layers rebound and are flung into space.
supernova.description: A supernova is the catastrophic finale of a star's evolution, accompanied by a huge energy release (~10^44 J) and synthesis of heavy elements. Types are distinguished: I (no hydrogen in spectrum) and II (with hydrogen lines). Type II supernovae occur from the iron-core collapse of a massive star (>8 solar masses), type Ia from the thermonuclear explosion of a white dwarf in a binary system upon reaching the Chandrasekhar limit. The explosion creates neutron stars or black holes and scatters matter, enriching the galaxy with elements.
supernova.history: The term 'supernova' was coined by Fritz Zwicky and Walter Baade in 1934, suggesting that core collapse leads to the formation of a neutron star. Previously observed 'guest stars', such as in 1054 (the Crab Nebula), were interpreted as novae. A type classification was developed by Rudolph Minkowski in 1941 based on spectra. In 1987, supernova 1987A in the Large Magellanic Cloud provided an opportunity to test theories and record a neutrino burst from the collapse.
supernova.how_it_works: For core-collapse supernovae (Type II): when the core exhausts its nuclear fuel by fusing iron, degenerate electron pressure can no longer withstand gravity. The core collapses at up to 70,000 km/s, reaching nuclear density. Neutronization occurs: p + e- → n + ν_e.
