They consist of three main components: galaxies (observed in the optical), intracluster medium (hot gas, temperature ~2–10 keV, visible in X-rays), and dark matter (detected via gravitational lensing). Their distribution and evolution are determined by cosmological parameters (Ω_m, σ_8). Clusters are used as probes for studying dark energy through the mass function and observations of the Sunyaev–Zeldovich effect in the cosmic microwave background.
raw.history: The first cluster (Coma) was catalogued by Charles Messier in 1781. In 1933, Fritz Zwicky, studying the velocity dispersion of galaxies in this cluster and applying the virial theorem, discovered that to hold the system together, a mass many times greater than the visible one is needed, and introduced the term 'dark matter'. In the 1970s, X-ray satellites (Uhuru, Einstein) revealed the hot intergalactic gas. By the 1980s, it was established that the baryonic component (gas + stars) constitutes only a small fraction of the total mass, with the bulk being dark matter. Vera Rubin confirmed the necessity of dark matter on galactic scales.
raw.how_it_works: Gravitational binding of clusters is described by Newton's law of universal gravitation and Einstein's equations. The velocity dispersion of galaxies (σ ~ 800 km/s) and the gas temperature (T) reflect the depth of the gravitational potential: kT ~ μ m_p σ², where μ is the mean molecular mass. The hot gas emits bremsstrahlung and lines of highly ionized ions (e.g., iron Fe XXV-XXVI), allowing measurement of temperature and metallicity. Gravitational lensing of background galaxies provides an independent method of mass mapping, confirming the presence and distribution of dark matter. Modern cosmological simulations (Illustris, Millennium) reproduce cluster formation in the hierarchical cold dark matter model (ΛCDM).
key_problems: The nature of dark matter
Physics of the intracluster medium: heating and cooling of gas, feedback mechanisms from active galactic nuclei
Accurate determination of mass and density profiles of dark matter
The cusp problem—discrepancy between observed and simulated central density profiles
raw.fun_fact: The mass of a cluster is sometimes estimated from the visible distortions of distant galaxies—as if we were looking at them through an uneven glass (gravitational lensing).

meteorite.name: meteorite
meteorite.mini: A meteorite is a messenger from deep space, a stone that survived a fiery flight through the atmosphere and holds the secrets of the Solar System's birth.
meteorite.practical_application: Meteorites are free samples of ancient material, allowing us to study the composition of the protoplanetary cloud without space missions and find organic molecules—the building blocks of life.
meteorite.description_popular: Meteorites are solid extraterrestrial bodies that have fallen onto a planet's surface. Sizes range from millimeter-sized dust to blocks several meters across. The main source is asteroids, but fragments from the Moon and Mars are also found. By composition, they are classified as stony meteorites (including chondrites and achondrites), iron meteorites, and stony-iron meteorites. Chondrites contain chondrules—millimeter-sized spheres formed by rapid cooling of matter in the protoplanetary disk.
meteorite.fun_fact_popular: The Allende meteorite, which fell in Mexico in 1969, is 4.567 billion years old—older than Earth.
meteorite.description_simple: A meteorite is a piece of cosmic rock or metal that has landed on Earth's surface. Entering the atmosphere, it heats up and produces a bright flash—a meteor, or 'shooting star'. What does not burn up and reaches the ground is called a meteorite. They come in stony, iron, and stony-iron varieties.
meteorite.history_simple: People have long found unusual stones, but for a long time they did not believe in their celestial origin. In the early 19th century, a major meteorite shower in France and a thorough investigation by scientist Jean-Baptiste Biot convinced the scientific world that stones indeed fall from the sky.
meteorite.how_it_works_simple: Meteorites are fragments of asteroids, less often comets or other planets. As a result of collisions, they are ejected into interplanetary space and, after millions of years, may cross Earth's orbit. Just as a river brings stones from the mountains, cosmic trajectories deliver these celestial wanderers to us.
meteorite.description: A meteorite is extraterrestrial material delivered to Earth upon impact. Sources include asteroids (fragments from collisions), comets (rarely), and planets (ejecta from the Moon and Mars). Meteorites are valuable objects for studying the chemical and mineralogical composition of the early Solar System, processes of planetary differentiation, and age determination via isotopic systems (e.g., uranium-lead, potassium-argon). Carbonaceous chondrites are especially important, being rich in water and organics, including amino acids, which is relevant for the panspermia hypothesis—the possible transfer of life through space.
meteorite.history: The decisive moment in the recognition of meteorites was the event of 1803: after the meteorite shower in L'Aigle (France), Jean-Baptiste Biot collected eyewitness accounts and samples, convincing the Academy of Sciences. Meteoritics has developed at the intersection of astronomy, geology, and physics. X-ray diffraction and mass spectrometry have enabled detailed study of minerals and isotopic composition. The discovery of presolar grains—microscopic dust particles that survived supernova explosions before the birth of the Sun—opened a new chapter in the study of the history of matter.
meteorite.how_it_works: Entering the atmosphere at speeds from 11 to 72 km/s, a meteoroid undergoes strong aerodynamic heating, melting, and mass loss (ablation). The glow is caused by the shock wave and ionization of air. Equations of motion considering drag and ablation allow calculation of trajectory and final mass. The annual influx of meteoritic material is about 40,000 tons, predominantly in the form of micrometeorites. Networks of observatory cameras (e.g., the Fireball Network) record bright fireballs, enabling reconstruction of the meteoroid's orbit. Mineralogical analysis relies on X-ray diffraction and Bragg's law: nλ = 2d sinθ.
key_problems: Origin of chondrules—the exact mechanism of rapid melting and solidification is still unclear
Linking specific meteorites to parent bodies (asteroid families)
The role of meteorite bombardment in the origin of life on Earth
meteorite.fun_fact: The Murchison meteorite contains over 70 different amino acids, many of which are absent in terrestrial life, providing direct evidence of their extraterrestrial synthesis.

formulas.description: Radioactive decay equation for age determination
formulas.meaning: t — age of the sample, λ — decay constant (for ²³⁸U λ ≈ 1.55×10⁻¹⁰ yr⁻¹), N_0 — initial quantity of the parent isotope, N — current quantity of the parent isotope

raw.mini: A meteorite is a cosmic stone that survived a fall through Earth's atmosphere, preserving the history of our planet's birth.
raw.practical_application: Meteorites are free samples of matter from the past Solar System; their analysis reveals the composition of the protoplanetary cloud and may even contain organic compounds.
raw.description_popular: Meteorites are solid extraterrestrial objects, from millimeter-sized to multi-meter, that fall onto a planet's surface. Most are asteroid fragments, but some originate from the Moon or Mars. They are classified into stony (chondrites, achondrites), iron, and stony-iron. Chondrites contain chondrules—tiny rounded formations indicating rapid cooling in the protoplanetary disk.
raw.fun_fact_popular: The Allende meteorite, which fell in Mexico in 1969, is older than Earth: its age is 4.567 billion years.
raw.description_simple: A meteorite is a piece of rock or metal that comes from space and lands on Earth. When entering the atmosphere, such an object heats up and glows—this is called a meteor or 'shooting star'. Parts that do not burn up and reach the ground are meteorites. They can be stony, iron, or stony-iron.
raw.history_simple: People have always found unusual stones, but for a long time they did not believe they came from the sky. In the early 19th century, after a meteorite shower in France, science acknowledged their cosmic origin.
raw.how_it_works_simple: Just as a river current brings an unusual stone from afar, cosmic streams deliver fragments of asteroids and comets to Earth. They travel for millions of years until they intersect with our planet.
raw.description: A meteorite is extraterrestrial matter delivered to Earth's surface upon meteorite impact. Sources include asteroids (fragments knocked out during collisions), comets (rarely produce meteorites), and large bodies (ejecta from the Moon and Mars). The study of meteorites enables investigation of the chemical and mineralogical composition of the early Solar System, differentiation processes of planetary bodies, and cosmochronology (age determination via isotopic systems, e.g., ²³⁸U → ²⁰⁶Pb, ⁴⁰K → ⁴⁰Ar). Carbonaceous chondrites are of particular interest, rich in water and organic compounds, including amino acids, raising questions about panspermia.
raw.history: The recognition of the extraterrestrial nature of meteorites dates to 1803, when after the meteorite shower in L'Aigle (France), Jean-Baptiste Biot conducted a comprehensive investigation and convinced the scientific community. Meteoritics has become an interdisciplinary field at the intersection of astronomy, geology, and physics. The development of instrumental methods (X-ray diffraction by Bragg, mass spectrometry) allowed detailed study of mineralogy and isotopic composition. The discovery of isotopic anomalies in some inclusions (e.g., in primitive chondrites) indicates the presence of presolar grains—dust particles that survived supernova explosions before the formation of the Solar System.
raw.how_it_works: When entering the atmosphere at speeds of 11 to 72 km/s, the meteoroid body undergoes aerodynamic heating, melting, and ablation. The shock wave causes glow (meteor) and can lead to fragmentation. The trajectory and final size can be modeled with equations of motion accounting for drag and mass loss. About 40 thousand tons of meteoritic material falls on Earth annually, mostly in the form of microparticles. Determination of the meteorite's orbit before impact is possible thanks to camera networks (e.g., the Fireball Network). Mineralogical analysis uses Bragg's law (nλ = 2d sinθ) to identify crystal structure.
key_problems: Origin of chondrules—the exact mechanism of rapid melting and solidification is still unclear
Linking specific meteorites to parent bodies (asteroid families)
The role of meteorite bombardment in the origin of life on Earth
raw.fun_fact: The Murchison meteorite contains over 70 amino acids, including some not found in terrestrial life, proving their extraterrestrial origin.

radio_astronomy.name: radio astronomy
radio_astronomy.mini: A cosmic radio receiver: catches radio waves from the depths of the Universe, just as an antenna picks up a radio station signal.
radio_astronomy.practical_application: It helps discover pulsars, capture the echo of the Big Bang, and see through dust clouds.
radio_astronomy.description_popular: Radio astronomy explores the Universe in the radio wave range (wavelengths from fractions of a millimeter to tens of meters). Radio telescopes consist of an antenna and a sensitive receiver, often combined into interferometers to achieve high angular resolution. Key sources: synchrotron radiation from relativistic electrons in magnetic fields (supernova remnants, active galactic nuclei), spectral lines of hydrogen (21 cm) and molecules.
radio_astronomy.fun_fact_popular: Jansky's first radio telescope was built from wood and wire, while today the largest radio telescope, FAST in China, has a diameter of 500 meters. It is so sensitive that it picks up signals from the edge of the observable universe.
radio_astronomy.description_simple: Radio astronomy studies the sky in long invisible rays—radio waves. It's like a thermal camera that sees heat, except here cosmic radio emission is captured.
radio_astronomy.history_simple: In 1932, Karl Jansky accidentally discovered a radio signal from the center of the Galaxy, and since then astronomers have been building radio telescopes.
radio_astronomy.how_it_works_simple: Giant dish antennas, like ears, catch cosmic radio echoes, amplify them, and translate them into images—where it's brighter, the source is stronger.
radio_astronomy.description: Radio astronomy is a branch of astronomy that studies electromagnetic radiation from celestial bodies with wavelengths from ≈1 mm (submillimeter) to tens of meters (decameter). It is characterized by the use of antenna arrays and aperture synthesis methods to obtain radio images with resolution equivalent to a telescope with a diameter equal to the interferometer baseline. Receivers are cooled to cryogenic temperatures to reduce noise. Main mechanisms of radio emission: thermal (bremsstrahlung), cyclotron and synchrotron, maser, and spectral lines (HI 21 cm, CO, OH, etc.). Radio interferometry has achieved sub-millisecond resolution (VLBI networks), enabling study of galactic nuclei structure and measurement of pulsar parallaxes.
radio_astronomy.history: Radio astronomy was born in 1932 when Karl Jansky discovered radio emission from the Milky Way. In the 1940s, Grote Reber built the first parabolic radio telescope and mapped the sky. After World War II, rapid development led to the discovery of discrete sources, the synchrotron mechanism, and the 21 cm line (Ewen and Purcell, 1951). In the 1960s, Jocelyn Bell Burnell discovered pulsars, and Arno Penzias and Robert Wilson discovered the cosmic microwave background. Modern networks (ALMA, SKA) continue the tradition.
radio_astronomy.how_it_works: An antenna collects radio waves and focuses them onto a feed, where the signal is converted into an electric current. After amplification and frequency conversion, power is measured. In an interferometer, signals from multiple antennas are correlated, forming a visibility function. Inverse Fourier transformation using the Stokes theorem reconstructs the image. Angular resolution is determined by the diffraction limit θ ≈ λ/D. Autocorrelation and acousto-optic spectrometers are used for spectroscopy.
key_problems: Achieving angular resolution comparable to optical (requires intercontinental baselines)
Protection from radio frequency interference of man-made origin
Development of efficient antennas for decameter waves
radio_astronomy.fun_fact: Radio telescopes are so sensitive that they could detect a cell phone signal on Mars.

formulas.description: Diffraction limit of angular resolution for a radio telescope
formulas.meaning: θ — angular resolution (radians), λ — observation wavelength (m), D — antenna diameter or interferometer baseline (m)

raw.mini: Listening to the sky on radio waves, like a radio receiver catches music, but from space.
raw.practical_application: Discovery of pulsars, cosmic microwave background radiation, studying regions of galaxies invisible in optics.
raw.description_popular: Radio astronomy explores the Universe in the radio wave range (wavelengths from fractions of a millimeter to tens of meters). Radio telescopes consist of an antenna and a sensitive receiver, often combined into interferometers to achieve high angular resolution. Key sources: synchrotron radiation from relativistic electrons in magnetic fields (supernova remnants, active galactic nuclei), spectral lines of hydrogen (21 cm) and molecules.

raw.description_simple: Radio astronomy is the study of celestial objects by radio waves (long invisible rays that many objects emit). It's like looking at the world through a thermal imager, but only for the radio band.
raw.history_simple: In 1932, Karl Jansky accidentally discovered a radio signal from the center of the Galaxy, and since then astronomers have been building radio telescopes.
raw.how_it_works_simple: Huge dish antennas pick up radio waves from space, amplify them, and turn them into images or graphs showing where and how strong the radiation is.
raw.description: Radio astronomy is a branch of astronomy that studies electromagnetic radiation from celestial bodies with wavelengths from ≈1 mm (submillimeter range) to tens of meters (decameter range). It is characterized by the use of antenna arrays and aperture synthesis methods to obtain radio images with resolution equivalent to a telescope with a diameter equal to the interferometer baseline. Receivers are cooled to cryogenic temperatures to reduce noise. Main mechanisms of radio emission: thermal (bremsstrahlung), cyclotron and synchrotron, maser, and spectral lines (HI 21 cm, CO, OH, etc.). Radio interferometry has achieved sub-millisecond resolution (VLBI networks), enabling study of galactic nuclei structure and measurement of pulsar parallaxes.

After World War II, rapid development led to the discovery of discrete sources, the synchrotron mechanism, and the 21 cm line (Ewen and Purcell, 1951). In the 1960s, Jocelyn Bell Burnell discovered pulsars, and Arno Penzias and Robert Wilson discovered the cosmic microwave background. Modern networks (ALMA, SKA) continue the tradition.

After amplification and frequency conversion, power is measured. In an interferometer, signals from multiple antennas are correlated, forming a visibility function. Inverse Fourier transformation using Stokes' theorem reconstructs the image. Angular resolution is determined by the diffraction limit θ ≈ λ/D. Autocorrelation and acousto-optic spectrometers are used for spectroscopy.

gravity.name: gravity
gravity.mini: An apple falls, planets orbit—all this is the action of an invisible attraction that binds everything with mass. Gravity keeps us on Earth and guides the Moon in its orbit.
gravity.practical_application: Without accounting for gravity, satellite launches and accurate GPS operation would be impossible: clocks on satellites run slightly faster due to lower gravity, and without corrections, a navigator would be off by kilometers.
gravity.description_popular: Gravity is the weakest of the fundamental interactions, but it shapes the large-scale structure of the Universe. Classical physics describes it with Newton's law of universal gravitation, where the force is inversely proportional to the square of the distance. General Relativity replaces force with geometry: mass and energy tell spacetime how to curve, and curved spacetime tells matter how to move.
gravity.fun_fact_popular: Clocks on the top floor of a skyscraper run slightly faster than on the ground floor: over 100 years, the difference would be about 10 microseconds—a manifestation of gravitational time dilation.
gravity.description_simple: Gravity is the force of attraction between any objects that have mass (i.e., they contain matter). The heavier the object and the closer it is, the stronger the attraction. Earth attracts us because it has huge mass. To visualize how large masses warp space, you can compare it to a stretched trampoline: a heavy ball creates a dent, and a light ball rolls toward it.
gravity.history_simple: Isaac Newton in 1687 first realized that the force that makes an apple fall also governs the Moon's motion. In the 20th century, Albert Einstein showed that gravity is not just a force but curved space and time.
gravity.how_it_works_simple: Earth attracts you because it is very massive. You also attract Earth, but your mass is tiny, so it's imperceptible. In space, gravity keeps the Moon near Earth, and Earth in orbit around the Sun.
gravity.description: Gravity is a fundamental interaction, presumably mediated by gravitons or through the geometry of spacetime. General Relativity describes it with Einstein's equations, relating the energy-momentum tensor to the curvature of spacetime. In weak fields and at low velocities, GR reduces to Newtonian gravity. Key predictions: gravitational lensing, black holes, gravitational waves, and cosmological expansion. Experiments (LIGO, Event Horizon Telescope) confirm the theory with high precision.
gravity.history: Kepler's laws (early 17th century) described planetary motions, but the universal law of gravitation was formulated by Newton. In 1915, Einstein created General Relativity, where gravity propagates at the speed of light, not instantaneously. The ideas of Henri Poincaré and Hendrik Lorentz paved the way for relativity. Direct proof of GR came with the detection of gravitational waves from black hole mergers by LIGO in 2015.
gravity.how_it_works: According to GR, mass and energy curve spacetime, and free particles move along geodesics—the shortest paths in this curvature. Einstein's equations: R_{μν} - ½ R g_{μν} = (8πG/c⁴) T_{μν} define this connection. In the weak-field limit, Newton's law F = G·m₁·m₂/r² is obtained. Gravitational waves are perturbations of the metric propagating at the speed of light. Quantum gravity attempts to describe gravitational interaction at Planck scales.
key_problems: Creating a quantum theory of gravity compatible with quantum mechanics
The nature of dark energy and the accelerated expansion of the Universe
The problem of singularities in black holes and the Big Bang
Explaining the hierarchy of fundamental interactions (why gravity is so weak)
gravity.fun_fact: The gravitational potential energy of two palms pressed together is equivalent in mass to about one red blood cell, yet this does not affect their weight.

formulas.description: Newton's law of universal gravitation
formulas.meaning: F — force of attraction between bodies, G = 6.67430×10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg² — gravitational constant, m₁, m₂ — masses of the bodies, r — distance between their centers

formulas.description: Gravitational radius (Schwarzschild radius) for a black hole
formulas.meaning: R_s — event horizon radius, G — gravitational constant, M — object's mass, c ≈ 3×10⁸ m/s — speed of light

raw.mini: An apple falls to the ground, planets revolve around the Sun—all this is the work of an invisible glue that attracts everything with mass. Gravity is what keeps our feet on the floor and the Moon in orbit.
raw.practical_application: Without accounting for gravity, satellite launches and accurate GPS operation would be impossible: clocks on satellites run slightly faster due to lower gravity, and without corrections, a navigator would be off by kilometers.
raw.description_popular: Gravity is the weakest of the fundamental interactions, but it shapes the large-scale structure of the Universe. Classical physics describes it with Newton's law, where the force is inversely proportional to the square of the distance, and General Relativity replaces force with geometry: matter tells space how to curve, and space tells matter how to move.
raw.fun_fact_popular: Clocks on the top of a skyscraper run faster than at its base: over a hundred years, the difference will be about 10 microseconds due to gravitational time dilation.
raw.description_simple: Gravity is the force of attraction between any objects that have mass (i.e., any weight). The heavier the object and the closer it is, the stronger the attraction. Imagine a trampoline: if you put a bowling ball on it, it creates a dent, and a tennis ball rolls toward it. That's how huge stars and planets warp space and attract things.
raw.history_simple: Isaac Newton in 1687 first realized that the same force that drops an apple governs the Moon's motion. And in the 20th century, Albert Einstein showed that gravity is not just a force but curved space and time.
raw.how_it_works_simple: Earth attracts you because it is very massive. You also attract Earth, but your mass is tiny, so it's imperceptible. In space, gravity keeps the Moon near Earth and Earth in orbit around the Sun.
raw.description: Gravity is a fundamental interaction, presumably mediated by gravitons or through the geometry of spacetime. In General Relativity (GR), it is described by Einstein's equations, linking the energy-momentum tensor to the curvature of spacetime. At low speeds and in weak fields, GR reduces to Newtonian gravity. Key predictions include gravitational lensing, black holes, gravitational waves, and cosmological expansion. Modern experiments, such as LIGO and the Event Horizon Telescope, confirm the theory with high precision.
raw.history: Kepler's laws (early 17th century) described planetary motions, but it took the genius of Newton to create the universal law of gravitation. In 1915, Einstein proposed General Relativity, replacing Newtonian instantaneous action with the finite speed of gravitational propagation. Henri Poincaré and Hendrik Lorentz anticipated relativistic ideas. In 2015, LIGO detectors recorded gravitational waves from a black hole merger, providing direct proof of GR.
raw.how_it_works: According to GR, mass and energy curve spacetime, and free particles move along geodesics—the shortest paths in this curvature. Einstein's equations: R_{μν} - ½ R g_{μν} = (8πG/c⁴) T_{μν} establish this connection. In weak fields and at low velocities, the approximation yields Newton's law F = G·m₁·m₂/r². Gravitational waves are perturbations of the metric propagating at the speed of light. Quantum gravity attempts to describe interactions at Planck scales, where spacetime may become 'foamy'.
key_problems: Creating a quantum theory of gravity compatible with quantum mechanics
The nature of dark energy and the accelerated expansion of the Universe
The problem of singularities in black holes and the Big Bang
Explaining the hierarchy of fundamental interactions (why gravity is so weak)
raw.fun_fact: Paradox: the gravitational energy of two palms pressed together is roughly equal to the mass of one red blood cell, but if you bring the palms into contact, this energy does not noticeably change their mass.

inflation.name: inflation
inflation.mini: Cosmic inflation is an instantaneous, ultra-fast stretching of space right after the birth of the Universe, smoothing it to homogeneity like taut tension straightens a crumpled sheet.
inflation.practical_application: Inflation explains why the Universe is so flat and homogeneous, and predicts specific temperature patterns in the cosmic microwave background, detected by the COBE and WMAP satellites.
inflation.description_popular: Cosmic inflation is a hypothetical stage in the early Universe's history when its size grew exponentially: over equal tiny time intervals, the scale increased by the same factor. It lasted from roughly 10⁻³⁶ to 10⁻³² seconds after the Big Bang, and during this time the Universe expanded by at least a factor of 10²⁶. The cause of such rapid growth was a special field (inflaton) with negative pressure — it created not attraction but powerful repulsion, similar to antigravity.
inflation.fun_fact_popular: Thanks to inflation, opposite points on the sky, separated by billions of light-years, have the same temperature: before inflation they were close together and had time to reach thermal equilibrium.
inflation.description_simple: In the very first moments after the Big Bang, a tiny region of space suddenly began expanding with colossal acceleration. In a minuscule fraction of a second, it inflated to sizes exceeding the entire currently visible Universe. This process is called inflation. It made space very flat and homogeneous — like the surface of a huge balloon appears flat to an ant.
inflation.history_simple: The idea of inflation was proposed by American physicist Alan Guth in 1980. He sought to explain the mysteries of the Big Bang — for example, why the Universe is so homogeneous in all directions.
inflation.how_it_works_simple: A special field arose in space, similar to a magnetic field but causing repulsion instead of attraction. It forced space to expand faster than light (the expansion speed of space itself is not limited by the speed of light). Later this field decayed, and its enormous energy turned into particles, from which galaxies later formed.
inflation.description: Cosmological inflation is a stage of exponential expansion of the early Universe, proposed to explain its large-scale homogeneity, geometric flatness, and the absence of relic magnetic monopoles. It solves the horizon problem (why distant regions look the same, though they could never have exchanged light) and the flatness problem (why the curvature of space is close to zero). Before inflation, all currently causally disconnected regions were within a single horizon, and inflation smoothed out the curvature. Moreover, quantum fluctuations of the inflaton field, stretched to cosmological scales, gave rise to the seeds of future galaxy clusters.
inflation.history: The concept of inflation was put forward by Alan Guth in 1980, but his initial model encountered the graceful exit problem (inflation couldn't end smoothly). Soon Andrei Linde, as well as Paul Steinhardt and Andreas Albrecht independently proposed the slow-roll model, solving this difficulty. Later Linde developed the theory of chaotic inflation. The ideas of inflation build on earlier work by Alexei Starobinsky and on the Big Bang theory of Georges Lemaître.
inflation.how_it_works: Inflation is driven by the inflaton — a scalar field slowly rolling toward the minimum of its potential. According to Einstein's equations, a field with negative pressure generates accelerated expansion. Quantum fluctuations of this field during inflation turn into perturbations of the spacetime metric. After inflation ends, they become adiabatic density perturbations with a nearly scale-invariant spectrum (n_s < 1), which is confirmed by CMB observations. Inflation also predicts primordial gravitational waves.
key_problems: What is the physical nature of the inflaton field?
What exactly started and stopped inflation at such an early stage?
Is inflation a necessary consequence of a fundamental theory, or just a convenient hypothesis?
Are there unambiguous direct proofs of inflation?
inflation.fun_fact: Quantum fluctuations, magnified by inflation, became the large-scale structure of the Universe — thus the microworld determined the pattern of galaxies.

formulas.description: Exponential expansion of the scale factor at constant Hubble parameter
formulas.meaning: a(t) — scale factor (characterizing the size of the Universe at time t), a₀ — initial scale factor, H — Hubble parameter (approximately constant during inflation), t — time

raw.mini: Cosmic inflation — ultra-fast stretching of space at the dawn of time, smoothing the universe to homogeneity, like strong tension straightens a crumpled sheet.
raw.practical_application: Inflation explains why the Universe is flat and homogeneous, and predicts patterns in the CMB, confirmed by the COBE and WMAP satellites.
raw.description_popular: Inflation is a hypothetical stage of exponential expansion (growth with a constant factor, where size increases by the same number of times over equal intervals) of the Universe, lasting from about 10⁻³⁶ to 10⁻³² seconds after the Big Bang. The scale increased by at least a factor of 10²⁶. Inflation was driven by an inflaton field (hypothetical field causing inflation) with large negative pressure (pressure directed outward and accelerating expansion), acting like antigravity.
raw.fun_fact_popular: Inflation explains why the Universe looks the same from one side and the other — regions far apart were once in causal contact before inflation.
raw.description_simple: Right after the Big Bang (the name given to the beginning of the Universe's expansion from a super-dense state), it began to expand. But in the first 10⁻³⁵ seconds (an unimaginably brief moment) the expansion accelerated so that a tiny piece of space inflated to the size of the entire currently visible Universe. This is inflation. It made the Universe flat and homogeneous — like the surface of an inflated balloon appears flat to an ant.
raw.history_simple: The idea of inflation was proposed by American physicist Alan Guth in 1980 to solve the mysteries of the Big Bang, for example, why the Universe is so homogeneous.
raw.how_it_works_simple: A field appeared in space (an invisible force, similar to a magnetic field) that created repulsion and inflated space faster than light. When this field decayed, its energy turned into particles that later assembled into galaxies.
raw.description: Cosmological inflation is a period of exponential expansion of the early Universe, proposed to explain large-scale homogeneity, flatness, and the absence of magnetic monopoles. Inflation solves the horizon and flatness problems: before inflation, all regions that now appear causally disconnected were within a single horizon, and the high energy density of the inflaton field made the curvature of space negligibly small. Inflation also generates primordial density perturbations from quantum fluctuations stretched to macroscopic scales, which serve as seeds for large-scale structure formation.
raw.history: The concept of inflation was proposed by Alan Guth in 1980, but his initial model had a 'graceful exit' problem. Soon Andrei Linde, as well as Paul Steinhardt and Andreas Albrecht independently developed the 'new inflation' model, fixing this flaw. Further development led to Linde's chaotic inflation models. Inflation ideas build on earlier work by Alexei Starobinsky and on the Big Bang theory laid down by Georges Lemaître.
raw.how_it_works: Inflation is driven by a scalar field (inflaton) in a slow-roll potential. Einstein's equations for a homogeneous isotropic universe give accelerated expansion if a field with negative pressure dominates. Quantum fluctuations of this field lead to metric perturbations, which after inflation turn into adiabatic density perturbations, predicting a slightly red spectrum (n_s < 1). Inflation also predicts relic gravitational waves.
key_problems: The nature of the inflaton field is unknown
Why inflation started and ended exactly at these times
Is inflation uniquely predicted or just a convenient hypothesis
Are there direct proofs of inflation
raw.fun_fact: Inflation stretched quantum fluctuations to the size of galaxy clusters — the quantum world gave rise to the largest structures.

metallicity.name: metallicity
metallicity.mini: The chemical shade of a star: how many 'heavy' elements (in astronomy, all elements heavier than helium, from lithium to uranium, are called metals) it contains compared to the original hydrogen and helium. The higher the metallicity, the more the star is saturated with the products of the life cycles of previous generations of stars.
metallicity.practical_application: Metallicity determines a star's color, temperature, lifetime, and the probability of rocky planet formation around it; for galaxies, it is a chronicle of chemical evolution, allowing the reconstruction of the history of star formation and mergers.
metallicity.description_popular: Metallicity (usually denoted as Z) is a quantitative characteristic that determines what fraction of the mass of a star or interstellar gas consists of elements heavier than helium. Although astrophysicists call them 'metals', this group includes carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and even uranium and gold. The index [Fe/H] is widely used — the difference of the decimal logarithms of the ratio of the number of iron atoms to the number of hydrogen atoms in the star and in the Sun: [Fe/H] = log₁₀(N_Fe/N_H) – log₁₀(N_Fe/N_H)_⊙. Iron was chosen because its lines are easy to measure in spectra. Solar metallicity by mass Z_⊙ ≈ 0.016 (according to modern data ~0.012). Stars with high Z have stronger winds due to many spectral lines that absorb radiation, and they are cooler at the same mass. The presence of giant planets is statistically correlated with increased metallicity of the parent stars.
metallicity.fun_fact_popular: The record low-metallicity star SMSS 0313-6708 has [Fe/H] ≈ –7.4 — meaning it has almost 25 million times less iron than the Sun. It formed from gas enriched probably by just one supernova explosion in the early Universe.
metallicity.description_simple: In astrophysics, all elements heavier than helium — from carbon to iron and uranium — are called metals. Metallicity indicates what fraction of a star’s or gas cloud’s mass consists of these heavy atoms. It’s like a recipe: the Sun has about 1.6% metals, while the oldest stars in the galactic halo have only 0.001–0.01%. The rest is hydrogen (about 70%) and helium (about 28%), left over from the Big Bang. Metallicity is often expressed through the iron-to-hydrogen ratio [Fe/H] on a logarithmic scale relative to the Sun: 0 is solar value, –1 means 10 times less, +1 means 10 times more.
metallicity.history_simple: In the first half of the 20th century, while studying stellar spectra, astronomers noticed that some stars had stronger absorption lines of metals than others. In 1925, Cecilia Payne-Gaposchkin convincingly showed in her dissertation that hydrogen is the main element of stars, and differences in line intensities are explained by conditions in atmospheres. Later, Walter Baade introduced the concepts of stellar populations I and II, linking them to the abundance of heavy elements.
metallicity.how_it_works_simple: Heavy elements are born inside massive stars through thermonuclear reactions and are scattered into interstellar space by supernova explosions or stellar winds. New stars condense from this enriched gas, so each subsequent generation gets a little more metals. Thus, the oldest Population II stars are very metal-poor ([Fe/H] < –1), while young ones like our Sun (Population I) are relatively rich. It’s like a family heirloom: each generation adds new 'jewels' (elements) to the common treasury.
metallicity.description: Metallicity Z is the mass fraction of all chemical elements except hydrogen and helium. Since most metals are synthesized in stars, their abundance serves as a cosmic clock: low Z indicates a primitive, early age of the object. The logarithmic index [Fe/H] is used as a proxy because iron lines are abundant and easy to measure in the optical range. Sometimes the ratio of α-elements (O, Mg, Si, …) to iron — [α/Fe] — is analyzed separately, reflecting the relative contribution of supernovae of different types. Metallicity critically affects stellar evolution: it sets the opacity (the absorption coefficient is determined by bound-bound and bound-free transitions), and hence the energy transport regime (radiative or convective), effective temperature, and main-sequence lifetime. In the interstellar medium, the amount of dust increases with Z, enhancing cooling and facilitating molecule formation. In galaxies, a radial metallicity gradient is observed — a decline from the center to the periphery, caused by the history of star formation 'from the inside out'.
metallicity.history: At the beginning of the 20th century, spectroscopists noticed that stars differ in the intensity of metal lines. The key breakthrough was made by Cecilia Payne-Gaposchkin in 1925: applying Saha’s ionization theory, she proved that stars consist mainly of hydrogen, and differences in spectra are explained by temperature, not composition. In the 1940s, Walter Baade, studying galaxies resolved into individual stars, identified Population I (metal-rich, disk) and Population II (metal-poor, halo). With the advent of high-sensitivity spectroscopy and large-scale surveys (SDSS, Gaia), the metallicities of millions of stars have been measured, allowing the reconstruction of the chemical evolution of the Milky Way.
metallicity.how_it_works: Elements heavier than helium, except for a small amount of lithium and beryllium, are synthesized inside stars. On the main sequence, hydrogen is converted into helium, then in later stages helium is converted into carbon, oxygen, and so on up to iron (the peak of the binding energy curve). Heavy nuclei (copper, gold, uranium) are created by neutron capture processes: the slow s-process in the shells of asymptotic giant branch stars and the rapid r-process during supernova explosions or neutron star mergers. The products of nucleosynthesis are ejected into the interstellar medium through winds, planetary nebulae, and explosions. Subsequent generations of stars form from this enriched material, increasing the average metallicity with time. The observed distribution of [Fe/H] in different components of the Galaxy (thin disk, thick disk, halo) reflects their age and migration. Thus, massive elliptical galaxies retain metals in a deep potential well and exhibit high Z. In stellar models, metallicity enters into the equation of state, opacity (calculated using OPAL or OP grids), and nuclear reaction rates. For spectroscopic determination of [Fe/H], the measured equivalent widths of Fe I and Fe II lines are compared with synthetic spectra, solving the transfer equations assuming local thermodynamic equilibrium (LTE) or taking into account non-LTE deviations.
key_problems: What is the true fraction of metals locked in dust grains in different phases of the interstellar medium?
How do inhomogeneous mixing and radial migration of stars affect the metallicity gradient of the Galaxy?
What are the properties of the very first stars (Population III) with zero metallicity, and how did they enrich the early intergalactic medium?
How does metallicity influence the formation of supermassive black holes and the initial mass function?
Does the predicted extremely metal-poor gas exist in the circumgalactic medium, and how can it be detected?
metallicity.fun_fact: The Sun belongs to the ~10% most metal-rich stars of its age in the solar neighborhood. This 'super-metallicity' likely aided the formation of rocky terrestrial planets and, perhaps, the emergence of life.

formulas.description: Logarithmic measure of metallicity based on the iron-to-hydrogen ratio
formulas.meaning: N_Fe — number of iron atoms, N_H — number of hydrogen atoms, the ⊙ symbol denotes solar values (for the Sun, log₁₀(N_Fe/N_H)⊙ ≈ –4.5)

formulas.description: Approximate relation between the mass fraction of metals Z and the index [Fe/H]
formulas.meaning: Z — mass fraction of metals, Z_⊙ — solar metallicity (Z_⊙ ≈ 0.0122 according to Asplund et al. 2009)

star_formation.name: star formation
star_formation.mini: The process of star birth from giant cold clouds of gas and dust: as if an invisible hand pulls together a tenuous nebula into a dazzling plasma sphere, igniting a thermonuclear furnace.
star_formation.practical_application: Understanding the mechanisms of star formation is necessary to explain why galaxies look the way they do, where the chemical elements that make up us and our planet come from, and to predict the prevalence of planetary systems.
star_formation.description_popular: Star formation is a multi-stage process triggered in the densest cores of giant molecular clouds (with masses up to 10⁶ solar masses). External influences — spiral density waves, shock waves from supernovae, cloud collisions — create local over-densities. Gravitational instability overcomes the combined support of thermal pressure, magnetic fields, and turbulence. The initial isothermal collapse transitions to adiabatic when the core becomes opaque to its own radiation. A protostar forms with an accretion disk and bipolar outflows. When the central temperature reaches millions of degrees, deuterium ignites first, and at ~10⁷ K, hydrogen ignites in the pp-chain or CNO cycle. The star arrives on the main sequence, where it spends almost its entire life. The mass distribution of newborns (the initial mass function) is roughly universal: many low-mass and very few massive stars.
star_formation.fun_fact_popular: The Orion Nebula is the nearest 'stellar nursery' to us (about 1300 light-years away), where among the glowing gas one can see protoplanetary disks like dark silhouettes against a bright background. Planets are assembling in them right now.
star_formation.description_simple: The birth of a star begins in a cold (10–30 degrees above absolute zero) and dense molecular cloud, composed mainly of hydrogen and dust. In such clouds, stretching over many light-years, clumps — pockets of slightly higher density — form randomly. Gravity pulls the material together, and it heats up (like a pump compressing air). If the clump can get rid of heat (radiating it as infrared light through dust grains), the collapse continues. At the center, a dense core forms — a protostar, surrounded by a rotating disk. When the core temperature reaches about 10 million degrees, a thermonuclear reaction ignites: hydrogen converts into helium, releasing colossal energy — the star turns on. The remaining disk material can clump together into planets.
star_formation.history_simple: Ancient observers considered stars eternal, but already in the 18th century, Immanuel Kant and Pierre-Simon Laplace proposed the nebular hypothesis (from Latin nebula — mist): stars form from rotating gas clouds. In the early 20th century, James Jeans mathematically described the conditions for gravitational collapse. And with the development of infrared astronomy in the second half of the 20th century, astronomers were able to peer into the dense dust and directly observe protostars.
star_formation.how_it_works_simple: The main battle in star formation is between gravity, trying to compress the cloud, and thermal pressure, which pushes it outward. If the cloud cools effectively (radiating energy), gravity wins. As it contracts, however, the material spins up due to conservation of angular momentum, so a disk forms around the protostar. Some gas is ejected along the rotation axis in narrow jets. The mass of the newborn star determines its fate: lightweight red dwarfs shine dimly for trillions of years, while heavy giants burn out quickly and explode as supernovae.
star_formation.description: Star formation is the astrophysical process of transforming interstellar gas into stars. It takes place in the superdense cores of molecular clouds (temperature 10–30 K, density 10³–10⁶ cm⁻³, dominated by H₂ with traces of CO and dust). Key stages: (1) Collapse when the Jeans criterion is satisfied (M > M_J); otherwise the cloud stabilizes. Magnetic flux can slow collapse (critical mass-to-flux ratio). Free-fall time is t_ff ~ (Gρ)^-1/2 ~ 10⁵ years. (2) Formation of the first hydrostatic core (size ~5 AU, ~100 K) and subsequent second collapse upon dissociation of molecular hydrogen, leading to a stellar core. (3) The protostar grows via disk accretion; magnetocentrifugal winds carry away angular momentum. Accretion is episodic (due to gravitational or magnetorotational instabilities). (4) In the pre-main-sequence phase, the protostar contracts along Hayashi or Henyey tracks until hydrogen ignites in the core. Massive stars (≥8 M⊙) reach the main sequence while still accreting. The efficiency of star formation per free-fall time is low (ε ~ 0.01). The initial mass function (IMF) is approximated by a power law dN/dM ∝ M^-α, α ≈ 2.35 for M > 0.5 M⊙, with a log-normal downturn at low masses. Feedback from protostellar jets and radiation regulates efficiency, and massive stars, with their hard radiation and explosions, can disrupt the parent cloud.
star_formation.history: The nebular hypothesis was proposed in the 18th century by Swedenborg, Kant, and Laplace. In the early 20th century, James Jeans examined the gravitational instability of a homogeneous medium (Jeans criterion). The discovery of dark nebulae by Barnard and others confirmed the existence of cold clouds. Infrared astronomy (IRAS, Spitzer, Herschel) in the 1970s–2010s revealed protostars, and the ALMA interferometer allowed seeing disks and jets in detail. Subrahmanyan Chandrasekhar made a fundamental contribution to the theory of stellar structure and evolution, formulating the Chandrasekhar limit for white dwarfs.
star_formation.how_it_works: Star formation is governed by the balance of gravity, pressure, magnetic fields, and turbulence. Jeans criterion: a cloud of mass M collapses if M > M_J = (π/6) c_s³ G^(-3/2) ρ^(-1/2). In magnetized media, the critical mass-to-magnetic flux ratio must be exceeded. Efficient cooling via dust emission in the far-IR ensures isothermality of the early collapse. Once the optical depth reaches about unity, the core heats up. Conservation of angular momentum leads to the formation of an accretion disk; magnetic braking can reduce it. Accretion onto the protostar generates shock waves emitting in the UV and optical. At ~10⁶ K, deuterium ignites; at ~10⁷ K, hydrogen ignites. For massive stars, radiation pressure plays a role, which can halt spherical accretion; material falls through the disk. Bipolar outflows (jets) carry away excess material and angular momentum. On galactic scales, the star formation rate is related to the gas surface density by the empirical Kennicutt-Schmidt law: Σ_SFR ∝ Σ_gas^n, where n ≈ 1.4. Key equations: hydrostatic equilibrium dP/dr = -G M_r ρ/r², continuity equation, energy transport and nuclear energy generation equations, equation of state (ideal gas with radiation pressure), opacity. The initial mass function (IMF) results from turbulent fragmentation and thermal physics; the IMF Salpeter law is often used for high masses.
key_problems: What determines the initial mass function and its possible non-universality in extreme conditions?
How does feedback from stars (radiation, winds, supernovae) communicate to the multiphase interstellar medium and regulate the global star formation rate?
What is the role of magnetic fields in the formation of protostellar disks and the launching of outflows?
How do the first stars (Population III) form in metal-free gas, and what are their characteristic masses?
How does the star formation process change in extreme environments — in galactic centers, during mergers, in the early Universe?
star_formation.fun_fact: In regions like the Orion Nebula, over a thousand stars are born in a few million years, but the efficiency is low: only 10–20% of the cloud's mass turns into stars, the rest is swept away by powerful radiation and winds from the most massive newborns. These same winds, compressing the cloud's edge, can trigger a new wave of star formation.

formulas.description: Jeans critical mass for gravitational collapse
formulas.meaning: c_s — speed of sound in the cloud (determined by temperature and composition), G — gravitational constant (6.674×10⁻¹¹ m³ kg⁻¹ s⁻²), ρ — gas density

formulas.description: Free-fall time — characteristic collapse time of a uniform pressure-free cloud
formulas.meaning: G — gravitational constant, ρ — mean density of the cloud

formulas.description: Empirical Kennicutt-Schmidt law relating surface density of star formation rate to gas
formulas.meaning: Σ_SFR — surface density of star formation rate (M⊙ yr⁻¹ kpc⁻²), Σ_gas — surface density of gas (M⊙ pc⁻²), A — efficiency constant, n ≈ 1.4

electromagnetism.name: electromagnetism
electromagnetism.mini: Electricity and magnetism are two sides of one force: moving charges create magnetism, and a changing magnetic field generates electricity. This is the basis of generators, motors and wireless communication.
electromagnetism.practical_application: All modern communications, from Wi-Fi to satellites, use electromagnetic waves, and without electromagnetism, electric motors, dynamos and MRI scanners would not work. From electric lighting to radio, television and the internet—all this is embodied electromagnetism.
electromagnetism.description_popular: Maxwell's equations describe how electric and magnetic fields are created by charges and currents and how they mutually generate each other. A changing electric field creates a vortex magnetic field, and a changing magnetic field creates a vortex electric field. This tandem propagates in a vacuum as an electromagnetic wave at speed c = 1/√(μ₀ε₀), where ε₀ and μ₀ are the electric and magnetic constants, measured in experiments with charges and currents. The spectrum of these waves ranges from low-frequency radio waves to gamma rays. Quantum electrodynamics complements the picture, describing the interaction as an exchange of photons—massless particles of light.
electromagnetism.fun_fact_popular: The speed of light c is not just a speed, but a limit embedded in the very laws of electromagnetism; the sizes of atoms and chemistry depend on it, and hence the conditions for life in the Universe.
electromagnetism.description_simple: Electricity (movement of charges) and magnetism (forces that attract iron and turn a compass needle) are two sides of one fundamental interaction—electromagnetism. A flow of electrons (tiny negatively charged particles) through a wire creates a magnetic field around it (the region of magnetic forces). And vice versa: if you quickly move a magnet inside a wire coil, an electric current appears in it. This 'dance' generates waves that spread through space at the speed of light: from radio waves and microwaves to visible light and X-rays.
electromagnetism.history_simple: Until the beginning of the 19th century, electricity and magnetism were considered independent. In 1820, Hans Christian Ørsted discovered that a current deflects a magnetic needle, and Michael Faraday in 1831 showed that a moving magnet creates a current in a coil. In the 1860s, the Scottish physicist James Clerk Maxwell generalized these laws into a system of equations that not only described all known phenomena but also predicted electromagnetic waves.
electromagnetism.how_it_works_simple: When electrons in an antenna oscillate, they create changing electric and magnetic fields around them, which, like ripples on water, spread through space as an electromagnetic wave. The eye perceives high-frequency waves as light, and an antenna picks up radio frequencies as a Wi-Fi signal.
electromagnetism.description: The electromagnetic interaction is one of the four fundamental interactions of nature, responsible for the forces between electrically charged particles and for all classical electromagnetic phenomena. Classical electrodynamics is unified by four Maxwell equations: Gauss's law for electricity, Gauss's law for magnetism, Faraday's law of induction, Ampère's law with Maxwell's correction. Their solution yields electromagnetic waves traveling at speed c. In quantum electrodynamics, the interaction is represented as the exchange of virtual photons—massless gauge bosons. The absence of mass for the photon explains the infinite range and the inverse-square law. The U(1) gauge symmetry guarantees the conservation of electric charge and underlies the Standard Model.
electromagnetism.history: The recognition of the connection between electricity and magnetism began with the experiment of H.C. Ørsted (1820), showing that a current causes a magnetic needle to deflect. M. Faraday in 1831 discovered electromagnetic induction—the emergence of current in a circuit when the magnetic flux changes. A brilliant synthesis was accomplished by James Clerk Maxwell: in 1861–64 he formulated a system of equations, adding to Ampère's law the 'displacement current'—a term with the time derivative of the electric field—which ensured consistency and predicted waves. The existence of waves was experimentally confirmed by H. Hertz in 1887. Hendrik Lorentz in 1895 introduced the Lorentz force and created the electron theory, explaining the behavior of charges at the microlevel. The quantum stage was completed in the mid-20th century by the works of R. Feynman, J. Schwinger and S. Tomonaga, who built renormalizable quantum electrodynamics.
electromagnetism.how_it_works: Electrodynamics obeys the principle of relativity and is invariant under Lorentz transformations. From Maxwell's equations follows the wave equation for the fields. In quantum electrodynamics, the state of a system is described by a vector in Fock space, creation and annihilation operators for photons are built from the potentials, and the interaction with matter (fermions) is given by the interaction Lagrangian eψ̄γ^μψA_μ (where e is the charge, ψ is the Dirac field, A_μ is the electromagnetic potential). Scattering processes are calculated perturbatively in the fine-structure constant α ≈ 1/137 using Feynman diagrams. At small distances, the effective charge increases (vacuum polarization effect), which has been verified with high accuracy. The classical limit gives the Lorentz force F = q(E + v × B), describing the motion of charged bodies in fields.
key_problems: The absence of observed magnetic monopoles, although Maxwell's equations allow them.
The reason for electric charge quantization: why charge is always a multiple of the elementary charge.
Combining quantum electrodynamics with general relativity to describe strong gravitational fields.
electromagnetism.fun_fact: Modern experiments test Coulomb's law down to distances of 10⁻¹⁸ m, and it has not been violated so far—the inverse-square dependence holds with fantastic accuracy. This is one of the most precisely tested laws of physics.

formulas.description: Maxwell-Faraday equation: a changing magnetic field creates a vortex electric field.
formulas.meaning: ∇× is the curl (vortex) of the field, E is the electric field vector (V/m), B is the magnetic induction vector (T), ∂/∂t is the partial derivative with respect to time.

formulas.description: Speed of light as a consequence of the electromagnetic properties of the vacuum.
formulas.meaning: c is the speed of light in vacuum (c ≈ 3×10⁸ m/s), μ₀ is the magnetic constant (μ₀ = 4π×10⁻⁷ N/A²), ε₀ is the electric constant (ε₀ ≈ 8.854×10⁻¹² F/m).

raw.mini: A magnet in a coil generates current, and current through a coil attracts iron—two forces, electricity and magnetism, are fused into a single whole. Generators, motors and all radio waves are based on this.
raw.practical_application: All modern communications, from Wi-Fi to satellites, use electromagnetic waves, and without electromagnetism, electric motors, dynamos and MRI scanners would not work.
raw.description_popular: Maxwell's equations describe how electric and magnetic fields are created by charges and currents and how they mutually generate each other. A changing electric field creates a vortex magnetic field, and a changing magnetic field creates a vortex electric field. This tandem propagates in a vacuum as an electromagnetic wave at speed c = 1/√(μ₀ε₀), where μ₀ is the magnetic constant and ε₀ is the electric constant (characteristics determined from laboratory measurements of current and charge). The spectrum of these waves ranges from low-frequency radio waves to gamma rays. Quantum electrodynamics complements the picture, describing the interaction as an exchange of photons.
raw.fun_fact_popular: If the speed of light were different, the sizes of atoms and all chemical bonds would change, and hence the very possibility of life in the Universe.
raw.description_simple: Electricity (movement of charges) and magnetism (forces that attract iron and turn a compass needle) are two sides of one fundamental interaction—electromagnetism. A flow of electrons (tiny negatively charged particles) through a wire creates a magnetic field around it (the region of magnetic forces). And vice versa: if you quickly move a magnet inside a wire coil, an electric current appears in it. This 'dance' generates waves that spread through space at the speed of light: from radio waves and microwaves to visible light and X-rays.
raw.history_simple: For a long time people considered electricity and magnetism unrelated phenomena. The experiments of Hans Christian Ørsted (1820) and Michael Faraday (1830s) first demonstrated their deep connection. The Scottish physicist James Clerk Maxwell in the 1860s cast these ideas into strict mathematical form—the famous four equations. They unified all the known laws of electricity and magnetism and predicted the existence of electromagnetic waves.
raw.how_it_works_simple: Throw a stone into a pond—ripples will appear. An oscillating electric charge (an electron quivering in an antenna) generates 'ripples' in the electromagnetic field—a wave. This wave carries energy and information. Our retina picks up such microscopic ripples as light; a phone's antenna—as a Wi-Fi signal.
raw.description: The electromagnetic interaction is one of the four fundamental interactions of nature, responsible for the forces between electrically charged particles and for all classical electromagnetic phenomena. Classical electrodynamics is unified by four Maxwell equations: Gauss's law for electricity, Gauss's law for magnetism, Faraday's law of induction, Ampère's law with Maxwell's correction. Their solution yields electromagnetic waves traveling at speed c. In quantum electrodynamics, the interaction is represented as the exchange of virtual photons—massless gauge bosons. The absence of mass for the photon explains the infinite range and the inverse-square law. The U(1) gauge symmetry guarantees the conservation of electric charge and underlies the Standard Model.
raw.history: The recognition of the connection between electricity and magnetism began with the experiment of H.C. Ørsted (1820), showing that a current causes a magnetic needle to deflect. M. Faraday in 1831 discovered electromagnetic induction—the emergence of current in a circuit when the magnetic flux changes. A brilliant synthesis was accomplished by James Clerk Maxwell: in 1861–64 he formulated a system of equations, adding to Ampère's law the 'displacement current'—a term with the time derivative of the electric field—which ensured consistency and predicted waves. The existence of waves was experimentally confirmed by H. Hertz in 1887. Hendrik Lorentz in 1895 introduced the Lorentz force and created the electron theory, explaining the behavior of charges at the microlevel. The quantum stage was completed in the mid-20th century by the works of R. Feynman, J. Schwinger and S. Tomonaga, who built renormalizable quantum electrodynamics.
raw.how_it_works: Electrodynamics obeys the principle of relativity and is invariant under Lorentz transformations. From Maxwell's equations follows the wave equation for the fields. In quantum electrodynamics, the state of a system is described by a vector in Fock space, creation and annihilation operators for photons are built from the potentials, and the interaction with matter (fermions) is given by the interaction Lagrangian eψ̄γ^μψA_μ (where e is the charge, ψ is the Dirac field, A_μ is the electromagnetic potential). Scattering processes are calculated perturbatively in the fine-structure constant α ≈ 1/137 using Feynman diagrams. At small distances, the effective charge increases (vacuum polarization effect), which has been verified with high accuracy. The classical limit gives the Lorentz force F = q(E + v × B), describing the motion of charged bodies in fields.
key_problems: The magnetic monopole problem: why do all known magnets have two poles, despite the symmetry of the equations allowing isolated magnetic charges?
Electric charge quantization: why are the charges of all observed particles multiples of the elementary charge?
Unification with gravity at the quantum level in a single theory.
raw.fun_fact: Modern experiments test Coulomb's law down to distances of 10⁻¹⁸ m, and it has not been violated so far—the inverse-square dependence holds with fantastic accuracy.

silicon.name: silicon
silicon.mini: Hidden in sand and rocks, and in the form of the thinnest wafers it serves as the 'brain' of every digital device.
silicon.practical_application: Microchips in every phone and laptop; solar panels on roofs; glass and concrete around us.
silicon.description_popular: Silicon (Si) is a metalloid, i.e., an element with properties intermediate between metals and nonmetals. Its atoms are arranged in a diamond-like lattice. It is the second most abundant element in the Earth's crust, found in quartz and silicates. A bandgap of 1.1 eV makes it a classic semiconductor: doping with phosphorus creates free electrons (n-type), and with boron—holes (p-type). The contact of p- and n-regions forms a p–n junction, the basis of diodes, transistors and solar cells.
silicon.fun_fact_popular: The whole Silicon Valley is named after this element. The first silicon transistor was made in 1954, and since then the number of transistors on a chip has been doubling approximately every two years (Moore's law).
silicon.description_simple: Silicon is the main element of sand. It is a semiconductor: in pure form it hardly conducts current, but small additions turn it into a controlled conductor. It is this property that allows creating transistors and microchips.
silicon.history_simple: Swedish chemist Jöns Jacob Berzelius first obtained pure silicon in 1824. But its electronic talents were discovered much later. In the 20th century, scientists learned to purify silicon and add impurities (doping), creating the transistor—the digital age began.
silicon.how_it_works_simple: Pure silicon blocks the movement of charges, like a roadblock. A small addition of boron creates vacant spots—'holes', through which current is passed along a chain. The addition of phosphorus, conversely, gives extra electrons. By combining regions with different dopants, we get switches that process information.
silicon.description: Silicon crystallizes in the diamond structure (lattice constant 0.543 nm). Its bandgap is indirect: an electron transition between bands requires the participation of a phonon, so silicon emits light weakly but absorbs it effectively. Electronic properties are explained by band theory, based on the Schrödinger equation and the Pauli principle. Silicon chip technology (CMOS) dominates thanks to the high-quality native oxide SiO₂, used as a gate insulator in MOSFETs. Doping introduces impurity levels into the bandgap, controlling the concentration of charge carriers.
silicon.history: Jöns Jacob Berzelius isolated amorphous silicon in 1824, and Henri Sainte-Claire Deville obtained crystalline silicon in 1854. The first point-contact transistor (1947) worked on germanium, but silicon quickly replaced it. In 1954, Morris Tanenbaum created the first silicon transistor at Bell Labs. Leo Esaki contributed to the understanding of junctions by investigating quantum tunneling and inventing the tunnel diode. Since then, miniaturization has progressed exponentially, approaching physical limits.
silicon.how_it_works: When n- and p-regions come into contact, a depletion region arises, creating a potential barrier. Under forward bias, the current increases exponentially; under reverse bias, it is blocked. This rectifying property is described by the diode equation. In metal-oxide-semiconductor field-effect transistors (MOSFETs), the voltage on the gate controls the conductivity of the channel. Operation is based on electrostatics, the Poisson equation, and carrier statistics (Fermi–Dirac distribution). In solar cells, the p–n junction separates photogenerated electron-hole pairs, generating an electric current.
key_problems: Physical limits of miniaturization of silicon transistors (quantum tunneling at nanometer scales)
Development of silicon photonics for high-speed data transfer within chips
Increasing the efficiency of silicon solar cells beyond the Shockley-Queisser limit
silicon.fun_fact: Pure silicon shines like a metal, but in powder form it looks dark grey. Silicon wafers for chips are polished to atomic smoothness—they are flatter than any mirror.

formulas.description: Industrial production of silicon by reducing quartz with carbon
formulas.meaning: SiO₂ — silicon dioxide (quartz, sand), C — carbon (coke), Si — elemental silicon, CO — carbon monoxide (carbon monoxide).

raw.mini: It makes up sand and rocks; sliced into wafers, it becomes the 'brain' of computers.
raw.practical_application: Microchips in every phone and laptop; solar panels on roofs; glass and concrete around us.
raw.description_popular: Silicon (Si, atomic number 14) is a metalloid with a diamond-like crystal lattice. It is the second most abundant element in the Earth's crust (after oxygen), occurring in quartz and silicates. A bandgap of 1.1 eV makes it a classic semiconductor: doping with phosphorus creates free electrons (n-type), and with boron—holes (p-type). The contact of p- and n-regions forms a p–n junction, the basis of diodes, transistors and solar cells.
raw.fun_fact_popular: The whole Silicon Valley is named after this element. The first silicon transistor was made in 1954, and since then the number of transistors on a chip has been doubling approximately every two years (Moore's law).
raw.description_simple: Silicon is the element that makes up almost entirely sand. It is not a metal, but neither an insulator—it is a semiconductor (it can conduct current under certain conditions). By adding tiny impurities, we control the electric flow—this is how transistors and computer chips work.
raw.history_simple: Swedish chemist Jöns Jacob Berzelius first obtained pure silicon in 1824. But its electronic talents were discovered much later. In the 20th century, scientists learned to purify silicon and add impurities (doping), creating the transistor—the digital age began.
raw.how_it_works_simple: Pure silicon is like a roadblock for electricity. Add a little boron—and 'holes' appear, through which current runs like a green wave of a traffic light. And if you add phosphorus, extra electrons will run. Combining such regions, we get tiny switches that count and remember information.
raw.description: Silicon crystallizes in the diamond structure (lattice constant 0.543 nm). Its bandgap is indirect: an electron transition between bands requires the participation of a phonon, so silicon emits light weakly but absorbs it effectively. Electronic properties are explained by band theory, based on the Schrödinger equation and the Pauli principle. Silicon chip technology (CMOS) dominates thanks to the high-quality native oxide SiO₂, used as a gate insulator in MOSFETs. Doping introduces impurity levels into the bandgap, controlling the concentration of charge carriers.
raw.history: Jöns Jacob Berzelius isolated amorphous silicon in 1824, and Henri Sainte-Claire Deville obtained crystalline silicon in 1854. The first point-contact transistor (1947) worked on germanium, but silicon quickly replaced it. Leo Esaki (listed) investigated quantum tunneling in solids and invented the tunnel diode (on other semiconductors), making important contributions to the understanding of junctions. In 1954, Morris Tanenbaum created the first silicon transistor at Bell Labs. Since then, miniaturization has progressed exponentially, approaching physical limits.
raw.how_it_works: When n- and p-regions come into contact, a depletion region arises, creating a potential barrier. Under forward bias, the current increases exponentially; under reverse bias, it is blocked. This rectifying property is described by the diode equation. In metal-oxide-semiconductor field-effect transistors (MOSFETs), the voltage on the gate controls the conductivity of the channel. Operation is based on electrostatics, the Poisson equation, and carrier statistics (Fermi–Dirac distribution). In solar cells, the p–n junction separates photogenerated electron-hole pairs, generating an electric current.
key_problems: Physical limits of miniaturization of silicon transistors (quantum tunneling at nanometer scales)
Development of silicon photonics for high-speed data transfer within chips
Increasing the efficiency of silicon solar cells beyond the Shockley-Queisser limit
raw.fun_fact: Pure silicon shines like a metal, but in powder form it looks dark grey. Silicon wafers for chips are polished to atomic smoothness—they are flatter than any mirror.

lagrangian.name: lagrangian
lagrangian.mini: The Lagrangian is a 'recipe' for motion: the difference between the energy of speed and the energy of position sets the system on the path of least resistance.
lagrangian.practical_application: Used for calculating the orbits of planets, trajectories of rockets, optimizing mechanical devices, and in the foundations of quantum theory.
lagrangian.description_popular: The Lagrangian is a function depending on the coordinates and velocities of all parts of a system. Instead of considering forces, as in Newtonian mechanics, one operates with energies: kinetic (energy of motion) and potential (energy of position). From the principle of least action it follows that the actual motion of a system occurs such that the total difference of these energies along the trajectory is minimal. This approach is particularly convenient for systems with constraints, e.g., a pendulum on an inextensible string, and allows one to derive the equations of motion (Euler-Lagrange equations).
lagrangian.fun_fact_popular: Lagrange's method made it possible to solve problems that were too complex for the direct application of Newton's laws, such as the motion of many-body systems with constraints.
lagrangian.description_simple: The Lagrangian is a quantity equal to the difference between the kinetic energy (energy of motion) and the potential energy (energy of position) of a system. Knowing the Lagrangian, one can predict how the system will change over time: nature always chooses the trajectory for which the total difference of these energies over the entire path is the smallest.
lagrangian.history_simple: Developed by Joseph-Louis Lagrange in 1788 as a universal method for describing motion, alternative to Newton's laws.
lagrangian.how_it_works_simple: Just as water flows downhill, choosing the path of least resistance, so any mechanical system evolves by minimizing the accumulated difference of kinetic and potential energies, given by the Lagrangian.
lagrangian.description: The Lagrangian L(q, q̇, t) is a fundamental function in analytical mechanics, defined as the difference between the kinetic energy T and the potential energy V: L = T − V. Hamilton's principle (the principle of stationary action) states that the true trajectory of a system between two moments in time makes the action integral S = ∫ L dt stationary. Variation of S leads to the Euler-Lagrange equations: d/dt (∂L/∂q̇_i) − ∂L/∂q_i = 0, which are equivalent to Newton's second law but applicable in arbitrary coordinates. The Lagrangian formalism extends to field theory (via Lagrangian density), quantum mechanics (Feynman path integrals), and elementary particle physics. Symmetries of the Lagrangian lead to conservation laws via Noether's theorem.
lagrangian.history: First presented by Joseph-Louis Lagrange in 'Analytical Mechanics' (1788) with the goal of constructing mechanics without geometric constructions. In the 19th century, William Rowan Hamilton reformulated it into Hamiltonian formalism. Henri Poincaré developed the theory of dynamical systems. Emmy Noether in 1918 proved the theorem linking continuous symmetries of the Lagrangian to conserved quantities. In quantum field theory, the Lagrangian became the basis of the Standard Model.
lagrangian.how_it_works: For a system with generalized coordinates q_i, the Lagrangian is written as L(q, q̇, t) = T − V. The Euler-Lagrange equations are obtained by requiring the variation of the action to be zero. For example, for a particle in a potential field, L = ½ m ẋ² − V(x). Noether's theorem states: to each continuous symmetry of the Lagrangian there corresponds a conserved integral of motion (energy, momentum, etc.).
key_problems: Constructing Lagrangians for systems with nonholonomic constraints
Justification of the principle of least action in non-Hamiltonian systems
Quantization of Lagrangians with gauge symmetries
lagrangian.fun_fact: The Lagrangian of the Standard Model of elementary particles, when written in a compact form, fits on one page, although it describes all known interactions except gravity.

formulas.description: Lagrangian function for the simplest mechanical system
formulas.meaning: L is the Lagrangian, T is kinetic energy, V is potential energy

formulas.description: Euler-Lagrange equations
formulas.meaning: L is the Lagrangian, q_i is a generalized coordinate, \dot{q}_i is a generalized velocity, t is time

raw.mini: The Lagrangian is like the difference between excitement and fatigue in a game, by which nature chooses the most economical path.
raw.practical_application: Used for predicting the motion of planets, calculating spacecraft trajectories, and optimizing mechanisms.
raw.description_popular: The Lagrangian is a function of coordinates and velocities, determining the dynamics of a system through the principle of least action. Unlike the Newtonian approach with forces, the Lagrangian formalism operates with energies and generalized coordinates, which simplifies the description of systems with constraints (e.g., a pendulum on a rod). The Euler-Lagrange equations are obtained by varying the action and yield the equations of motion.
raw.fun_fact_popular: Lagrange's method allowed solving problems inaccessible to Newton, such as the motion of many-body systems with complex constraints.
raw.description_simple: The Lagrangian is a special function, equal to the difference between kinetic energy (energy of motion) and potential energy (energy of position) of a system. From the form of the Lagrangian one can learn how the system will evolve over time, using the principle of least action: actual motion minimizes the accumulated action.
raw.history_simple: Developed by Joseph-Louis Lagrange in 1788 as an alternative to Newton's laws.
raw.how_it_works_simple: Just as water flows in a riverbed, always choosing the path of least resistance, a physical system moves so that the integral of the Lagrangian (action) is minimal.
raw.description: The Lagrangian L(q, q̇, t) is a fundamental function in analytical mechanics, defined as the difference between the kinetic energy T and the potential energy V: L = T − V. Hamilton's principle (the principle of stationary action) states that the true trajectory of a system between two moments in time makes the action integral S = ∫ L dt stationary. Variation of S leads to the Euler-Lagrange equations: d/dt (∂L/∂q̇_i) − ∂L/∂q_i = 0, which are equivalent to Newton's second law but applicable in arbitrary coordinates. The Lagrangian formalism extends to field theory (via Lagrangian density), quantum mechanics (Feynman path integrals), and elementary particle physics. Symmetries of the Lagrangian lead to conservation laws via Noether's theorem.
raw.history: First presented by Joseph-Louis Lagrange in 'Analytical Mechanics' (1788) with the goal of constructing mechanics without geometric constructions. In the 19th century, William Rowan Hamilton reformulated it into Hamiltonian formalism. Henri Poincaré developed the theory of dynamical systems. Emmy Noether in 1918 proved the theorem linking continuous symmetries of the Lagrangian to conserved quantities. In quantum field theory, the Lagrangian became the basis of the Standard Model.
raw.how_it_works: Dynamics follows from a variational principle. For a system of particles, the Lagrangian is constructed as the difference of energies, then the equations of motion are found. For example, for a point mass in a potential field L = ½ m ẋ² - V(x). In generalized coordinates q_i, this gives equations that take geometry into account. Noether's theorem: each continuous symmetry of the Lagrangian corresponds to a conserved integral of motion.
key_problems: Constructing Lagrangians for systems with nonholonomic constraints
Justification of the principle of least action beyond classical physics
