large_hadron_collider.name: Large Hadron Collider
large_hadron_collider.mini: A giant ring that accelerates protons to near-light speeds and smashes them head-on to peer deep into matter.
large_hadron_collider.practical_application: The LHC discovered the Higgs boson — the last missing piece of the Standard Model. The superconducting magnets and vacuum technologies developed for it are now used in proton cancer therapy and for sterilizing medical equipment.
large_hadron_collider.description_popular: The Large Hadron Collider is a 27-kilometer ring, buried underground at depths of up to 175 meters. In it, two counter-rotating beams of protons are accelerated to an energy of 6.8 TeV each (speed — 0.99999999 of the speed of light) and collide head-on with a total energy of 13.6 TeV. The beams are kept in orbit by 1232 superconducting magnets, cooled by liquid helium to 1.9 K (colder than outer space). At the collision points, gigantic detectors — ATLAS, CMS, LHCb, and ALICE — record the hundreds of particles born in each collision.
large_hadron_collider.fun_fact_popular: In one second, protons in the LHC circle the ring 11,245 times, and the total energy of each beam is comparable to that of a high-speed train.
large_hadron_collider.description_simple: The LHC is the most powerful particle accelerator in the world. It is built in an underground tunnel on the border between Switzerland and France. Two beams of protons (or lead nuclei) are accelerated to near-light speeds and collide head-on in four locations. There, detectors the size of a multi-story building record what is born in these micro-explosions.
large_hadron_collider.history_simple: Construction began in 1998 at CERN. The first launch was on September 10, 2008, but a few days later a serious accident disabled a sector, and repairs took more than a year. Full high-energy collisions began in 2010, and in 2012 the ATLAS and CMS collaborations announced the discovery of the Higgs boson — a particle predicted 48 years earlier.
large_hadron_collider.how_it_works_simple: Protons, like two trains rushing along opposite tracks, are accelerated by electric fields and held by magnets. When they collide head-on, new particles are born, like fragments from a crash. Detectors work like giant microscopes, examining these fragments.
large_hadron_collider.description: The Large Hadron Collider (LHC) is a proton-proton and nucleus-nucleus collider, designed for research at center-of-mass energies up to 14 TeV (protons) and 2.76 TeV per nucleon (Pb-Pb). Acceleration occurs in several stages: Linac4 linear accelerator → PSB booster synchrotron → PS proton synchrotron → SPS super proton synchrotron, then injection into the main LHC ring. To keep the beams on track, dipole magnets based on NbTi superconductor are used, operating at superfluid helium temperature. Focusing is provided by quadrupole and higher-order magnets. The luminosity (collision density) reaches 2×10³⁴ cm⁻²s⁻¹, enabling gigahertz event rates. Major discoveries: Higgs boson (2012), pentaquarks, study of quark-gluon plasma.
large_hadron_collider.history: The LHC project emerged in the early 1980s as a successor to the Large Electron-Positron Collider (LEP). After approval in 1994, construction proceeded from 1998 to 2008. On September 10, 2008, a beam was successfully circulated, but 9 days later an electrical fault caused a helium explosion and took a sector out of commission. A restart after repairs and upgrades occurred in November 2009, and the first 7 TeV collisions took place in March 2010. Thousands of CERN engineers and scientists played a key role in creating the accelerator, and the ATLAS and CMS collaborations were instrumental in discovering the Higgs boson.
large_hadron_collider.how_it_works: Protons are accelerated by high-frequency resonators (400 MHz), and the trajectory is maintained by superconducting magnets. When counter-rotating bunches collide at the intersection points, thousands of particles are born, which are registered by multi-layered detectors: tracking systems (silicon pixels), calorimeters (electromagnetic made of PbWO₄ crystals, hadronic — iron/scintillator), muon chambers. Magnetic fields in the detectors (solenoids or toroidal magnets) enable momentum measurement. A huge challenge is extracting rare events from the background at high luminosity — this is accomplished using online trigger systems and offline analysis on computer farms worldwide (GRID).
key_problems: The hierarchy problem: why is the mass of the Higgs boson many orders of magnitude smaller than the Planck mass, although quantum corrections should make it huge?
Dark matter: despite extensive searches, the LHC has not found candidates (WIMP particles, superpartners), challenging theories.
Incompleteness of the Standard Model: it does not include gravity, does not explain dark energy, and does not account for the observed predominance of matter over antimatter.
large_hadron_collider.fun_fact: The total energy of the beam in the LHC can melt a ton of copper. To direct a focused beam onto a graphite block, special ultra-strong absorbers — collimators — are required.

formulas.description: Center-of-mass collision energy for counter-rotating beams of equal energy.
formulas.meaning: \sqrt{s} is the total energy in the center-of-mass system (effective collision energy), E_beam is the energy of one proton in the beam. At E_beam = 6.8 TeV, \sqrt{s} = 13.6 TeV.

raw.mini: A 27-kilometer ring that accelerates particles almost to the speed of light to collide them and understand what matter is made of.
raw.practical_application: The discovery of the Higgs boson at the LHC completed the Standard Model; technologies of superconducting magnets and vacuum are used in proton cancer therapy and industrial sterilization.
raw.description_popular: The LHC is a ring collider with a circumference of 26.7 km, located at depths of up to 175 meters. Protons are accelerated to an energy of 6.8 TeV per beam (reaching a speed of 0.99999999c) and collide with a total energy of 13.6 TeV. The magnetic system of 1232 superconducting dipole magnets is cooled by liquid helium to 1.9 K, creating a field of 8.3 T. The ATLAS, CMS, LHCb, ALICE detectors register collision products — hundreds of particles per event.
raw.fun_fact_popular: Inside the collider, protons make 11,245 revolutions per second, and the full beam carries as much kinetic energy as a train speeding at 150 km/h.
raw.description_simple: The Large Hadron Collider (LHC) is the most powerful particle accelerator in the world. It is located in a tunnel underground on the border of Switzerland and France. Inside it, two beams of protons (or lead nuclei) race around a ring towards each other with enormous energy and collide at four points, where huge detectors study the collision debris.
raw.history_simple: Construction of the LHC began in 1998 at CERN (European Organization for Nuclear Research), with launch in 2008. However, a few days after startup, an accident knocked the accelerator out of commission for a year. After repairs in 2010, full collisions began, and in 2012 the discovery of the Higgs boson was announced — a particle predicted back in 1964.
raw.how_it_works_simple: Two toy trains on head-on tracks are accelerated faster and faster by electric fields, while powerful magnets keep them on a circular trajectory. In the LHC, protons collide head-on, and at the moment of impact, new particles are born — like fragments from a shattered toy. Detectors, like giant microscopes, study these fragments.
raw.description: The Large Hadron Collider (LHC) is a proton-proton and nucleus-nucleus collider, designed for research at center-of-mass energies up to 14 TeV (protons) and 2.76 TeV per nucleon (Pb-Pb). Acceleration occurs in several stages: Linac4 linear accelerator → PSB booster synchrotron → PS proton synchrotron → SPS super proton synchrotron, then injection into the main LHC ring. To keep the beams on track, dipole magnets based on NbTi superconductor are used, operating at superfluid helium temperature. Focusing is provided by quadrupole and higher-order magnets. The luminosity (collision density) reaches 2×10^34 cm⁻²s⁻¹, enabling gigahertz event rates. Major discoveries: Higgs boson (2012), pentaquarks, study of quark-gluon plasma.
raw.history: The LHC project emerged in the early 1980s as a successor to the Large Electron-Positron Collider (LEP). After approval in 1994, construction proceeded from 1998 to 2008. On September 10, 2008, a beam was successfully circulated, but 9 days later an electrical fault caused a helium explosion and took a sector out of commission. A restart after repairs and upgrades occurred in November 2009, and the first 7 TeV collisions took place in March 2010. Thousands of CERN engineers and scientists played a key role in creating the accelerator, and the ATLAS and CMS collaborations were instrumental in discovering the Higgs boson.
raw.how_it_works: Protons are accelerated by high-frequency resonators (400 MHz), and the trajectory is maintained by superconducting magnets. When counter-rotating bunches collide at the intersection points, thousands of particles are born, which are registered by multi-layered detectors: tracking systems (silicon pixels), calorimeters (electromagnetic made of PbWO4 crystals, hadronic — iron/scintillator), muon chambers. Magnetic fields in the detectors (solenoids or toroidal magnets) enable momentum measurement. A huge challenge is extracting rare events from the background at high luminosity — this is accomplished using online trigger systems and offline analysis on computer farms worldwide (GRID).
key_problems: Mass hierarchy: why is the Higgs boson so light compared to the Planck scale?
Dark matter: candidate particles (WIMPs, axions) have not been found, although the LHC actively searched for supersymmetry.
The full picture: the Standard Model is complete, but it does not explain gravity, dark energy, baryon asymmetry.
raw.fun_fact: The total energy of the beam in the LHC can melt a ton of copper; to direct a focused beam onto a graphite block, special ultra-strong absorbers — collimators — are required.

wave_particle_duality.name: wave-particle duality
wave_particle_duality.mini: Light and matter behave as waves when we don't track their path, and as particles when we do — like a chameleon that changes its appearance depending on the environment.
wave_particle_duality.practical_application: The wave nature of electrons allows electron microscopes to distinguish individual atoms through diffraction. The particle nature of photons underlies the photoelectric effect used in solar cells and light-sensitive detectors.
wave_particle_duality.description_popular: Wave-particle duality means that quantum objects are neither exclusively particles nor exclusively waves — they exhibit properties of both depending on how they are observed. The classic double-slit experiment demonstrates this: single photons or electrons, accumulating on a detector, form an interference pattern typical of waves. This is explained by the superposition of alternative paths: the particle seems to pass through both slits simultaneously. However, any attempt to determine which slit it went through destroys the superposition, and the pattern becomes particle-like. Mathematically, duality is described by the wave function, and the Heisenberg uncertainty principle (the more precisely the position is measured, the less defined the momentum) quantitatively expresses the connection between wave and particle manifestations.
wave_particle_duality.fun_fact_popular: The de Broglie wavelength of a baseball flying at 40 m/s is approximately 10⁻³⁴ m — negligibly small compared to its size. That's why in everyday life we don't notice that a baseball is also a wave.
wave_particle_duality.description_simple: If you throw a handful of sand through two slits, two separate piles form behind them — that's particle behavior. If you send water, a pattern of alternating peaks and troughs emerges — a wave property. When single photons or electrons are sent through the same slits, a wave pattern gradually appears on the screen, even though each particle leaves a dot. This shows that micro-objects behave as waves (passing through both slits at once) and as particles (being detected at a specific point). This duality is called wave-particle duality. It was proposed by French physicist Louis de Broglie in 1924, who suggested that every moving particle has an associated wavelength.
wave_particle_duality.history_simple: The debate about the nature of light dates back to the 17th century: Newton thought light was a stream of particles, Huygens — a wave. By the 19th century, the wave theory prevailed. However, in 1905, Einstein, explaining the photoelectric effect, introduced the concept of photons — particles of light. In 1924, de Broglie hypothesized that if light (a wave) shows particle properties, then electrons (particles) should also display wave properties. Already in 1927, experiments by Davisson and Germer on electron scattering from crystals confirmed their diffraction. Since then, duality has been recognized as a universal property of matter.
wave_particle_duality.how_it_works_simple: A moving electron can be compared to a stone thrown into a pond: the stone is a particle, and the spreading ripples are a wave. In the quantum world, these two aspects are inseparable: the more precisely the electron's position is determined (particle property), the less defined its momentum (wave property), and vice versa. The double-slit experiment illustrates this: without a detector, the electron goes through both slits simultaneously, creating an interference pattern; but if a detector is placed, the electron chooses one slit and interference disappears.
wave_particle_duality.description: Wave-particle duality is a fundamental principle of quantum physics, stating that all micro-objects (photons, electrons, atoms, and even large molecules) possess both wave properties (interference, diffraction) and particle properties (discrete interactions with transfer of energy and momentum). In quantum mechanics, duality is explained by the probabilistic interpretation of the wave function: its squared modulus gives the probability density of finding the particle at a given point, and interference arises from the addition of complex amplitudes. The contradiction is resolved in quantum field theory, where particles are quantized excitations of corresponding fields; depending on the energy and experimental conditions, wave or particle features manifest. Bohr's complementarity principle emphasizes that wave and particle descriptions are complementary: one cannot simultaneously measure parameters belonging to both pictures with arbitrary precision.
wave_particle_duality.history: Historically, duality goes back to the dispute over the nature of light: Newton advocated the corpuscular theory, Huygens — the wave theory. Diffraction experiments by Young and Fresnel in the early 19th century strengthened the wave theory, and Maxwell's equations (1864) described light as an electromagnetic wave. A turning point came in 1905 when Einstein explained the photoelectric effect by introducing the concept of photons. In 1923, Compton observed the scattering of X-rays off electrons, proving that the photon behaves like a particle with momentum. In 1924, de Broglie generalized duality, associating a wave of length λ = h/p with every particle. The hypothesis was confirmed in 1927 by electron diffraction by Davisson and Germer. Experiments with fullerene C₆₀ interference (1999) demonstrated duality at mesoscopic scales.
wave_particle_duality.how_it_works: In the framework of quantum mechanics, the state of a particle is described by a wave function ψ(x,t), which obeys the Schrödinger equation or, for relativistic particles, the Dirac equation. In the double-slit experiment, the wave function passes through both slits, forming a superposition of states 'particle went through slit 1' and 'particle went through slit 2'. Interference between these states yields a probability distribution on the screen with maxima and minima. Measurement of the particle's position collapses the wave function to a single point, so individual particles are registered as dots, and the interference pattern builds up statistically. The Heisenberg uncertainty principle Δx·Δp ≥ ħ/2 links the precision of position and momentum measurement, consistently expressing the complementarity of wave and particle manifestations.
key_problems: The interpretation problem of quantum mechanics: does a real trajectory of the particle exist before measurement?
The quantum-classical transition: at what size or mass does an object stop exhibiting interference?
Experimental demonstration of duality for increasingly massive particles (the decoherence boundary).
wave_particle_duality.fun_fact: In 1999, an interference pattern was obtained for fullerene C₆₀ molecules — spherical structures of 60 carbon atoms. This is the most massive object for which wave duality has been reliably recorded.

formulas.description: De Broglie wavelength formula
formulas.meaning: λ is the de Broglie wavelength, h is Planck's constant, h ≈ 6.626×10⁻³⁴ J·s; p is the particle's momentum, p = mv (for a non-relativistic particle).

raw.mini: Light and matter behave as waves when we don't look, and as particles when we do — like a shadow that becomes three-dimensional when a lamp is turned on.
raw.practical_application: The wave nature of electrons allows electron microscopes to show individual atoms; the particle-like nature of photons is used in photocells and solar batteries.
raw.description_popular: Wave-particle duality means that quantum objects do not fit into the classical categories of 'particle' or 'wave', but possess properties of both depending on the experimental setup. In the classic double-slit experiment, single photons or electrons, accumulating on a detector, form an interference pattern characteristic of continuous waves. This is interpreted as a superposition of alternative trajectories: the particle does not pass through one slit, but simultaneously 'samples' all possible paths. However, any attempt to track the particle destroys this superposition and restores the particle picture. Mathematically, duality is described by the de Broglie wave function, and the Heisenberg uncertainty principle quantitatively links the 'clarity' of wave and particle behavior.
raw.fun_fact_popular: The de Broglie wavelength of a baseball flying at 40 m/s is about 10⁻³⁴ m — it is negligibly small. Therefore, in the macrocosm we don't notice that a baseball is also a wave.
raw.description_simple: Throw a handful of sand through two slits — you get two piles behind; send water — you see a complex pattern of peaks and troughs. The first experiment is particle behavior, the second is wave. Now send single particles of light (photons) or electrons through the same slits: on the screen a similar wave pattern slowly appears, even though each particle hits a single point. So, each object seems to go through both slits at once — like a wave — but manifests in a specific place — like a particle. This is called wave-particle duality. The idea was put forward in 1924 by French physicist Louis de Broglie, who suggested that any moving body has a wavelength.
raw.history_simple: Back in the 17th century, scientists debated whether light is a stream of particles (Newton) or waves (Huygens). By the 19th century, the wave theory had won. But in 1905, Einstein showed that light knocks electrons out of metal like a billiard ball — and so the photon appeared. In 1924, de Broglie said: if light (a wave) behaves like a particle, then an electron (a particle) might also be a wave. Just three years later, experiments with electron scattering from crystals confirmed they produce a diffraction pattern. Since then, duality has been considered a universal property of all matter.
raw.how_it_works_simple: A flying electron resembles a stone thrown into a pond: the stone itself is a particle, and the spreading ripples are a wave. But in the quantum world, the stone and the wave are inseparable: the more precisely we measure where the electron is (as a particle), the less definite its wave 'ripple' becomes, and vice versa. The double-slit experiment demonstrates this: as long as we don't know the path, the electron goes through both slits as a wave, creating interference; as soon as we put a detector on the slits, the particle chooses one, and the interference pattern disappears.
raw.description: Wave-particle duality is a fundamental principle of quantum physics, stating that all micro-objects (photons, electrons, atoms, and even large molecules) possess both wave properties (interference, diffraction) and particle properties (discrete interactions with transfer of energy and momentum). In quantum mechanics, it finds a natural explanation in the probabilistic interpretation of the wave function: its squared modulus gives the probability density of finding the particle at a specific point, and interference effects arise from the addition of complex amplitudes. Duality is not a contradiction but is resolved within quantum field theory, where particles are viewed as quantized excitations of fields; depending on the energy and scale of the experiment, different aspects manifest. Niels Bohr's complementarity principle emphasizes that the wave and particle descriptions are complementary: one cannot simultaneously measure properties belonging to both pictures with arbitrary precision.
raw.history: Historically, duality goes back to the dispute over the nature of light: Isaac Newton advocated the corpuscular theory, Christiaan Huygens the wave theory. Diffraction experiments by Thomas Young and Augustin Fresnel in the early 19th century tipped the scales in favor of waves, and James Clerk Maxwell's equations (1864) described light as an electromagnetic wave. The turning point came in 1905 when Albert Einstein explained the photoelectric effect using light quanta (photons). In 1923, Arthur Compton observed the scattering of X-rays off electrons, confirming that the photon behaves as a particle with momentum. Louis de Broglie in 1924 generalized duality to all matter, hypothesizing that a wave with length λ = h/p is associated with every particle. This hypothesis was experimentally confirmed by the electron diffraction of Davisson and Germer in 1927. Subsequent experiments, including the interference of fullerenes C₆₀ in 1999, demonstrated duality up to mesoscopic scales.
raw.how_it_works: In the framework of quantum mechanics, the state of a particle is described by a wave function ψ(x,t), which obeys the Schrödinger equation or, for relativistic particles, the Dirac equation. In the double-slit experiment, the wave function passes through both slits, forming a superposition of states 'particle went through slit 1' and 'particle went through slit 2'. Interference between these states yields a probability distribution on the screen with maxima and minima. Measurement of the particle's position collapses the wave function to a single point, so individual particles are registered as dots, and the interference pattern builds up statistically. The Heisenberg uncertainty principle Δx·Δp ≥ ħ/2 links the precision of position and momentum measurement, consistently expressing the complementarity of wave and particle manifestations.
key_problems: The interpretation problem of quantum mechanics: does a real trajectory of the particle exist before measurement?
The quantum-classical transition: at what size or mass does an object stop exhibiting interference?
Experimental demonstration of duality for increasingly massive particles (the decoherence boundary).
raw.fun_fact: In 1999, an interference pattern was obtained for fullerene C₆₀ molecules — spherical structures of 60 carbon atoms. This is the most massive object for which wave duality has been reliably recorded.

helium.name: helium
helium.mini: Helium — the second lightest element: an invisible gas from stellar furnaces that makes voices squeaky and cools the most powerful magnets.
helium.practical_application: Used in breathing mixtures for deep-sea diving, in helium-neon lasers, and for cooling superconducting magnets in medical tomographs.
helium.description_popular: Helium is the second element of the periodic table, a colorless and odorless noble gas. Its atom contains two protons (the He-4 isotope has two neutrons, He-3 has one). The filled electron shell makes helium chemically inert. Under normal conditions, it is a monatomic gas (atoms do not form molecules) with very low density. When liquid helium-4 is cooled to 2.17 K, it transitions to a superfluid state: it flows without friction and has enormous thermal conductivity.
helium.fun_fact_popular: Helium is the only simple substance that remains liquid at absolute zero temperature (at normal pressure); quantum fluctuations prevent it from freezing.
helium.description_simple: Helium is a chemical element, the second most abundant in the Universe after hydrogen. It is an inert gas that practically does not react chemically. Helium has low density and is non-flammable, so it is used to fill balloons and airships. When inhaled, it temporarily changes the timbre of the voice because the speed of sound in helium is higher. In the liquid state at ultralow temperatures, helium becomes superfluid: it flows without friction and can 'escape' from an open container through microscopic gaps.
helium.history_simple: Helium was first noticed not on Earth but on the Sun: in 1868, astronomers discovered a bright yellow line in the solar spectrum that did not belong to any known element. It was attributed to a new element, named helium after Helios, the god of the Sun. On Earth, helium was isolated only in 1895 from uranium ore.
helium.how_it_works_simple: Helium is extracted from natural gas, where it accumulates over millions of years as a product of radioactive decay of heavy elements. The unusual properties of helium at low temperatures are explained by quantum effects: its atoms are so light that when cooled they behave like waves, creating superfluidity — frictionless flow.
helium.description: Helium is the second element of the periodic system, an inert noble gas. Its nucleus (α-particle) is exceptionally stable, making helium the end product of thermonuclear reactions in stars. The He-4 isotope is a boson and below the lambda point (2.17 K) transitions to a superfluid state, described by Landau's two-fluid model. Quantized vortices appear and the fountain effect is possible. He-3, being a fermion, becomes superfluid only at millikelvin temperatures due to the formation of Cooper pairs, similar to superconductivity.
helium.history: In 1868, Pierre Janssen and Norman Lockyer independently discovered a bright yellow line in the solar spectrum; Lockyer suggested it belonged to a new element — helium. In 1895, William Ramsay isolated helium from the mineral cleveite, and Per Teodor Cleve and Nils Langlet determined its atomic mass. In 1908, Heike Kamerlingh Onnes first obtained liquid helium; superfluidity in He-4 was discovered in 1937 by Pyotr Kapitsa, with theoretical explanation provided by Landau and others.
helium.how_it_works: Superfluidity of helium-4 is explained by Bose-Einstein condensation: a macroscopic fraction of atoms transitions to the ground state with zero momentum. Quantum coherence of the condensate over large distances leads to dissipationless flow. For He-3, superfluidity arises from the formation of pairs with antiparallel spins (analogous to Cooper pairs), described by the Ginzburg–Landau equation. In astrophysics, helium is synthesized in stellar interiors (proton-proton and CNO cycles) and during primordial nucleosynthesis after the Big Bang.
key_problems: Precise description of turbulence in superfluid helium and its connection to classical turbulence.
Understanding the phase transition between superfluid and normal states in strong fields and under rotation.
Studying gravitational effects in superfluidity, for example, the influence on quantum vortices.
Helium shortage: depletion of earthly reserves, especially the rare He-3 isotope, critical for scientific detectors.
helium.fun_fact: In superfluid helium-4, quantum vortices arise with circulation quantized in units of h/m, vividly demonstrating the principle of superposition and the uniqueness of the wave function.

formulas.description: Quantization of circulation in superfluid helium
formulas.meaning: ∮ v_s·dl is the circulation of the superfluid velocity around a closed contour; h = 6.62607015×10⁻³⁴ J·s is Planck's constant; m_He is the mass of a helium-4 atom (6.646×10⁻²⁷ kg); n is an integer (quantum number of the vortex). Consequence of the uniqueness of the wave function of the Bose condensate.

raw.mini: Helium — the second lightest element: an invisible gas from stellar furnaces that makes voices squeaky and cools the most powerful magnets.
raw.practical_application: Used in breathing mixtures for deep-sea diving, in helium-neon lasers, and for cooling superconducting magnets in MRI.
raw.description_popular: Helium (He) is a noble gas with atomic number 2, without color, smell, or taste. Its atoms consist of two protons and two neutrons (isotope He-4) or two protons and one neutron (He-3). Thanks to its filled electron shell, helium is chemically inert. At room temperature, it is a monatomic (single-atom) gas with very low density. Upon cooling, He-4 at 2.17 K transitions to a superfluid state (He-II) with anomalously high thermal conductivity and no viscosity.
raw.fun_fact_popular: Helium is the only simple substance that remains liquid at absolute zero temperature (at normal pressure; quantum fluctuations prevent it from freezing).
raw.description_simple: Helium is a chemical element, the second most abundant in the Universe after hydrogen. It is an inert gas, meaning it hardly reacts with anything. Helium is lighter than air and non-flammable, so it is used to fill balloons and airships. When inhaled, helium temporarily alters the voice because sound travels faster in it. In the liquid state at ultralow temperatures, helium becomes superfluid — it flows without friction and can 'escape' from a container through the walls.
raw.history_simple: Helium was first noticed not on Earth but on the Sun: in 1868, astronomers saw a bright yellow line in the solar spectrum that did not belong to any known element. It was attributed to a new element — helium, named after Helios, the sun god. On Earth, helium was found only in 1895 in uranium ores.
raw.how_it_works_simple: Helium is often obtained from natural gas, where it accumulates over millions of years as a product of radioactive decay of heavy elements. Its unusual properties when cooled are explained by quantum effects: helium atoms are so light that at low temperatures they behave like waves rather than particles, creating superfluidity.
raw.description: Helium is the second element of the periodic system, an inert noble gas. Its nucleus (α-particle) is exceptionally stable, making helium the end product of a chain of thermonuclear reactions in stars. The He-4 isotope is a boson (a particle with integer spin) and below the lambda point (2.17 K) undergoes a phase transition to a superfluid state, described by the two-fluid model. Quantized vortices appear and the fountain effect is possible. He-3, being a fermion, exhibits superfluidity only at millikelvin temperatures due to the formation of Cooper pairs, analogous to superconductivity.
raw.history: The yellow line in the solar spectrum during the 1868 eclipse was independently discovered by Pierre Janssen and Norman Lockyer; Lockyer concluded it belonged to an unknown element, named helium. In 1895, William Ramsay isolated helium from the uranium ore cleveite, and Per Teodor Cleve and Nils Abraham Langlet determined its atomic mass. In 1908, Heike Kamerlingh Onnes first obtained liquid helium, and in 1937, Pyotr Kapitsa discovered superfluidity in He-4. Theoretical explanation was given by Landau and others.
raw.how_it_works: Superfluidity of helium-4 is explained by Bose-Einstein condensation of a macroscopic fraction of atoms into the ground state with zero momentum. According to quantum mechanics, the wave function of such a condensate phase is coherent on macroscopic scales, leading to dissipationless flow. For He-3, superfluidity arises through the formation of pairs with antiparallel spins (like Cooper pairs), whose motion is described by the Ginzburg–Landau equation. In astrophysics, helium is formed in stellar interiors through the proton-proton cycle and the CNO cycle (nuclear fusion), as well as during primordial nucleosynthesis after the Big Bang.
key_problems: Precise description of turbulence in superfluid helium and its connection to classical turbulence.
Understanding the superfluid–normal liquid phase transition in strong fields and under rotation.
Search for gravitational effects in superfluidity (e.g., influence on quantum vortices).
Helium shortage: rapid depletion of helium reserves on Earth, especially the He-3 isotope critical for some detectors.
raw.fun_fact: Liquid helium-4 in the superfluid state can form 'quantum vortices' with circulation quantized in units of h/m, which is a direct manifestation of the superposition principle and the uniqueness of the wave function.

cosmic_dust.name: cosmic dust
cosmic_dust.mini: Cosmic dust is the smoky veil of the Universe, hiding the light of distant stars while simultaneously serving as raw material for new worlds.
cosmic_dust.practical_application: Cosmic dust forms planets and even living organisms; moreover, dust clouds scatter radio waves, creating interference for satellite communications and navigation.
cosmic_dust.description_popular: Cosmic dust consists of solid microparticles of carbon, silicates, and ices, born in the atmospheres of cool giant stars or during supernova explosions. It absorbs ultraviolet and visible light, re-emitting energy in the infrared range. Dense dust clouds can completely hide objects in the optical spectrum, so astronomers use infrared and radio telescopes to study them.
cosmic_dust.fun_fact_popular: In one cubic kilometer of interstellar space, there are on average only a few hundred dust grains, but the total mass of interstellar dust in the galaxy is comparable to the mass of millions of stars.
cosmic_dust.description_simple: Cosmic dust is tiny solid grains the size of smoke or flour particles, floating in interstellar space. Like dust in a flashlight beam, each grain is invisible, but accumulations of these particles can block starlight. They consist of carbon, silicates (compounds of silicon and oxygen), and frozen gases; they form in the shells of dying stars or during supernova explosions.
cosmic_dust.history_simple: The existence of cosmic dust was hypothesized as early as the 19th century, but serious study began with the development of astrophotography in the 20th century, when dark spots blocking stars were noticed on photographs.
cosmic_dust.how_it_works_simple: Just as dust in the atmosphere gives sunsets red hues, cosmic dust makes the light from distant stars redder. Small particles scatter blue light more strongly than red, so the red part of the spectrum predominantly reaches the observer — this effect is called interstellar reddening.
cosmic_dust.description: Cosmic dust consists of solid microscopic particles of irregular shape, ranging in size from a few nanometers to fractions of a millimeter. The chemical composition includes silicates (amorphous and crystalline), carbon compounds (graphite, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons), ices (water, methane, ammonia), and refractory metals. Dust forms in stellar atmospheres (especially the asymptotic giant branch) and is ejected into the interstellar medium via stellar winds, or condenses in expanding shells of supernovae and novae. In dense molecular clouds, dust catalyzes molecule formation and plays a key role in gas cooling, promoting star formation. Optical properties are determined by Mie scattering theory for particles comparable to the wavelength of light, leading to interstellar absorption and reddening. Polarization of light passing through dust clouds aligned by magnetic fields allows the study of the galactic magnetic field structure.
cosmic_dust.history: The existence of dark regions in the Milky Way has been known since antiquity, but their nature remained a mystery for a long time. In the 18th century, William Herschel suggested they were 'holes in the heavens'. Only in the early 20th century did Edward Barnard and other astronomers prove that they are clouds of light-absorbing matter. A decisive contribution was made by Robert Trumpler in 1930, who showed that distant star clusters appear redder and dimmer due to interstellar absorption, revealing the existence of scattering dust particles. Later, spectroscopic and infrared observations allowed detailed study of the composition and distribution of dust.
cosmic_dust.how_it_works: Cosmic dust interacts with electromagnetic radiation through absorption (photons turn into thermal energy) and scattering (photons change direction). Mie theory is used to describe scattering on spherical particles, and the Rayleigh approximation for very small particles. Dust also emits in the infrared range according to Planck's radiation law, modified for emissivity depending on composition. Chemical reactions on the surface of dust grains, including the formation of molecular hydrogen, are catalyzed by low temperatures and a large surface area. Elongated particles, aligned by the galactic magnetic field, cause partial linear polarization of transmitted light (polarimetry).
key_problems: The exact chemical composition and nuclear structures of dust grains, especially the carbon component, remain a subject of debate.
The role of dust in the formation of complex organic molecules, including prebiotic ones, is not fully understood.
Dust introduces uncertainty into measurements of cosmological redshift and dark energy parameters.
cosmic_dust.fun_fact: Analysis of meteorites shows that cosmic dust can preserve presolar grains — microscopic crystals of silicon carbide and diamond, formed in the atmospheres of ancient stars and surviving the formation of the Solar System.

formulas.description: Power-law size distribution of dust grains according to the Mathis–Rumpl–Nordsieck (MRN) model
formulas.meaning: n(a) is the number of dust grains with radius a; the distribution is valid for the size range from about 0.005 to 0.25 µm and describes the relative abundance of particles of different sizes in diffuse interstellar clouds.

raw.mini: Cosmic dust is the smoky veil of the Universe, hiding the light of distant stars while simultaneously serving as raw material for new worlds.
raw.practical_application: From it, planets and even ourselves form; dust scatters radio signals, interfering with satellite communications and GPS.
raw.description_popular: Cosmic dust consists of solid microscopic particles of carbon, silicates, and ices, formed in the atmospheres of cool giant stars or during supernova explosions. It efficiently absorbs ultraviolet and visible radiation, re-emitting it in the infrared. The thickness of dust clouds can be such that they completely hide optical radiation, allowing hidden objects to be studied only in infrared and radio waves.
raw.fun_fact_popular: In one cubic kilometer of interstellar space, there can be only a few hundred dust grains, but their total mass in the galaxy is comparable to billions of suns.
raw.description_simple: Cosmic dust is tiny particles (the size of smoke or flour) floating in space. They are like dust specks in a flashlight beam: almost invisible individually, but together they obscure the light of distant stars. Dust consists of carbon, silicates (compounds of silicon and oxygen), and ices, forming in the atmospheres of dying stars or during supernova explosions (giant stellar explosions).

red_dwarf.name: red dwarf
red_dwarf.mini: A cosmic candle burning so frugally that it will last for trillions of years — longer than the Universe has existed.
red_dwarf.practical_application: The most numerous stars in the Universe. Most known exoplanets, including those in the habitable zone where liquid water is possible, orbit them.
red_dwarf.description_popular: Red dwarfs are M-class stars (mass from 0.075 to 0.5 solar masses) with surface temperatures of 2000–3500 K. Their luminosity ranges from hundredths to a few percent of the Sun's. Thanks to full convection in their interiors, hydrogen is consumed extremely sparingly, so their lifetime exceeds the age of the Universe, and no red dwarf has yet left the main sequence. Many have planets, and terrestrial-type planets often lie in the habitable zone.
red_dwarf.fun_fact_popular: The closest red dwarf, Proxima Centauri, has an Earth-sized planet in the habitable zone, despite frequent powerful flares from the star.
red_dwarf.description_simple: A red dwarf is a small and relatively cool star. Like a slowly smoldering campfire, it consumes fuel extremely frugally, so it lives very long. The red color comes from the low surface temperature — 2000–3500 °C (the Sun is about 5500 °C). Red dwarfs are lighter and dimmer than the Sun, but for every star like ours, there are several such dwarfs.
red_dwarf.history_simple: The first red dwarfs were observed as early as the 19th century. In the early 20th century, astronomers identified them as a separate class of low-mass stars. For a long time, it was believed that they could not have planets with life, but modern discoveries have shown otherwise.
red_dwarf.how_it_works_simple: Inside a red dwarf, hydrogen turns into helium, releasing energy, but the reactions proceed slowly due to low core temperature and pressure. Unlike the Sun, the star's material is fully mixed: hydrogen from outer layers constantly flows into the core, and helium does not accumulate. Hence, the star shines stably for trillions of years.
red_dwarf.description: Red dwarfs are low-mass stars (0.075–0.5 solar masses) of spectral class M on the main sequence. Due to low core temperatures (a few million kelvin), thermonuclear fusion occurs only via the proton-proton chain (the CNO cycle does not operate). Full convection mixes all stellar material, ensuring a homogeneous chemical composition and preventing the accumulation of a helium core. Surface temperature (2000–3500 K) shifts the emission maximum to the infrared. Luminosity ranges from 0.0001 to 0.05 solar. The rate of thermonuclear reactions in such stars is extremely low, so their main-sequence lifetime reaches 10¹²–10¹³ years. Many red dwarfs exhibit flare activity due to strong magnetic fields generated by convection and rotation. During flares, ultraviolet and X-ray radiation can increase hundreds or thousands of times, creating extreme conditions for the atmospheres of close exoplanets.
red_dwarf.history: Class M was identified in the Harvard spectral classification in the early 20th century. Henri Poincaré laid the foundations of the theory of convective stars, and later Subrahmanyan Chandrasekhar described the structure of low-mass stars in detail. In the 1990s, surveys showed that red dwarfs are the most numerous type of stars in the Galaxy. Interest in them grew with the search for exoplanets: the first Earth-like planets in the habitable zone were found around red dwarfs.
red_dwarf.how_it_works: Thermonuclear fusion in red dwarfs proceeds via the proton-proton chain: the core temperature is insufficient for the CNO cycle. The key evolutionary parameter is mass. At masses less than 0.35 M☉, the star remains fully convective throughout its life; at higher masses, a radiative core eventually forms. Energy generation is described by the equation of state for degenerate or ideal gas depending on mass. Due to low luminosity, the main-sequence stage lasts trillions of years. After exhausting hydrogen, the star (theoretically) becomes a blue dwarf and then a white dwarf. Full convection, through the dynamo mechanism, generates strong magnetic fields that cause powerful flares and coronal mass ejections. The emission spectrum follows Planck's law with a peak in the near-infrared; class M is characterized by molecular bands of titanium and vanadium oxides (TiO, VO).
key_problems: The red dwarf paradox: the observed number of low-mass stars is lower than theoretical star formation models predict.
Atmospheric erosion of exoplanets around active red dwarfs: can life survive under intense ultraviolet radiation and flares?
The exact mechanism of magnetic field generation in fully convective stars.
red_dwarf.fun_fact: If humanity survived until the time when the Sun becomes a red giant and fades, the nearest red dwarfs would still shine almost as brightly as today — their age at that point would be only 0.1% of their total lifetime.

raw.mini: A cosmic candle burning so frugally that it will last for trillions of years — longer than the Universe has existed.
raw.practical_application: The most common stars in the Universe; most discovered exoplanets, including potentially habitable ones, orbit them.
raw.description_popular: Red dwarfs are M-class main-sequence stars with masses from 0.075 to 0.5 solar masses and photospheric temperatures of 2000–3500 K. Their luminosity can range from 0.01% to a few percent of the Sun's. Thanks to full convection in their interiors, they consume hydrogen extremely sparingly: their lifetime exceeds the age of the Universe, so no red dwarf has yet left the main sequence. Research shows that many red dwarfs have planetary systems, and terrestrial-type planets often lie in the habitable zone.
raw.fun_fact_popular: The closest red dwarf to the Sun, Proxima Centauri, has an Earth-sized planet in the habitable zone, although the star periodically bombards it with powerful flares.
raw.description_simple: A red dwarf is a small and relatively cool star. Imagine a campfire that smolders rather than blazes: fuel (hydrogen) is consumed slowly, so the star lives very long. Its color is red because the surface is heated to 'only' 2000–3500 degrees (the Sun is 5500). Red dwarfs are lighter and dimmer than the Sun, but there are so many of them that for every star like ours, there are several dwarfs.
raw.history_simple: The first red dwarfs were observed as early as the 19th century. In the early 20th century, astronomers realized they were a separate class of low-mass stars. For a long time, it was believed that they could not have planets with life, but modern discoveries have shown otherwise.
raw.how_it_works_simple: Inside a red dwarf, hydrogen turns into helium, releasing energy, but the process occurs leisurely. Due to the small mass, core temperature and pressure are low, so reactions proceed slowly. Unlike more massive stars, the material inside is fully mixed: 'fresh' hydrogen constantly flows into the core, and helium does not accumulate. Therefore, the dwarf shines stably almost unchanged for trillions of years.
raw.description: Red dwarfs are low-mass stars (0.075–0.5 M☉) of spectral class M on the main sequence. Their internal structure differs from the Sun's: due to low core temperatures (a few million kelvin), the CNO hydrogen cycle does not operate, and energy is produced exclusively via the proton-proton chain. The most important feature is full convection, covering the entire volume of the star: this ensures a uniform chemical composition and prevents the accumulation of a helium core. Surface temperature ranges from 2000–3500 K, making the emission peak in the infrared region. Typical luminosity is 0.0001–0.05 L☉. Since the rate of thermonuclear reactions strongly depends on temperature and density, in low-mass stars it is extremely low, and the main-sequence lifetime reaches 10¹²–10¹³ years. Many red dwarfs exhibit flare activity associated with magnetic fields generated by convection and differential rotation. During active phases, their ultraviolet and X-ray radiation can exceed the quiet level by orders of magnitude, creating extreme conditions for the atmospheres of close exoplanets.
raw.history: Spectral class M was identified in the Harvard classification in the early 20th century. A decisive contribution was made by Henri Poincaré, who laid the foundations of the theory of convective stars, and later Subrahmanyan Chandrasekhar investigated the structure of low-mass stars. In the 1990s, surveys with new-generation telescopes showed that red dwarfs are the numerically dominant stellar population in the galaxy. Interest in them grew in connection with the search for exoplanets: the first Earth-like planets in the habitable zone were discovered around red dwarfs.
raw.how_it_works: Thermonuclear fusion in red dwarfs is maintained by the proton-proton chain, as the core temperature is insufficient to overcome the Coulomb barrier in the CNO cycle. The main parameter determining evolution is mass: at mass less than 0.35 M☉ the star is fully convective throughout its life; at higher mass, a radiative core eventually forms. Energy generation is regulated by the equation of state for degenerate or ideal gas depending on mass. Due to low luminosity, the main sequence is the longest evolutionary stage, and after hydrogen exhaustion, the star transitions to a blue dwarf stage (theoretically) and then a white dwarf. Full convection generates strong magnetic fields via the dynamo mechanism, explaining powerful flares and coronal mass ejections. Planck's radiation law determines the spectrum with a peak in the near-IR, and spectral class M is additionally characterized by molecular bands of TiO and VO.
key_problems: The red dwarf paradox: the observed number of low-mass stars is lower than theoretical star formation models predict.
Atmospheric erosion of exoplanets around active red dwarfs: can life survive under intense ultraviolet radiation and flares?
The exact mechanism of magnetic field generation in fully convective stars.
raw.fun_fact: If humanity survived until the era when the Sun becomes a red giant and fades, the nearest red dwarfs would still shine almost as brightly as today — their age at that point would not exceed 0.1% of their total lifetime.

category.name: category
category.mini: A category is a map of relations, where what matters is not the objects but the transformations between them: as if instead of cities, you studied the network of roads and the rules of their connection.
category.practical_application: This approach facilitates the transfer of ideas between different branches of mathematics. In computer science, categories are used for strict type checking of programs, and in physics, for modeling quantum computations.
category.description_popular: Formally, a category is defined by a class of objects and, for each pair of objects, a set of morphisms (arrows representing admissible transformations) with a composition operation. Composition is associative: if you apply arrows f, then g, then h, it does not matter whether you group them as h after (g after f) or as (h after g) after f. For each object, there is an identity arrow that leaves it unchanged. The essence of the theory is to ignore the internal structure of objects and focus on the connections between them, providing a universal language for constructing such things as the product of objects (a way to combine two objects) or the colimit (a way to merge information from several objects).
category.fun_fact_popular: Category theory is sometimes jokingly called 'abstract nonsense', emphasizing its extreme generality, which nonetheless has provided a rigorous foundation for many areas of mathematics.
category.description_simple: In a category, there are objects (e.g., numbers or geometric figures) and arrows showing how one object transforms into another. Arrows can be connected: if there is an arrow from A to B and from B to C, then you get a composite arrow from A to C. It is like a road system: from city A to city C you can travel via city B; the whole route is like a composite arrow.
category.history_simple: Category theory was created in the 1940s by American mathematicians Saunders Mac Lane and Samuel Eilenberg. They needed a language to compare very different areas — algebra and topology.
category.how_it_works_simple: The basic rule is the ability to combine arrows without additional conditions. If you go from A to B, then from B to C, and then from C to D, the final route does not depend on how you group the segments: first go A→B→C and then C→D, or A→B and then B→C→D. Thanks to this, routes can be safely combined into long chains.
category.description: Category theory is a branch of mathematics in which properties of objects are described through the connections between them rather than their internal structure. A category C consists of: 1) a class of objects Ob(C); 2) for each ordered pair of objects (A, B), a set of morphisms Hom_C(A, B); 3) an associative binary operation of composition ∘: Hom(B, C) × Hom(A, B) → Hom(A, C); 4) for each object A, an identity morphism id_A satisfying f ∘ id_A = f = id_B ∘ f for every morphism f: A → B. Functors (mappings between categories preserving structure), natural transformations, and adjoint functors provide the basis for universal properties. The categorical language has penetrated algebraic topology, algebraic geometry, logic (toposes as models of set theory), and computer science (categorical semantics of programming languages).
category.history: Categories emerged in the work of Samuel Eilenberg and Saunders Mac Lane (1942–1945) on the axiomatization of natural transformations in algebraic topology. In the 1950s–60s, Alexander Grothendieck rebuilt algebraic geometry in categorical language and introduced the notion of a topos. William Lawvere developed categorical logic. Since the 1980s, categories have been actively applied in computer science (e.g., monads in functional programming). In the 21st century, applications appeared in quantum physics (categorical quantum mechanics) and type theory.
category.how_it_works: The work is based on the axioms of composition and universal properties. For example, the product of objects A and B in a category is defined not by construction but by a property: it is an object P with a pair of projections p₁: P→A, p₂: P→B, such that for any object X with morphisms f: X→A and g: X→B, there exists a unique morphism h: X→P making the diagram commute. This allows proving theorems without referring to the elements of objects. The principle of duality (reversing all arrows) doubles the number of provable statements.
key_problems: Justifying the consistency of category theory for large cardinals (Grothendieck's size problem).
Developing synthetic homotopy theory and its application in physics (higher categories for quantum gravity).
Constructing a rigorous categorical model of a full quantum field theory.
category.fun_fact: The Yoneda lemma — a cornerstone of the theory — states that an object is completely determined by how it interacts with all other objects, which is a mathematical analogue of the saying 'tell me who your friends are, and I'll tell you who you are'.

formulas.description: Associativity law for composition of morphisms
formulas.meaning: f: A → B, g: B → C, h: C → D are morphisms in the category; ∘ is the composition operation. Associativity means that the result of sequential application does not depend on the parentheses ordering.

raw.mini: A mathematical category is a universe where you study not the objects themselves, but how they are connected. As if instead of describing people, you described only their relationships and how they interact.
raw.practical_application: This viewpoint simplifies the transfer of ideas between different branches of mathematics. It is also used in computer science: functional programming languages (Haskell) use categories for strict typing, and in physics, for describing quantum computations.
raw.description_popular: Formally, a category is defined by a class of objects and a set of morphisms between them with a composition operation. Composition is associative: h∘(g∘f) = (h∘g)∘f, and for each object there is an identity morphism (an arrow that changes nothing). The idea is to abandon the internal structure of objects and focus on external connections. This has allowed the creation of a universal language in which such constructions as product, colimit, or natural transformation are formulated.
raw.fun_fact_popular: Category theory is sometimes called 'abstract nonsense' — a playful name that reflects its all-pervading abstraction, which, nevertheless, has provided rigorous foundations for many areas of mathematics.
raw.description_simple: In a category, there are objects — for example, numbers, spaces, or groups — and arrows (morphisms) showing how one object turns into another. These arrows can be chained: if there is an arrow from A to B and from B to C, then you can build an arrow from A to C simply by going through them. It is like a road system between cities: you can travel from city A to city C via city B, and the route itself is like a composite arrow.
raw.history_simple: Category theory was proposed in the 1940s by American mathematicians Saunders Mac Lane and Samuel Eilenberg. They needed a language to compare very different areas of mathematics — algebra and topology.
raw.how_it_works_simple: The basic rule: arrows obey 'associativity'. This means that if you go from A to B, then from B to C, then from C to D, it does not matter how you group the path: first (A→B→C) and then C→D, or first A→B and then (B→C→D) — the final route is the same. Thanks to this rule, arrows can be safely combined.
raw.description: Category theory is a branch of mathematics studying properties of mappings that are invariant under replacement of objects. A category C consists of: 1) a class of objects Ob(C); 2) for each ordered pair of objects (A, B), a set of morphisms Hom_C(A, B); 3) an associative binary operation of composition ∘: Hom(B, C) × Hom(A, B) → Hom(A, C); 4) for each object A, an identity morphism id_A such that for any morphism f: A → B, f ∘ id_A = f = id_B ∘ f. The concepts of functor (a mapping between categories preserving structure), natural transformation, and adjoint functor serve as the basis for constructing universal properties. The categorical language has penetrated algebraic topology, algebraic geometry, logic (toposes as a categorical model of set theory), and theoretical computer science (categorical semantics of programming languages).
raw.history: The theory originated from the joint work of Samuel Eilenberg and Saunders Mac Lane (1942–1945) on the axiomatization of natural transformations in algebraic topology. In the 1950s–60s, Alexander Grothendieck rethought algebraic geometry in terms of categories and introduced the notion of a topos. In parallel, William Lawvere developed categorical logic. Since the 1980s, categories have been actively used in computer science (e.g., monads in functional programming). In the 21st century, applications emerged in quantum physics (categorical quantum mechanics) and type theory.
raw.how_it_works: The principle of operation is based on the axioms of composition and universal properties. For example, the product in a category is defined not by a construction but by a property: for any two objects A and B, their product is an object P with a pair of projections p₁: P→A, p₂: P→B, such that for any object X with morphisms f: X→A, g: X→B, there exists a unique morphism h: X→P making the diagram commute. This allows proving theorems without referring to the elements of objects. Categorical dualizations (reversing all arrows) double the number of theorems (duality principle).
key_problems: Justifying the consistency of category theory for large cardinals (Grothendieck's size problem).
Developing synthetic homotopy theory and its transfer to physics (higher categories for quantum gravity).
Constructing a rigorous categorical model of a full quantum field theory.
raw.fun_fact: The Yoneda lemma, a cornerstone of the theory, states that an object is completely determined by how all other objects act on it — an analogue of the proverb 'tell me who your friends are, and I'll tell you who you are' in mathematics.

kilonova.name: kilonova
kilonova.mini: A kilonova is a cosmic explosion a thousand times brighter than a nova, creating gold and platinum during the merger of neutron stars.
kilonova.practical_application: Studying kilonovae reveals the origin of heavy elements — from gold in jewelry to rare-earth metals in electronics.
kilonova.description_popular: A kilonova flares up when two neutron stars or a neutron star and a black hole collide. The ejected matter, supersaturated with neutrons, transforms into heavy nuclei via rapid neutron capture (r-process). The brightness of the flare is powered by the radioactive decay of newborn isotopes such as nickel-56 and even californium.
kilonova.fun_fact_popular: A single kilonova can synthesize an amount of gold comparable to the mass of several Earth-like planets.
kilonova.description_simple: The collision of two neutron stars — superdense remnants of dead stars the size of a city — produces an explosion called a kilonova. Like the merging of two drops of water, but instead of splashes, jets of matter rich in precious metals fly out. The glow lasts several days.
kilonova.history_simple: Theory predicted kilonovae in 1998, but they were first observed in 2017, simultaneously recording gravitational waves from the same event.
kilonova.how_it_works_simple: The impact generates a cloud of superheavy atomic nuclei, which quickly decay, glowing due to radioactivity — like the flickering embers of a campfire. This glow is what astronomers observe.
kilonova.description: A kilonova is an astronomical transient (variable event) with a peak luminosity on the order of 10⁴¹ erg/s, roughly a thousand times brighter than a classical nova but fainter than a supernova. The energy source is the radioactive decay of unstable nuclei synthesized via the r-process (rapid neutron capture) in the ejected material. Neutron star mergers are accompanied by a gravitational-wave signal, a short gamma-ray burst, and subsequent afterglow in optical, infrared, and radio bands. The kilonova spectrum features broad absorption lines formed in the expanding shell, rich in lanthanides and actinides. Expansion velocities reach 0.1–0.3 times the speed of light. Modeling shows multiple components of the ejecta: a dynamical (tidal) tail, wind from the accretion disk, and a jet outflow.
kilonova.history: The kilonova concept was proposed in 1998 by Li-Xin Li and Bohdan Paczyński, who predicted bright radioactive glow from matter ejected during neutron star mergers, calling it a 'macronova' or 'r-process supernova'. The term 'kilonova' later stuck by analogy with nova and supernova. The first observation occurred on August 17, 2017, when the LIGO and Virgo detectors registered gravitational waves from the merger of two neutron stars (GW170817), and over the following hours and days, observatories worldwide detected the electromagnetic source AT2017gfo, unambiguously identified as a kilonova.
kilonova.how_it_works: During the merger of neutron stars, tidal forces eject part of the material at high speed. This material is neutron-rich (neutron density reaches 10²⁰ cm⁻³) and undergoes rapid neutron capture, forming elements up to uranium and thorium. Unstable isotopes then decay, releasing energy: beta decay and nuclear fission heat the shell to temperatures around 10,000 K, causing thermal emission in the optical range. The light curve (brightness over time) has a characteristic hump with a rise time of about a day and subsequent decline following a power law. Gravitational waves from the merger are described by Einstein's equations of general relativity, and accretion disk dynamics by magnetohydrodynamic equations.
key_problems: What is the contribution of kilonovae to the galactic content of heavy elements compared to other sources (supernovae, collapsars)?
The equation of state of neutron star matter is unknown, affecting ejecta parameters and luminosity.
The merger rate of neutron stars remains uncertain, limiting the accuracy of predictions for the chemical evolution of galaxies.
A more detailed understanding of ejecta opacity, especially for lanthanides, is needed to compare models with observations.
kilonova.fun_fact: If you are holding a gold ring, remember: the atoms of that gold were likely born in a kilonova — the collision of two neutron stars long before the formation of the Solar System.

raw.mini: A kilonova is a cosmic explosion a thousand times brighter than a nova, creating gold and platinum during the merger of neutron stars.
raw.practical_application: Kilonovae help understand the origin of heavy elements, including those in your smartphone and jewelry.
raw.description_popular: A kilonova occurs during the merger of two neutron stars or a neutron star with a black hole. The process is accompanied by the ejection of neutron-rich material, where the r-process (rapid neutron capture) synthesizes elements heavier than iron. The luminosity is sustained by the decay of radioactive isotopes, mainly nickel-56 and heavier nuclei such as californium.
raw.fun_fact_popular: A single kilonova can produce an amount of gold several times the mass of Earth.
raw.description_simple: When two superdense neutron stars (remnants of exploded giants, compressed to the size of a city) collide, a tremendous explosion occurs — a kilonova. It's like colliding two drops of water, but instead of splashes, a stream of precious metals scatters through space. The main glow lasts several days.
raw.history_simple: Predicted in 1998, first spotted by telescopes in 2017 simultaneously with gravitational waves — 'ripples in spacetime'.
raw.how_it_works_simple: The impact creates a cloud of superheavy nuclei that rapidly decay, glowing due to radioactivity — like the flickering embers of a campfire. This light is the kilonova.
raw.description: A kilonova is an astronomical transient (variable event) with a peak luminosity on the order of 10⁴¹ erg/s, roughly a thousand times brighter than a classical nova but fainter than a supernova. The energy source is the radioactive decay of unstable nuclei synthesized via the r-process (rapid neutron capture) in the ejected material. Neutron star mergers are accompanied by a gravitational-wave signal, a short gamma-ray burst, and subsequent afterglow in optical, infrared, and radio bands. The kilonova spectrum features broad absorption lines formed in the expanding shell, rich in lanthanides and actinides. Expansion velocities reach 0.1–0.3 times the speed of light. Modeling shows multiple components of the ejecta: a dynamical (tidal) tail, wind from the accretion disk, and a jet outflow.

The term 'kilonova' later stuck by analogy with nova and supernova. The first observation occurred on August 17, 2017, when the LIGO and Virgo detectors registered gravitational waves from the merger of two neutron stars (GW170817), and over the following hours and days, observatories worldwide detected the electromagnetic source AT2017gfo, unambiguously identified as a kilonova.
raw.how_it_works: During the merger of neutron stars, tidal forces eject part of the material at high speed. This material is neutron-rich (the neutron-to-nucleus ratio reaches 10²⁰) and undergoes rapid neutron capture, forming elements up to uranium and thorium. Unstable isotopes then decay, releasing energy: beta decay and nuclear fission heat the shell to temperatures around 10,000 K, causing thermal emission in the optical range. The light curve (brightness over time) has a characteristic hump with a rise time of about a day and subsequent decline following a power law. Gravitational waves from the merger are described by Einstein's equations of general relativity, and accretion disk dynamics by magnetohydrodynamic equations.

The equation of state of neutron star matter is unknown, affecting ejecta parameters and luminosity.
The merger rate of neutron stars remains uncertain, limiting the accuracy of predictions for the chemical evolution of galaxies.
A more detailed understanding of ejecta opacity, especially for lanthanides, is needed to compare models with observations.
