active_galactic_nuclei.name: active galactic nucleus
active_galactic_nuclei.mini: A galactic nucleus blazing brighter than all its stars combined, powered by matter falling into a supermassive black hole.
active_galactic_nuclei.practical_application: Quasars — the most powerful active nuclei — serve as beacons for studying the Universe in its infancy. Their jets heat intergalactic gas, influencing the growth of entire galaxies.
active_galactic_nuclei.description_popular: An active galactic nucleus is a compact central region whose luminosity far exceeds the total radiation of all the galaxy's stars. The energy comes from accretion (infall) of gas and dust onto a supermassive black hole (millions to billions of solar masses). The material spirals into a disk, heating to millions of degrees, and shines brightly in X-ray and ultraviolet. Powerful magnetic fields sometimes create jets — relativistic plasma streams ejected over tens of thousands of light-years.
active_galactic_nuclei.fun_fact_popular: Light from the most distant quasar traveled 13 billion years to reach us — it existed when the Universe was only 700 million years old.
active_galactic_nuclei.description_simple: At the heart of most large galaxies lies a supermassive black hole — an object with gravity so strong that even light cannot escape. When gas and dust fall into the hole, they swirl into a flattened disk, collide, and heat to incredible temperatures, producing a dazzling glow. This turns an ordinary galactic center into an active source, shining like a cosmic spotlight.
active_galactic_nuclei.history_simple: The first active nuclei — quasars — were discovered in the early 1960s. Radio astronomers found point-like sources resembling stars, but their spectra matched no known object. In 1963, Maarten Schmidt realized they were distant galaxies with an unusually bright center.
active_galactic_nuclei.how_it_works_simple: Gas and dust around the black hole form a rapidly rotating accretion disk — a flattened cloud like a whirlpool. Friction and gravity convert rotational energy into heat and light. Some material is ejected outward as narrow jets — along the black hole's rotation axis.
active_galactic_nuclei.description: An active galactic nucleus (AGN) is the central region of a galaxy exhibiting non-stellar electromagnetic radiation with significant variability on timescales from hours to years. The model includes a supermassive black hole (mass 10^6 to 10^9 solar masses) and an accretion disk, where material heats through viscous friction to temperatures from tens of thousands to millions of degrees, producing a characteristic 'ultraviolet bump'. The disk's ionizing radiation excites gas clouds, generating broad and narrow emission lines. Bipolar jets often form, radiating through synchrotron and inverse Compton processes. Depending on jet power and orientation to the observer, AGNs are classified as radio-loud (quasars, blazars) or radio-quiet (Seyfert galaxies).
active_galactic_nuclei.history: The first signs of nuclear activity were noticed by E. Fari in 1908, who detected bright lines in the spectrum of NGC 1068. In 1943, Carl Seyfert systematized such objects. Powerful radio sources of the 1950s, looking like stars but with strange spectra, led to the discovery of quasars. In 1963, Maarten Schmidt deciphered the spectrum of 3C 273, realizing the lines were highly redshifted due to cosmic expansion. Ya. B. Zeldovich and E. Salpeter independently proposed accretion as the energy source, and in 1969, D. Lynden-Bell proved that supermassive black holes hide in galactic centers. Modern telescopes — from Hubble to the Event Horizon Telescope — continue to unveil AGN structure.
active_galactic_nuclei.how_it_works: Accretion theory explains AGN energy output. Gas captured from the surroundings has angular momentum and forms a rotating disk around the black hole. Viscosity caused by magnetorotational instability (MRI) allows matter to lose energy and gradually approach the hole, heating and radiating. As it falls into the black hole, gravitational energy is released: up to about 10% of rest mass is converted to radiation (E=mc²), tens of times more efficient than thermonuclear fusion. Rotating magnetized black holes can also tap their spin energy via the Blandford–Znajek mechanism, launching relativistic jets. The non-thermal continuum arises from synchrotron radiation of electrons and inverse Compton scattering of photons on accelerated particles. The spectrum of radio-quiet AGNs is well described by the standard disk model of Shakura and Sunyaev.
key_problems: How did supermassive black holes acquire billions of solar masses so early in the Universe's history?
The nature of rapid AGN variability (hours to days) — disk instabilities or episodic magnetic events?
The exact mechanism of jet launching — how is rotational energy extracted and collimated?
Nuclear activity's impact on the host galaxy: how do radiation and jets quench or trigger star formation?
active_galactic_nuclei.fun_fact: Some AGNs can change brightness by tens of times in just a few hours. This indicates that the emitting region is extremely small — on the order of the black hole's gravitational radius, which for a billion solar masses is about three billion kilometers.

formulas.description: Eddington luminosity limit for spherical accretion, defining the balance between radiation pressure and gravity.
formulas.meaning: L_Edd is the Eddington luminosity (maximum luminosity for stable accretion), G is the gravitational constant (≈6.67×10⁻¹¹ m³ kg⁻¹ s⁻²), M is the black hole mass, m_p is the proton mass (≈1.67×10⁻²⁷ kg), c is the speed of light (≈3×10⁸ m/s), σ_T is the Thomson scattering cross-section of the electron (≈6.65×10⁻²⁹ m²)

raw.mini: A galactic heart beating so strongly that its light outshines all surrounding stars — this is the trace of matter falling into a supermassive black hole.
raw.practical_application: Quasars, the brightest active nuclei, are visible at the edge of the Universe and serve as beacons for studying its early history; AGN jets heat intergalactic gas, regulating galaxy growth.
raw.description_popular: An active galactic nucleus is a compact region at the center of a galaxy, whose luminosity far exceeds the total radiation of all its stars. The energy source is accretion (infall of matter) onto a supermassive black hole with a mass of millions to billions of solar masses. The rotating accretion disk heats to millions of kelvin, emitting in X-ray and ultraviolet. Magnetic fields can form powerful jets — relativistic plasma streams shooting out over tens of thousands of light-years.
raw.fun_fact_popular: The most distant known quasar existed when the Universe was only 700 million years old — its light traveled 13 billion years to us.
raw.description_simple: At the center of almost every large galaxy hides a supermassive black hole (an object with gravity so strong that even light cannot escape). As surrounding gas and dust are sucked in, they spiral into a disk, collide, and heat to enormous temperatures, emitting dazzling light. This process turns the galactic nucleus into an active source — a cosmic 'spotlight' visible across half the Universe.
raw.history_simple: The first active nuclei — quasars — were discovered in the early 1960s. Radio astronomers noticed bright point-like sources that looked like stars but had spectra unlike anything known. Maarten Schmidt in 1963 realized they were distant galaxies with exceptionally bright centers.
raw.how_it_works_simple: Around the black hole, gas and dust gather into a rapidly spinning accretion disk (a flattened cloud, like a whirlpool), where friction and gravity convert motion energy into heat and light. Some matter is ejected outward as narrow jets — along the black hole's rotation axis.
raw.description: An active galactic nucleus (AGN) is the central region of a galaxy exhibiting non-stellar electromagnetic radiation with significant variability on timescales from hours to years. The physical model includes a supermassive black hole (10^6–10^9 M☉) surrounded by an optically thick accretion disk that radiates thermal energy due to viscous heating. The inner parts of the disk reach temperatures of ~10^5–10^6 K, producing a characteristic 'ultraviolet bump'. The broad-line region (BLR) and narrow-line region (NLR) are excited by ionizing radiation from the disk. Bipolar collimated jets are often present, emitting through synchrotron and inverse Compton radiation, as well as radio lobes. The presence of jets and their orientation relative to the observer determines the classification into radio-loud (quasars, blazars) and radio-quiet (Seyfert galaxies) AGNs.
raw.history: In 1908, E. Fari first registered emission lines in the spectrum of NGC 1068, later classified as a Seyfert galaxy. Carl Seyfert in 1943 systematized such objects. In the 1950s, powerful radio sources identified with faint stars led to the discovery of quasars: in 1963, Maarten Schmidt identified the spectrum of 3C 273 and realized its lines were shifted due to cosmological redshift. Soon Ya. B. Zeldovich and E. Salpeter proposed accretion as the energy source, and D. Lynden-Bell in 1969 proved that supermassive black holes reside in galactic centers. Modern observations (Hubble, SDSS surveys, Event Horizon Telescope) detail AGN structure.
raw.how_it_works: AGN energy output is described by accretion theory. Matter from the interstellar medium with specific angular momentum forms a Keplerian accretion disk, viscosity arising from magnetorotational instability (MRI). Gravitational energy is released as matter falls into the black hole: the mass-to-radiation conversion efficiency reaches ~0.1 c² (Einstein's E=mc²), an order of magnitude more efficient than thermonuclear reactions. The black hole magnetosphere (Blandford–Znajek process) can extract rotational energy from the ergosphere, launching relativistic jets. Synchrotron radiation of electrons in magnetic fields and inverse Compton scattering form the non-thermal continuum. The classic 'standard disk' model of Shakura–Sunyaev successfully describes the spectrum of radio-quiet AGNs.
key_problems: The mechanism of formation and growth of supermassive black holes in the early Universe
The nature of short-timescale AGN variability (hours-days) — disk instabilities or magnetic flares?
Jet origin: exactly how is the black hole's rotational energy converted into a directed stream?
The connection between nuclear activity and galaxy evolution (coevolution) — feedback mechanisms
raw.fun_fact: Some active nuclei change brightness by tens of times in just a few hours — this defies naive models and points to extremely compact emitting regions on the order of the gravitational radius.

water.name: Water
water.mini: Water is the foundation of life, a substance capable of existing simultaneously as ice, liquid, and vapor under Earth conditions. Due to anomalous expansion upon freezing, ice floats, saving water bodies from freezing to the bottom.
water.practical_application: A universal solvent for chemical reactions, heat carrier in cooling and heating systems, basis of biological fluids, key factor in Earth's climate system.
water.description_popular: Water (H₂O) is a polar compound with a bond angle of about 104.5°. Oxygen pulls electrons, acquiring a partial negative charge, while hydrogens become positive. The resulting hydrogen bonds create a dynamic network, making water an excellent solvent for many substances. Water reaches its maximum density at 4 °C, not at freezing; this leads to convective mixing, preventing water bodies from freezing solid.
water.fun_fact_popular: Hot water sometimes freezes faster than cold water — the mysterious Mpemba effect, for which there is still no clear explanation.
water.description_simple: Water is a colorless and tasteless liquid with the formula H₂O. Its molecule is like a corner: oxygen in the center, two hydrogens on the sides. Water dissolves many substances, earning it the title 'universal solvent'. When freezing, water expands, so ice is lighter than liquid water and floats. High heat capacity allows water to heat up and cool down slowly, smoothing out climate fluctuations.
water.history_simple: In 1781, Henry Cavendish synthesized water by burning hydrogen and realized it was a compound. The precise atomic ratio (H₂O) was established by Joseph Louis Gay-Lussac and Alexander von Humboldt in the early 19th century.
water.how_it_works_simple: Water's unusual properties are due to hydrogen bonds: the positively charged hydrogens of one molecule attract the negative oxygens of another. This network is like many tiny magnets that hold molecules together and require a lot of energy to break — hence the high boiling and melting points compared to similar molecules.
water.description: Water is a triatomic polar molecule with sp³ hybridization of oxygen orbitals, giving a tetrahedral coordination and a network of hydrogen bonds. In the liquid phase, hydrogen bonds constantly break and reform (lifetime ~1–10 ps), but their cooperative dynamics lead to anomalous properties: negative coefficient of thermal expansion below 4 °C, high dielectric constant (~80), large surface tension. Near the critical point (647 K, 22.1 MPa), water becomes a supercritical fluid. Ice has more than ten crystalline phases, including common hexagonal ice Ih and ferroelectric ice XI.
water.history: In antiquity, water was considered one of the primary elements. In 1781, Henry Cavendish synthesized water; Lavoisier named its components hydrogen and oxygen. Gay-Lussac and Humboldt determined the volume ratio 2:1. In the 20th century, X-ray diffraction and neutron scattering revealed the structure of water and ice; high-pressure ice phases were discovered by Percy Bridgman. Quantum chemical models of hydrogen bonding were developed by John Pople.
water.how_it_works: The hydrogen bond in water has a partially covalent character (~10%) and energy of about 20 kJ/mol. Its directionality and saturation ensure a local tetrahedral structure, which makes ice have a more open packing and lower density. Upon melting, some bonds break, but short-range order persists. When heated from 0 to 4 °C, residual ordering collapses, increasing density. Quantum effects (proton tunneling) are important for the properties of heavy water and some ice phases.
key_problems: Complete description of ice's phase diagram at ultra-high pressures, including possible metallic and ionic forms.
Nature of supercooled water's vitrification and the hypothetical second liquid–liquid critical point.
Mechanism of the Mpemba effect.
Influence of quantum effects on hydrogen bond dynamics and enzymatic reactions in aqueous environment.
water.fun_fact: There is a hypothesis about 'wire water': in nanopores, molecules can line up into one-dimensional chains, exhibiting unusual electrical properties.

formulas.description: Ion product of water
formulas.meaning: K_w is the equilibrium constant of water autoprotolysis (ion product); [H⁺] is hydrogen ion concentration (mol/L); [OH⁻] is hydroxide ion concentration (mol/L). At 25 °C, K_w = 1.0×10⁻¹⁴. Depends on temperature.

raw.mini: Water — the 'solvent of life', the only substance that under ordinary conditions exists in three states: ice, liquid, vapor. Its anomalies — for example, ice is lighter than water — make life possible under ice.
raw.practical_application: A universal solvent for chemical reactions, heat carrier in cooling systems, basis of biological fluids and Earth's climate system.
raw.description_popular: Water (H₂O) is a polar (with uneven charge distribution) compound with an H–O–H bond angle of about 104.5°. The oxygen atom pulls electrons towards itself, creating a partial negative charge, and the hydrogens a positive one, which provides for the formation of hydrogen bonds. These bonds partially order liquid water, making it a unique solvent for ionic and many polar substances. Liquid water has its maximum density at 4 °C, not at the freezing point, which leads to convective mixing in water bodies.
raw.fun_fact_popular: When freezing, hot water sometimes turns into ice faster than cold water — this is the Mpemba effect, which still has no single generally accepted explanation.
raw.description_simple: Water is a transparent liquid without taste or smell, chemical formula H₂O. The water molecule is like a corner: an oxygen atom in the center, two hydrogens on the sides. Water is an excellent solvent, it dissolves more substances than any other liquid. Its density behaves unusually: when freezing, water expands, so ice floats. Thanks to its high heat capacity, water heats up and cools down slowly, softening the climate on Earth.
raw.history_simple: That water consists of hydrogen and oxygen was first guessed by Henry Cavendish in 1781, and the exact composition (H₂O) was established by Joseph Louis Gay-Lussac together with Alexander von Humboldt in the early 19th century.
raw.how_it_works_simple: The unusual properties of water are associated with hydrogen bonds: the positively charged hydrogens of one molecule attract the negative oxygens of another. This is like many tiny magnets that hold the molecules together and require a lot of energy to break them — hence the high boiling and melting points compared to similar substances.
raw.description: Water is a triatomic polar molecule with sp³ hybridization of oxygen orbitals, which sets a tetrahedral coordination and a network of hydrogen bonds. In the liquid phase, hydrogen bonds constantly break and reform (lifetime ~1–10 picoseconds), but their cooperative dynamics lead to anomalous thermodynamic properties: a negative coefficient of thermal expansion below 4 °C, high dielectric constant (~80), large surface tension. Near the critical point (647 K, 22.1 MPa), water becomes a supercritical fluid with unique dissolving properties. Ice has more than ten crystalline phases, including ice Ih (ordinary hexagonal) and ice XI with ferroelectric properties.
raw.history: Even in antiquity, water was considered one of the primary elements. In 1781, Henry Cavendish synthesized water by burning hydrogen. Antoine Lavoisier gave the names hydrogen and oxygen and proved that water is their compound. The exact volume ratios were determined by Gay-Lussac and Humboldt. In the 20th century, using X-ray diffraction and neutron scattering, the structure of water and ice was deciphered; the discovery of high-pressure ice phases is associated with the name of Percy Bridgman. Water anomalies were studied by many, including John Pople (development of quantum chemical models of the hydrogen bond).
raw.how_it_works: The hydrogen bond in water has a partially covalent character (up to ~10%) and energy on the order of 20 kJ/mol. Its directionality and saturation ensure a local tetrahedral structure, which leads to a looser packing of molecules in crystalline ice — hence ice's density is lower. Upon melting, some bonds break, the structure collapses, but short-range order persists, and when heated from 0 to 4 °C, residual ordering collapses, increasing density. Many anomalies are explained by the dynamic network disorder model. Quantum effects of the hydrogen nuclei (tunneling) are important for some ice phases and lead to differences in properties of light and heavy water. The Pauli principle explains the structure of electron shells.
key_problems: Complete description of ice's phase diagram at extremely high pressures, where metallic and ionic forms are possible.
Nature of supercooled water vitrification and the hypothetical second liquid–liquid critical point.
Mechanism of the Mpemba effect.
Quantum influence on hydrogen bond dynamics and enzyme reactions in aqueous environment.
raw.fun_fact: There is a hypothesis about 'polymerization' of water in nanoporous structures and the possibility of forming 'wire water', where molecules line up into one-dimensional chains exhibiting unique electrical properties.

solar_wind.name: Solar wind
solar_wind.mini: The solar wind is an invisible shower of charged particles that the Sun continuously sprays in all directions, like a giant garden sprinkler.
solar_wind.practical_application: The solar wind causes polar auroras, but it can also damage satellites and power grids; forecasting its gusts protects technological infrastructure.
solar_wind.description_popular: The solar wind is a stream of plasma (a mixture of free electrons and ions) flowing from the solar corona at speeds from 300 to 800 km/s. It carries the Sun's magnetic field frozen into it, which, due to the star's rotation, twists into a Parker spiral. Slow wind comes from quiet regions, fast wind from coronal holes. At Earth, the solar wind shapes the magnetosphere and causes geomagnetic storms.
solar_wind.fun_fact_popular: The solar wind creates a giant protective bubble around the Solar System — the heliosphere, which deflects most harmful galactic cosmic rays.
solar_wind.description_simple: The Sun constantly emits a stream of tiny charged particles: electrons (light negative) and protons (heavier positive). This wind stretches comet tails and, when it hits Earth's magnetic field, gives birth to polar auroras — shimmering curtains of light in polar regions.
solar_wind.history_simple: In the 1950s, physicist Eugene Parker predicted that the hot solar corona could not be held by gravity and must expand as a supersonic flow (faster than the speed of sound). In the early 1960s, the first spacecraft confirmed his theory.
solar_wind.how_it_works_simple: This can be compared to steam over a boiling pot: the outer atmosphere of the Sun (the corona) is heated to millions of degrees, causing particles to accelerate and fly off into space, dragging the magnetic field with them.
solar_wind.description: The solar wind is a continuous supersonic outflow of fully ionized hydrogen plasma from the solar corona. Acceleration is provided by the high pressure of the hot corona (temperature ~10⁶ K) and, presumably, wave processes (Alfvén waves). At a distance of several solar radii, the plasma speed exceeds the local Alfvén speed; this boundary is called the Alfvén surface. The wind then expands and cools, and its magnetic field twists into a Parker spiral due to the Sun's rotation. Interaction with planetary magnetospheres generates electric currents, auroras, and radiation belts. Direct measurements are made by the Parker Solar Probe and Solar Orbiter spacecraft.
solar_wind.history: The idea of particle outflow from the Sun was suggested as early as the early 20th century (Eddington assumed influence on comets), but the physical theory was developed by Eugene Parker in 1958. His work was met with skepticism until the Mariner 2 satellite registered a constant plasma flow in 1962. Further missions (Helios, Ulysses) revealed the three-dimensional structure of the wind.
solar_wind.how_it_works: The outflow is described by magnetohydrodynamic equations taking into account thermal conductivity and wave heating. In Parker's simplest model, the plasma pressure in the corona exceeds the pressure of the interplanetary medium, leading to continuous acceleration to supersonic speeds. The analytical solution of the equation of motion gives a transonic transition. The magnetic field frozen into the plasma creates the heliospheric current sheet and spiral structure. Modern models include kinetic effects and heating by coronal holes. Observations confirm predictions of the existence of slow and fast wind.
key_problems: What is the physical mechanism that heats the solar corona to millions of degrees (coronal heating problem)?
What exactly accelerates the fast solar wind in coronal holes?
How does the solar wind interact with the local interstellar medium at the heliosphere boundary?
Can extreme solar wind from young Sun-like stars completely strip the atmospheres of exoplanets?
solar_wind.fun_fact: During the solar superstorm of 1859 (the Carrington event), telegraph machines around the world sparked and worked while disconnected from batteries, and auroras were observed even in the tropics.

His work was met with skepticism until the Mariner 2 satellite registered a constant plasma flow in 1962. Further missions (Helios, Ulysses) revealed the three-dimensional structure of the wind.

sun.name: Sun
sun.mini: A giant thermonuclear reactor 150 million kilometers from Earth; its radiation is the source of almost all energy on our planet.
sun.practical_application: The basis of climate, photosynthesis, and all renewable energy; solar panels convert its light into electricity.
sun.description_popular: The Sun is a yellow dwarf of spectral class G2V, the central star of our system. Its mass is about 2×10³⁰ kg. In the core, at a temperature of 15 million kelvin, thermonuclear fusion occurs: the proton-proton chain turns hydrogen into helium-4, releasing neutrinos and gamma rays. Energy is transferred first by radiation, and closer to the surface by convection. The visible surface (photosphere) has a temperature of 5778 K and is covered with granules — convective cells. Above it are the chromosphere and corona, heated to millions of degrees (the heating mechanism is not fully clear). The Sun's activity follows an 11-year cycle, which affects the number of sunspots and flare frequency.
sun.fun_fact_popular: The Sun loses mass at a rate of about 4 million tons per second due to radiation and the solar wind, but even over billions of years this will amount to a minuscule fraction of a percent.
sun.description_simple: The Sun is a star, a hot gas ball shining due to thermonuclear conversion of hydrogen into helium in its core. Its surface temperature is about 5500 °C, in the interior — millions of degrees. By diameter it is 109 times larger than Earth, and by mass it accounts for 99.8% of the entire Solar System. Sunlight reaches Earth in 8 minutes. Without the Sun, there would be no day and night, no weather, no life.
sun.history_simple: The Sun was worshipped since ancient times. In the 19th century, Joseph Fraunhofer discovered dark lines in its spectrum, and in 1925, Cecilia Payne-Gaposchkin proved that the Sun consists mainly of hydrogen. In the 20th century, physicists uncovered the source of its energy — thermonuclear fusion.
sun.how_it_works_simple: In the Sun's core, under enormous pressure and temperature, hydrogen nuclei fuse into helium, releasing energy. This energy slowly pushes through the star's bulk to the surface and is radiated into space. Thus the Sun has been shining for 4.6 billion years and will last about as long again.
sun.description: The Sun is a main-sequence star with an age of 4.57 billion years. Hydrostatic equilibrium is maintained by a balance of gravity and gas and radiation pressure. Thermonuclear reactions take place in the core: the proton-proton chain provides 99% of the energy, the CNO cycle about 1%. The central density is about 150 g/cm³, temperature 15.7 million K. The radiative zone extends from the core to 0.7 solar radii, where the tachocline is located — a layer of sharp change in rotation velocity, important for magnetic field generation. Above is the convective zone, reaching the photosphere and manifesting as granulation. Above the photosphere — the chromosphere about 2000 km thick and the corona with a temperature around 2 million K, whose heating is likely provided by magnetic waves and field line reconnection. The solar wind carries away mass at a rate of about 10⁻¹⁴ solar masses per year. The star's activity is described by the 11-year Schwabe cycle, governed by magnetic dynamo processes. The spectrum is close to blackbody radiation with an effective temperature of ~5777 K, but contains Fraunhofer absorption lines. The total luminosity is 3.828×10²⁶ W. The Sun is located in the Gould Belt, about 8 kiloparsecs from the Galactic center, and moves in orbit at a speed of about 220 km/s.
sun.history: The Sun has been an object of worship and study since ancient times. A scientific breakthrough occurred in 1814 when Joseph von Fraunhofer systematically described the dark lines in the solar spectrum. In 1925, Cecilia Payne-Gaposchkin, applying ionization theory, proved that the Sun and stars consist mainly of hydrogen. The mechanism of thermonuclear fusion was revealed by Hans Bethe and Carl von Weizsäcker in the 1930s. In the second half of the 20th century, helioseismology allowed probing the Sun's interior, and spacecraft studied the solar wind and corona.
sun.how_it_works: The Sun's equilibrium is described by the hydrostatic equation: dP/dr = -G M(r) ρ / r². The rate of thermonuclear reactions strongly depends on temperature: for the proton-proton chain ε ∝ ρ T⁴. The energy release in the core is about 0.3 μW/cm³. In the radiative zone, energy is transported by radiation (opacity coefficient κ), in the convective zone by convection, according to the Schwarzschild criterion. The magnetic field is generated in the tachocline through the Ω-effect, and the α-effect is realized by turbulent convection. Flares are explained by magnetic reconnection — the rapid release of energy, described by magnetohydrodynamics. Doppler measurements allow studying surface motion and internal oscillations. The radiation spectrum is modeled by Planck's law with corrections for chemical composition and opacity. Detection of solar neutrinos confirms the models of thermonuclear burning.
key_problems: The solar neutrino problem: partly solved by the discovery of oscillations, but discrepancies remain in the low-energy region.
The mechanism of solar corona heating to millions of degrees is not fully established.
The nature of the 11-year activity cycle: why it is stable and whether future cycles can be predicted.
sun.fun_fact: The solar corona's temperature exceeds 2 million degrees, which is more than 200 times hotter than the photosphere. This contradicts everyday experience: moving away from a heat source, we expect cooling, but here the outer layers of the star turn out to be much hotter than the inner ones.

raw.mini: A giant thermonuclear reactor 150 million kilometers from us, whose radiation is the cause of almost all energy on Earth.
raw.practical_application: The basis of climate, photosynthesis, and all renewable energy; solar panels convert its light into electricity.
raw.description_popular: The Sun is a yellow dwarf of spectral class G2V, located at the center of the Solar System. Its mass is about 2×10³⁰ kg. The source of its energy is thermonuclear reactions in the core, where the temperature reaches 15 million K. The main cycle is the proton-proton cycle, converting hydrogen into helium-4 with the release of neutrinos and gamma rays. Energy is transferred outward first by radiation, and in the upper third by convection. The visible surface, the photosphere, has a temperature of 5778 K and is covered with granulation. Above it lie the chromosphere and corona, heated to millions of degrees — the mechanism of this heating is not yet fully clear. The Sun has an 11-year activity cycle, manifested in the number of sunspots and flares.
raw.fun_fact_popular: The Sun loses mass at a rate of about 4 million tons per second due to radiation and the solar wind, but even over billions of years this will amount to only a fraction of a percent.
raw.description_simple: The Sun is a star, a hot gas ball that shines thanks to the conversion of hydrogen into helium in its core. The surface temperature is about 5500 °C, and in the interior — millions of degrees. The Sun is 109 times wider than Earth and accounts for 99.8% of the entire Solar System's mass. Light from it takes 8 minutes to reach us. Without the Sun, there would be no day, no weather, no life.
raw.history_simple: People have worshipped the Sun since ancient times. In the 19th century, scientists began to understand its nature: Joseph Fraunhofer discovered lines in the Sun's spectrum, and later Cecilia Payne-Gaposchkin proved that it consists mainly of hydrogen. In the 20th century, physicists figured out the mechanism of its glow — thermonuclear fusion.
raw.how_it_works_simple: In the solar core, under enormous pressure and temperature, hydrogen nuclei fuse, forming helium. This releases energy, which gradually works its way to the surface and escapes into space as light and heat. It's like a huge furnace that has been 'burning' for 4.6 billion years and will burn for about as long again.
raw.description: The Sun is a main-sequence star 4.57 billion years old, in hydrostatic equilibrium: gravity is balanced by gas and radiation pressure. Thermonuclear fusion in the core proceeds via the proton-proton chain, partly via the CNO cycle (about 1% of energy). Central density ≈150 g/cm³, temperature 1.57×10⁷ K. The radiative zone extends from the core to 0.7 solar radii, where the tachocline is located — a region of sharp change in rotation velocity, playing a key role in magnetic field generation. The convective zone reaches the photosphere; its upper boundary is visible as granules. Above the photosphere — the chromosphere (thickness ~2000 km) and corona with a temperature ~2 million K, heated probably by magnetic waves and field line reconnection. The solar wind — a stream of charged particles, carrying away mass at a rate of ~10⁻¹⁴ M☉/year. Activity follows the 11-year Schwabe cycle, modulated by dynamo processes. The spectrum is close to blackbody radiation with T_eff ≈ 5777 K, but with Fraunhofer absorption lines. Total luminosity — 3.828×10²⁶ W. The Sun is in the Gould Belt, about 8 kpc from the Galactic center, rotating at ~220 km/s.
raw.history: The Sun has been an object of worship and study since antiquity. A scientific breakthrough was made by Joseph von Fraunhofer, who in 1814 systematically described the dark lines in the solar spectrum. In 1925, Cecilia Payne-Gaposchkin, using ionization theory, proved that the Sun and stars are predominantly composed of hydrogen. The physics of thermonuclear fusion was revealed by Hans Bethe and Carl von Weizsäcker in the 1930s. In the second half of the 20th century, helioseismology allowed peering into the interior, and spacecraft investigated the solar wind and corona.
raw.how_it_works: The Sun's equilibrium obeys the hydrostatic equilibrium equation dP/dr = -G M(r) ρ / r². Thermonuclear reactions are described by rates that strongly depend on temperature: for the pp-chain ε ∝ ρ T⁴. Energy release in the core is on the order of 0.3 μW/cm³. Energy transport: in the radiative zone — radiative heat conduction with opacity κ, in the convective zone — convection, obeying the Schwarzschild criterion. Magnetic field generation occurs in the tachocline via the Ω-effect, and the α-effect is realized by turbulent convection. Flare activity is associated with magnetic reconnection: rapid energy release described by magnetohydrodynamic equations. The Doppler effect allows measuring radial velocities of the surface and internal oscillations. The energy distribution of radiation is modeled by Planck's law taking into account chemical composition and opacity. Solar neutrinos are detected on Earth, confirming the burning models.
key_problems: Solar neutrino problem (partially solved by the discovery of oscillations, but discrepancies remain in low-energy spectra).
Heating of the solar corona to millions of degrees — the exact mechanism is not established.
The 11-year activity cycle: why it is so stable, and prediction of future cycles.
raw.fun_fact: The solar corona is so hot that it emits X-rays, and its temperature is more than 200 times higher than the temperature of the visible surface — this defies the intuition of ordinary cooling with distance from a heat source.

wormhole.name: Wormhole
wormhole.mini: A short tunnel in spacetime connecting distant corners of the Universe, like a burrow piercing a folded sheet.
wormhole.practical_application: So far pure theory, but wormhole research advances quantum gravity and may one day underlie superluminal communications or travel.
wormhole.description_popular: A wormhole is a hypothetical structure allowed by general relativity that connects two points in spacetime like a handle. It could link distant regions of the same universe or different universes. To keep the hole open for passage, exotic matter with negative pressure is needed, violating the usual energy conditions.
wormhole.fun_fact_popular: If you looked through a wormhole, you could see a distorted, lens-like but recognizable image of the other end — perhaps a star or galaxy that would otherwise take millions of years to reach.
wormhole.description_simple: A wormhole is like a tunnel through a mountain that connects two sides directly, without a detour. In space, such a tunnel would link distant points of spacetime (the unified fabric of space and time). The entrances would look like black spheres, but inside would be the shortest route.
wormhole.history_simple: The idea appeared almost immediately after the creation of general relativity: in 1916, Ludwig Flamm found something like a tunnel in Einstein's equations. Later, Einstein and Rosen described a 'bridge', and the name 'wormhole' was given by physicist John Wheeler in 1957.
wormhole.how_it_works_simple: Imagine a two-dimensional world as a sheet of paper. Curving it, you can punch a hole and connect two distant points with a short tube — that's how a wormhole creates a tunnel in spacetime. To prevent the tunnel walls from collapsing, exotic matter with negative pressure (pushing apart rather than attracting) is needed.
wormhole.description: In general relativity, a wormhole is a non-singular solution with a throat connecting two asymptotically flat regions. The Einstein–Rosen bridge is not traversable due to the event horizon. Traversable wormholes were constructed by Morris and Thorne in 1988: their metric contains a shape function b(r) and a redshift function Φ(r), with b(r₀)=r₀ at the throat. Matter that violates the weak energy condition is required; this is impossible in classical physics, but quantum effects (e.g., vacuum fluctuations) could provide negative energy.
wormhole.history: In 1916, Ludwig Flamm, interpreting the Schwarzschild solution, proposed the idea of a 'wormhole'. Einstein and Rosen in 1935 described the bridge as a geometrized model of an elementary particle. The term 'wormhole' was introduced by John Archibald Wheeler in 1957 in the context of quantum foam. Modern interest revived after the paper by Morris and Thorne, which showed the possibility of travel provided exotic matter exists.
wormhole.how_it_works: Einstein's equations link geometry (the Einstein tensor Gμν) to matter (the energy-momentum tensor Tμν). For a wormhole, the metric is given as ds² = -e^{2Φ(r)}c² dt² + dr²/(1 - b(r)/r) + r² dΩ². The absence of a horizon requires Φ(r) to be finite everywhere. Traversability imposes conditions on b(r) and Φ(r), and also requires violation of the null energy condition (negative energy). Quantum effects, such as the Casimir effect, provide such a possibility.
key_problems: The possible form of exotic matter suitable for stabilizing the wormhole is unknown
Travel through the wormhole could lead to causality violation (a time machine)
Quantum instability and Hawking radiation could destroy the wormhole
wormhole.fun_fact: A radio signal that passes through a wormhole would reach its destination faster than a signal sent through ordinary space. However, the speed of light is not locally exceeded: the path through the wormhole is simply shorter.

formulas.description: Metric of a traversable wormhole (Morris-Thorne)
formulas.latex: ds^2 = -c^2 e^{2\Phi(r)} dt^2 + \frac{dr^2}{1 - b(r)/r} + r^2 (d\theta^2 + \sin^2\theta d\phi^2)
formulas.meaning: ds — spacetime interval, c — speed of light in vacuum (≈3×10⁸ m/s), Φ(r) — redshift function (determines gravitational time dilation), b(r) — shape function (b(r₀)=r₀ at the throat), r — radial coordinate (circumference 2πr), t — time coordinate, θ and φ — angular coordinates on the sphere

raw.mini: A spatial tunnel, an instantaneous transition between distant worlds, like a hidden path through a fold of a piece of paper.
raw.practical_application: So far pure theory, but wormhole research advances quantum gravity and may one day underlie superluminal communications or travel.
raw.description_popular: A wormhole is a hypothetical solution of Einstein's equations describing a topological handle in spacetime. It can connect two distant regions of the same universe or even different universes. For a traversable wormhole to exist, violation of the energy condition is necessary, which requires the existence of exotic matter with negative pressure.
raw.fun_fact_popular: If you look through a wormhole, you could see a distorted but recognizable image of the other end — perhaps a star or galaxy that would otherwise take millions of years to reach.
raw.description_simple: A wormhole is like an underground passage drilled straight through a mountain instead of a detour on the surface. In space, it would be a tunnel connecting two points in spacetime (the unified fabric of space and time). The entrances of such a hole would look like black spheres, but inside — a short path.
raw.history_simple: The idea appeared almost immediately after the creation of general relativity: in 1916, Ludwig Flamm found something like a tunnel in Einstein's equations. Later, Einstein and Rosen described a 'bridge', and the name 'wormhole' was given by physicist John Wheeler.
raw.how_it_works_simple: If you take a sheet of paper — our space — and fold it, you can poke through with a pencil to get from one hole to the other faster than crawling along the sheet. The nature of such a puncture requires special matter with repulsive force (exotic matter) so that the tunnel does not collapse.
raw.description: In general relativity, a wormhole is defined as a non-singular manifold possessing a throat connecting two asymptotically flat regions. The simplest solution — the Einstein-Rosen bridge — is not traversable due to the event horizon. Traversable wormholes were first constructed by Michael Morris and Kip Thorne in 1988: their metric contains a shape function b(r) and a redshift function Φ(r), with b(r₀)=r₀ at the throat. It requires matter that violates the weak energy condition, which is impossible in classical physics but could be realized by quantum effects, such as vacuum fluctuations.
raw.history: As early as 1916, Ludwig Flamm, interpreting the Schwarzschild solution, proposed the idea of a 'wormhole', but without details. Einstein and Rosen in 1935 described the bridge as a geometrized model of an elementary particle. The term 'wormhole' was introduced by John Archibald Wheeler in 1957 in the context of quantum foam of spacetime. Modern interest revived after the paper by Morris and Thorne, which showed the possibility of using them for travel provided exotic matter exists.
raw.how_it_works: Einstein's equations link spacetime geometry (the Einstein tensor Gμν) with matter (the energy-momentum tensor Tμν). For a wormhole, the metric is given as ds² = -e^{2Φ(r)}c² dt² + dr²/(1 - b(r)/r) + r² dΩ². The absence of a horizon requires Φ(r) to be finite everywhere. Traversability imposes conditions on b(r) and Φ(r), and also requires violation of the null energy condition, i.e., the presence of matter with negative energy. Quantum effects such as the Casimir effect provide such a possibility.
key_problems: The possible form of exotic matter suitable for stabilizing the wormhole is unknown
Travel through the wormhole could lead to causality violation (a time machine)
Quantum instability and Hawking radiation could destroy the wormhole
raw.fun_fact: A radio signal sent into one end of a wormhole would exit the other end earlier than a signal going around, but not faster than light in the local sense — the speed of light is not violated.

quantum_superposition.name: Quantum superposition
quantum_superposition.mini: A quantum particle can simultaneously exist in multiple states, like a spinning coin on its edge — until it falls, it's both heads and tails.
quantum_superposition.practical_application: Quantum computers build superposition into the basis of computation: a qubit in a superposition of zero and one processes both values at once. This speeds up solving problems, such as modeling drugs or optimizing large systems.
quantum_superposition.description_popular: Quantum superposition is not just a mix of pre-existing states, but a fundamental ability of nature to form their coherent combinations. Formally, such a state is written as a sum of basis vectors with complex weights: |ψ⟩ = c₁|φ₁⟩ + c₂|φ₂⟩ + ... . The probability of finding the system in a particular state after measurement is equal to the square of the modulus of the corresponding weight. That is why in the double-slit experiment, the particle demonstrates interference, as if passing through both slits at once. Superposition is preserved only with complete isolation from the environment; interaction with the surroundings destroys coherence, causing it to quickly vanish in macroscopic objects.
quantum_superposition.fun_fact_popular: A quantum bit, or qubit, in a superposition of 0 and 1 is capable of processing both states in parallel. This gives quantum computers an exponential advantage over classical ones in certain computational tasks.
quantum_superposition.description_simple: Quantum superposition can be compared to a coin in flight: while it is spinning, you cannot say whether it will land heads or tails — both outcomes coexist. Similarly, a microparticle before measurement can combine several different states. After measurement, this uncertainty disappears — the state 'collapses' to one specific result. This behavior is called quantum superposition (from Latin superpositio — placing over). It follows from the basic equation of quantum mechanics — the Schrödinger equation: if two states are possible, then any combination of them is also possible.
quantum_superposition.history_simple: Formally, the principle of superposition of states was introduced in 1926 by Austrian physicist Erwin Schrödinger as a consequence of his famous wave equation. Before that, the phenomenon of superposition was well known for classical waves, but Schrödinger was the first to suggest that particles could behave similarly. Later, he proposed a thought experiment with a cat that is simultaneously alive and dead to demonstrate the strangeness of this idea applied to a large object.
quantum_superposition.how_it_works_simple: When ripples from two thrown stones spread across water, they overlay each other, creating an alternation of reinforced and weakened wavelets. Similarly, a quantum state can be a mixture of several wave 'images' at once. In isolation, a particle remains in such a blurred form. However, upon measurement, for example, of its energy or position, the mixture instantaneously turns into one definite value — this is called the collapse of superposition.
quantum_superposition.description: Quantum superposition is a central concept of quantum mechanics, stating that any linear combination of allowed states of a system is also an allowed state. In the mathematical apparatus of the theory, this is expressed through vectors in Hilbert space: if |φ₁⟩ and |φ₂⟩ are possible states, then the state α|φ₁⟩ + β|φ₂⟩ with complex α, β is physically realizable up to the moment of measurement. The principle of superposition underlies practically all non-classical effects: quantum interference, entanglement, tunneling, and the operation of quantum algorithms. After measurement, the system irreversibly transitions to one of the basis states with a probability equal to the square of the modulus of the corresponding amplitude; this process is called wave function reduction or collapse. Decoherence, caused by inevitable interaction with the environment, quickly suppresses the coherence of macroscopic superpositions, explaining why we do not observe them in everyday life.
quantum_superposition.history: The idea of superposition was born from attempts to reconcile the wave behavior of matter, predicted by Louis de Broglie in 1924, with the particle picture. Erwin Schrödinger in 1926 constructed a wave equation for which, as with any linear differential equations, the sum of solutions is also a solution — thus the formal principle of superposition of states arose. In 1935, he also devised the paradox of 'Schrödinger's cat', demonstrating the problem of the transition from the micro-world to the macro-world. John Stewart Bell in 1964 showed that superposition leads to statistical correlations that cannot be explained classically (Bell's theorem); later, laboratory tests, initiated by Alain Aspect, brilliantly confirmed this prediction. Since the end of the 20th century, superposition has been actively used in quantum computing; the pioneer of this direction was David Deutsch, who described the universal quantum computer in 1985.
quantum_superposition.how_it_works: The state of a system is encoded as a vector |ψ⟩ in Hilbert space. According to the superposition principle, any linear combination of the form Σ c_i |φ_i⟩, where {|φ_i⟩} is a basis, and c_i are complex amplitudes, describes a physically realizable state. Time evolution obeys the Schrödinger equation, which preserves superposition: if the initial state was a superposition, then the subsequent state will remain a superposition as long as the system is isolated. Measurement, on the contrary, irreversibly projects the vector onto one of the eigenstates of the measured quantity; the probabilities are determined by the squared moduli of the amplitudes. In real conditions, the inevitable coupling to the heat bath entangles the system with a huge number of environmental degrees of freedom, quickly destroying coherence (decoherence) — that is why macroscopic superpositions are extremely difficult to create and maintain.
key_problems: The measurement problem: why does the wave function collapse, and does it happen objectively?
Decoherence: how to isolate quantum systems to preserve their coherence?
The quantum-classical transition: where is the boundary beyond which superpositions cease to exist?
Creation and control of multi-qubit superpositions for fault-tolerant quantum computing.
quantum_superposition.fun_fact: Thanks to superposition, a particle can seep through an energy barrier that it classically cannot overcome — this is the tunneling effect. It explains how scanning tunneling microscopes work, which can 'see' individual atoms.

formulas.description: General form of a quantum superposition
formulas.meaning: |ψ⟩ — state vector of the system; c_i — complex amplitudes, the squared modulus |c_i|² gives the probability of finding the system in the basis state |φ_i⟩; |φ_i⟩ — orthonormal basis in Hilbert space.

raw.mini: A quantum particle can simultaneously be in all possible states, like a spinning top that before stopping contains all directions of falling.
raw.practical_application: Quantum computers use superposition for instantaneous enumeration of a huge number of options, speeding up codebreaking and the search for new drugs.
raw.description_popular: Quantum superposition is not just a mix of ready-made realities, but a fundamental property of micro-objects to form coherent combinations of states. Formally, it is written as a sum of basis vectors with complex weights: |ψ⟩ = c₁|φ₁⟩ + c₂|φ₂⟩ + ... . The squares of the moduli of these weights give the probabilities of finding the corresponding state upon measurement. That is why a particle seems to pass through both slits at once in an interference experiment, and on the screen we see a pattern typical of waves. It is important that superposition is preserved only in complete isolation from the external environment; interaction with it destroys coherence — that's how microscopic 'fuzziness' disappears in large, warm objects.
raw.fun_fact_popular: A quantum bit, or qubit, in a superposition of states 0 and 1 is capable of processing both values in parallel. It is this property that gives quantum computers an exponential advantage over classical ones in certain tasks.
raw.description_simple: Imagine a coin that in mid-air is neither heads nor tails, but both at once. Similarly, an electron in an atom can have several different energies simultaneously, until we try to measure it. At that moment, the state 'collapses' into one definite one. This ability was called quantum superposition (from Latin superpositio — placing over). It naturally follows from the main law of the quantum world — the Schrödinger equation: if a system has two allowed states, then any mixture (sum) of them is also allowed.
raw.history_simple: In 1926, Austrian physicist Erwin Schrödinger derived an equation that describes how a microparticle behaves. The idea of superposition in physics existed earlier — waves could overlay each other, but Schrödinger was the first to say that 'particles' could do the same. The famous thought experiment about a cat that is alive and dead at the same time was invented by him to show how strange this idea seems for the ordinary world.
raw.how_it_works_simple: When waves from two pebbles meet on a pond's surface, they overlay, creating areas with intensified and weakened ripples. So too, a quantum state can be a mixture of several wave 'images' at once. As long as there is no interaction with the surroundings, the particle is in this blurred, multi-faced form. But as soon as we ask a specific question (measure energy, position, momentum), the wave mixture instantly turns into one single answer — the collapse of superposition occurs.

nucleosynthesis.name: nucleosynthesis
nucleosynthesis.mini: All elements heavier than hydrogen and helium were forged in stellar furnaces and supernova explosions: from the carbon in muscles to the gold in jewelry — our body and the world around us literally consist of stardust.
nucleosynthesis.practical_application: Understanding the origin of elements and their abundance; dating the age of stellar populations; technologies using element synthesis (nuclear energy, medical isotopes).
nucleosynthesis.description_popular: Nucleosynthesis is the set of nuclear processes leading to the formation of chemical elements. Primordial nucleosynthesis (the first few minutes after the Big Bang) produced hydrogen (~75% by mass), helium (~25%), and traces of lithium. Stellar nucleosynthesis occurs in two stages: burning of light elements (up to iron) in stellar cores and explosive synthesis (heavier than iron) during supernovae and neutron star mergers. Main reactions: pp-chain, CNO cycle, triple-alpha process, s-process (slow neutron capture), r-process (rapid neutron capture).
nucleosynthesis.fun_fact_popular: The element technetium, which has no stable isotopes and is almost absent on Earth, has been detected in the spectra of giant stars — this is direct evidence that stars continuously produce new nuclei.
nucleosynthesis.description_simple: Stars are giant thermonuclear reactors. In their interiors, from hydrogen nuclei (protons) heavier and heavier elements are assembled: helium, carbon, oxygen, and so on up to iron. This process is like stringing beads onto a thread. When a star ends its life in an explosion, it scatters the created elements into space, from which new stars, planets, and even living organisms eventually form.
nucleosynthesis.history_simple: In the 1940s–1950s, scientists George Gamow, Ralph Alpher and others proposed that the elements originated in the hot early universe. But it later turned out that most elements were created in stars. In 1957, the famous B²FH paper was published, which described in detail the processes of stellar nucleosynthesis.
nucleosynthesis.how_it_works_simple: In the center of a star, temperatures of millions of degrees cause nuclei to collide and fuse, releasing energy, like compressed springs releasing their stored force when joined together. The star successively burns hydrogen, then helium, carbon and so on, until iron is formed. Iron is 'nuclear ash', further fusion yields no energy. Then the core collapses, and the star explodes, creating elements heavier than iron.
nucleosynthesis.description: Nucleosynthesis encompasses the entire history of element formation in the universe. Cosmological nucleosynthesis (BBN) took place at a temperature of ~10^9 K and a baryon density of ~10^-5 g/cm³ during the first 3 minutes, synthesizing mainly ^4He, ^2D, ^3He, ^7Li. The relative abundance of elements is determined by the expansion rate and the baryon-to-photon ratio. Stellar nucleosynthesis begins on the main sequence with hydrogen burning via pp-chains (energy release ~26.73 MeV) or the CNO cycle (at higher temperatures). Then follow the stages of helium burning (triple-alpha process: 3 ^4He → ^12C + γ, then ^12C + ^4He → ^16O), carbon, neon, oxygen, silicon burning, up to the formation of an iron core. All reactions proceed under gravitational confinement, and energy is released due to the mass defect (E=mc^2). Explosive nucleosynthesis during core collapse or thermonuclear explosion of white dwarfs produces iron-peak elements and rapid neutron capture (r-process), explaining the origin of elements heavier than iron (gold, uranium, thorium). Slow neutron capture (s-process) occurs in the envelopes of asymptotic giant branch stars.
nucleosynthesis.history: In the 1920s, Aston and Eddington proposed that the energy source of stars is nuclear reactions. In the 1940s, Bethe and von Weizsäcker discovered the CNO cycle. In 1948, Gamow and Alpher developed the hot universe model, predicting the cosmic microwave background and primordial nucleosynthesis. The key B²FH paper (Burbidge, Burbidge, Fowler, Hoyle) in 1957 systematized the processes of stellar nucleosynthesis, including s- and r-processes. For this research Fowler received the Nobel Prize in 1983.
nucleosynthesis.how_it_works: Nuclear reactions are initiated by the tunnel effect (quantum penetration through the Coulomb barrier). In stars, the weak interaction is essential, converting protons into neutrons, enabling the synthesis chains. Reaction rates are computed through cross-sections averaged over the Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution (Gamow peak). The released energy counteracts gravity, creating hydrostatic equilibrium. As fuel is exhausted, the core contracts and heats up, triggering the next stage. For the synthesis of elements heavier than iron, an external source of neutrons is required; the s-process occurs at low neutron density (~10^8 cm⁻³), the r-process during an explosion, when the neutron flux is enormous (~10^20–10^30 cm⁻³). The equivalence of mass and energy E=mc^2 determines the energetics.
key_problems: The cosmological lithium problem: the predicted abundance of ^7Li is 3–4 times higher than observed in old stars.
Precise rates of key reactions: for example, ^12C(α,γ)^16O, which determines the ratio of carbon to oxygen in the universe.
Astrophysical sites of the r-process: the contribution of supernovae and neutron star mergers is not yet fully clear.
Nucleosynthesis in the first generation of stars (Population III): how elements formed in the absence of metals.
nucleosynthesis.fun_fact: The most abundant isotope in the universe is hydrogen-1, but the second most abundant is helium-4, and carbon-12 owes its abundance to a fortuitous resonance in the triple-alpha process, predicted by Fred Hoyle (the Hoyle state).

formulas.description: Triple-alpha process: three helium-4 nuclei fuse into an excited state of carbon-12, which then transitions to the ground state, emitting a photon.
formulas.meaning: Three helium-4 nuclei (^4He) fuse to form a carbon-12 nucleus (^{12}C) and a gamma ray (γ), releasing energy of 7.275 MeV.

The pinnacle was the B²FH paper (Burbidge, Burbidge, Fowler, Hoyle) of 1957, which classified the processes: they introduced the concepts of s- and r-processes, e-process (equilibrium), etc. William Fowler received the Nobel Prize (1983) for this research. planetary_migration.name: planetary migration
planetary_migration.mini: Planets are not rigidly fixed to their orbits: gravitational tides from the gas disk can drag them away from or toward the star, like a boat changing course under the influence of a current.
planetary_migration.practical_application: Explanation of the architecture of exoplanetary systems (hot Jupiters, compact super-Earth systems); assessment of the habitability of planets that ended up in the habitable zone due to migration.
planetary_migration.description_popular: Planetary migration is a change in the orbital distance of a planet from its star due to gravitational torques from the protoplanetary disk. For terrestrial-type planets (mass up to ~10 M⊕), type I migration is typical: the body excites spiral density waves in the disk, creating a braking torque. More massive giant planets clear a ring-shaped gap in the disk and migrate via type II, following the viscous evolution of the gas. An additional mechanism is dynamical scattering of planets by each other after the disk dissipates.
planetary_migration.fun_fact_popular: Simulations show that in the Solar System, Jupiter first migrated inward to the orbit of Mars and then turned back due to resonance with Saturn — this 'stirred up' the asteroid belt and likely prevented super-Earths from forming.
planetary_migration.description_simple: Young planets move through the gas disk in which they formed, like beads in viscous porridge. The gravity of the gas pulls them, causing them to shift from their original orbits. Some planets move far from the star, others approach very closely and become 'hot Jupiters'.
planetary_migration.history_simple: Scientists suspected migration back in the 1970s, but the decisive moment came with the discovery in 1995 of the planet 51 Pegasi b — a hot Jupiter that could not have formed so close to the star. Since then, migration has become a key part of planet formation theories.
planetary_migration.how_it_works_simple: A planet's gravity perturbs the surrounding gas, generating spiral waves, like the wake of an oar in water. These waves sap energy from the planet and force it to migrate inward or outward. In a viscous disk, the planet drifts along with the gas, like a chip carried by a current.
planetary_migration.description: Planetary migration is a fundamental process in the evolution of planetary systems, whereby a planet changes its orbital semi-major axis due to angular momentum exchange with the gas-dust disk or other bodies. Mechanisms: type I migration (Lindblad and corotation torques from spiral waves excited by the planet; leads to rapid inward migration for bodies up to 10 M⊕); type II migration (a planet more massive than ~1 MJup opens a gap and migrates along with the accreting gas of the viscous disk); fast type III migration (in massive disks for intermediate-mass planets). Gravitational interaction of planets after disk dissipation also leads to orbital evolution (planet-planet scattering, secular resonances, Kozai-Lidov effect for inclined orbits). Observational evidence: hot Jupiters, resonant chains, misaligned orbits.
planetary_migration.history: The concept of migration dates back to the 1970s: Goldreich and Tremaine proposed it to explain the dynamics of giant planets. Wide acceptance came after the discovery of exoplanets, primarily 51 Pegasi b (1995). Key researchers include Papaloizou, Lin, Mordasini, Kley, and others. Modern hydrodynamic simulations, developing since the 2000s, have allowed a detailed description of migration types.
planetary_migration.how_it_works: It is based on the gravitational interaction of the planet with the disk. The planet excites spiral density waves in the disk at inner and outer Lindblad resonances. The transfer of angular momentum by these waves leads to braking of the planet (inward migration). Corotation torques (from gas on orbits close to the planet) can slow or reverse migration. Once the planet opens a gap (criterion: its tidal force exceeds viscous stresses), viscous evolution of the disk comes into play, and the planet drifts with the accretion flow. After the gas disappears, planet-planet scattering dominates. The law of conservation of angular momentum is key. The process is described by the equations of hydrodynamics and gravity, often using numerical modeling due to nonlinearity.
key_problems: Accurate prediction of type I migration speed: linear theory predicts too rapid migration, incompatible with planet formation (the 'fast migration' problem).
Influence of turbulence and magnetic fields on migration in real disks.
Stopping migration: why don't hot Jupiters fall into the star? Possible mechanisms: disk truncation by photoevaporation, magnetic cavities.
Migration in multi-planet systems: how resonances and mutual perturbations alter scenarios.
planetary_migration.fun_fact: The hot Jupiter WASP-12b is so close to its star that tidal forces have stretched its shape, and it is losing material, forming a gas cloud — this is the final stage of migration, leading to the planet's destruction.

Type I migration (for low-mass terrestrial planets) is distinguished, where the planet creates density waves in the disk that exert a negative torque on it, and type II migration (for giants capable of clearing a ring-shaped gap), in which the planet is tied to the viscous evolution of the disk. There is also migration caused by planets scattering each other (planet-planet scattering).
