quantum_error_correction.name: quantum error correction
quantum_error_correction.mini: Quantum data, fragile as a soap bubble, is safeguarded through encoding: an error doesn't reveal the secret but hints at a way to correct it — like a margin note that doesn't disclose the text.
quantum_error_correction.practical_application: The foundation of reliable quantum computers; without it, algorithms like Shor's factoring would drown in noise.
quantum_error_correction.description_popular: Quantum error correction is a set of methods that protect logical qubits (units of quantum information) from noise and decoherence (loss of quantum properties) by distributing information across redundant physical qubits. Unlike classical copying, nonlocal correlations are used here. Errors are detected by measuring stabilizers—special operators for which the code state is an eigenstate; such measurement does not destroy the encoded superposition. The simplest Shor code corrects any single error—bit flip or phase flip. The five-qubit code achieves the theoretical minimum for correcting a general error.
quantum_error_correction.fun_fact_popular: The smallest code that corrects any single error uses only five qubits – and this is the theoretical limit; no four-qubit code can do it.
quantum_error_correction.description_simple: Sending an important letter with a guarantee of recovery if letters get damaged is a classic example of protection. In the quantum world, qubits (analogs of bits capable of being in a superposition of 0 and 1 simultaneously) cannot be copied due to the no-cloning theorem. Therefore, information is distributed across several qubits, linking them through quantum entanglement (a correlation where a change in one instantly affects the other). An error disrupts the system's balance, and special checks (syndromes) detect the failure and indicate a corrective operation, without reading the message itself.
quantum_error_correction.history_simple: In 1995, Peter Shor proposed the first code, encoding one qubit into nine physical qubits and correcting any single error. Independently, Andrew Steane developed a seven-qubit code in 1996, confirming the feasibility of quantum correction.
quantum_error_correction.how_it_works_simple: Analogy with a triple lock. There are three copies of a key, and there is a concern that one might get damaged. Each key is not checked individually, because that would destroy the quantum information. Instead, consistency between the keys is compared: if a defect appears in one, the other two allow it to be corrected without revealing the original shape.
quantum_error_correction.description: Quantum error correction (QEC) is a fundamental technique that enables reliable storage and processing of information under decoherence. It is based on mapping the state of one logical qubit into a subspace of a larger number of physical qubits such that typical errors map states into orthogonal subspaces (error discretization). Detection is performed by projective measurement of the stabilizer group—an abelian subgroup of the Pauli group defining the code. The resulting syndrome indicates the type and location of the error without destroying the information; then a corrective operation is applied. The threshold theorem states that for error rates below a critical value, computations of arbitrary duration can be performed with exponential error suppression by increasing redundancy. Surface codes on two-dimensional lattices are popular due to local measurements and a high threshold (~1%).
quantum_error_correction.history: The concept arose from the realization of the fragility of quantum states and the impossibility of copying them (no-cloning theorem, 1982). In 1995, Peter Shor, using an analogy with classical codes and entanglement, proposed a nine-qubit code. Soon Andrew Steane developed a seven-qubit code based on linear algebra. Generalization led to the stabilizer formalism (Daniel Gottesman, 1996) and a family of codes, including topological ones, underlying fault-tolerant quantum computers.
quantum_error_correction.how_it_works: QEC uses code distance d: the minimum number of errors that turn one codeword into another. A code with distance d corrects any combination of errors of weight t ≤ (d−1)/2. Stabilizer formalism: the set S — an abelian subgroup of Pauli operators on n qubits without −I. The code space is the common eigenspace with eigenvalue +1. An error E either commutes or anticommutes with the generators; anticommutation changes the sign and is detected. Measuring the generators yields a syndrome, by which the error is recovered up to a stabilizer. Error correction condition: for any two correctable errors E_a, E_b, the projector P satisfies P E_a† E_b P = c_{ab} P. CSS codes are often used, combining classical codes for bit and phase flips.
key_problems: Enormous overheads: a logical qubit may require thousands of physical qubits
Creating and maintaining high-fidelity measurements with real-time feedback
Errors correlated in time and space violate model assumptions
Physical implementation of measurement circuits without introducing additional errors
quantum_error_correction.fun_fact: The surface code can be visualized as a two-dimensional grid of qubits with a checkerboard coloring; the error syndrome resembles a pattern of magnetic vortices, and correction reduces to finding the shortest chains on the grid.

raw.mini: Quantum data, fragile as a water bubble, is protected by encoding: an error under the microscope doesn't divulge the secret but merely indicates how to fix it – like a hint in the margin without reading the text.
raw.practical_application: The foundation of reliable quantum computers; without it, algorithms like Shor's factoring would drown in noise.
raw.description_popular: Quantum error correction is a set of schemes that protect logical qubits from inevitable noise and decoherence (loss of quantum properties) by distributing information across redundant physical qubits. Unlike classical multiple copying, the quantum code uses nonlocal correlations: errors are detected by measuring stabilizers (operators for which the code state is an eigenstate), which does not destroy the encoded superposition. The simplest Shor code corrects any single failure, be it a bit flip or phase flip, and codes with lower redundancy, such as the five-qubit code, achieve the smallest possible size for correcting a general error.
raw.fun_fact_popular: The smallest code that corrects any single error uses only five qubits – and this is the theoretical limit; no four-qubit code can do it.
raw.description_simple: Imagine that an important letter must be sent so that even if several letters are damaged, the message can be restored. In the classical world, copies can be made, but in the quantum world, qubits (quantum analogs of bits capable of being 0 and 1 simultaneously due to superposition) cannot be cloned because of a special prohibition (the no-cloning theorem). Therefore, quantum information is hidden not in one qubit but in several, linking them with entanglement (a mysterious connection where one instantaneously affects another). An error, such as a value flip, disturbs the balance in this group: special checks (syndromes) detect the failure and suggest an action for correction, without peeking into the message itself.
raw.history_simple: In 1995, mathematician Peter Shor found a way to encode a qubit into nine physical ones to correct any errors. Almost at the same time, Andrew Steane proposed a more compact seven-qubit code, proving that quantum correction is possible.
raw.how_it_works_simple: Analogy: a triple lock on a safe. Suppose you have three copies of a key, but you are afraid that one might break. You do not check each one separately (opening the lock would destroy the quantum secret), but you look at the consistency between them: do they match in pairs? If a crack appears in one, the other two will suggest how to fix it, without revealing the key's shape.
raw.description: Quantum error correction (QEC) is a fundamental technique that enables reliable storage and processing of information in a quantum computer, despite interaction with the environment causing decoherence. It is based on the idea of mapping the logical space of a single qubit into a subspace of states of a larger number of physical qubits such that typical errors map code vectors into orthogonal subspaces (error discretization). Error detection is carried out by projective measurement of the stabilizer group—an abelian subgroup of the Pauli group defining the code. The result of such measurement (syndrome) indicates the type and location of the error without destroying the quantum information; then a corrective operation is applied. The threshold theorem states that for a sufficiently low error rate on physical elements, computations can be performed for arbitrarily long times with exponential error suppression with a reasonable increase in overhead. Popular codes include surface codes on planar lattices, which require only local measurements and have a high threshold (~1%).
raw.history: The concept of quantum error correction arose from the realization of the fragility of quantum states and the apparent impossibility of copying them (no-cloning theorem, 1982). In 1995, Peter Shor, noticing an analogy between classical linear codes and quantum entanglement, proposed the first code correcting a single error using 9 qubits. Independently, Andrew Steane in 1996 developed a code based on ideas from linear algebra, reducing the number of qubits to 7. Further generalization led to the stabilizer formalism (Daniel Gottesman, 1996) and the creation of a whole family of codes, including topological ones, which underlie modern fault-tolerant quantum computer designs.
raw.how_it_works: QEC relies on a generalization of the classical code distance: the minimum number of errors that turn one codeword into another must be sufficiently large. Formally, a code with distance d corrects any combination of errors of weight t ≤ (d−1)/2. In practice, encoding is implemented through the stabilizer formalism: let S be an abelian subgroup of Pauli operators on n qubits not containing −I. The code space is the common eigenspace of all elements of S with eigenvalue +1. An error E either commutes or anticommutes with the generators of S; the latter changes the sign upon measurement and is detected. By measuring the generators, a syndrome is obtained, from which the error is uniquely recovered up to the stabilizer. The key requirement is the error correction condition: for any two correctable errors E_a, E_b, the projector onto the code space P satisfies P E_a† E_b P = c_{ab} P. For independent errors on individual qubits, Calderbank-Shor-Steane codes (CSS codes) are often used, combining classical codes for bit and phase errors.
key_problems: Enormous overheads: a logical qubit may require thousands of physical qubits
Creating and maintaining high-fidelity measurements with real-time feedback
Errors correlated in time and space violate model assumptions
Physical implementation of measurement circuits without introducing additional errors
raw.fun_fact: The surface code, one of the most promising, can be visualized as a two-dimensional grid of qubits with a checkerboard coloring; the error syndrome resembles a pattern of magnetic vortices, and their correction is mathematically equivalent to finding the shortest chains on the grid.

quantum_key_distribution.name: quantum key distribution
quantum_key_distribution.mini: A secret key woven from quanta of light: interception alters the photons, and the parties detect eavesdropping, growing a shared secret from the inviolability of quantum states.
quantum_key_distribution.practical_application: Protection of interbank transfers and government communications; prepares infrastructure for the era of quantum computers capable of breaking classical ciphers.
quantum_key_distribution.description_popular: Quantum key distribution (QKD) is a method of generating a shared secret key whose security is based on the laws of quantum mechanics rather than computational complexity. The BB84 protocol uses four photon states grouped into two non-orthogonal bases (rectilinear and diagonal). Non-orthogonal states cannot be distinguished with certainty, so interception introduces errors. After transmission, the parties perform bit reconciliation and privacy amplification to eliminate information leakage. Today, systems operate over optical fiber for distances up to hundreds of kilometers, and satellite communication extends the range to thousands.
quantum_key_distribution.fun_fact_popular: The first bank transfer protected by a quantum key took place in 2004 in Vienna; now quantum networks connect entire cities.
quantum_key_distribution.description_simple: Alice and Bob want to create a shared secret key over an open communication channel that Eve is eavesdropping on. Alice encodes bits into the polarization of single photons (the direction of wave oscillation) and sends them to Bob. For each photon, Bob randomly chooses one of two measurement bases (metaphorically 'questions'). After the exchange, they announce over the open channel which bases they used, without revealing the results themselves. Where the bases matched, the results become part of the key. If Eve attempts to measure the photons, she introduces errors that become apparent when comparing a control subset of bits.
quantum_key_distribution.history_simple: In the 1970s, Stephen Wiesner proposed the idea of quantum money that cannot be counterfeited. Quantum cryptography gained widespread recognition in 1984 after Charles Bennett and Gilles Brassard published the BB84 protocol.
quantum_key_distribution.how_it_works_simple: An analogy with two alphabets: one uses dots and dashes (vertical/horizontal polarization), the other uses zigzags (diagonal directions). Alice writes a bit with a random alphabet, and Bob reads it, also choosing an alphabet at random. If the alphabets match, the result is correct; if not, a random value is obtained. Then they exchange information about the chosen alphabets for each sent symbol, keeping bits only for cases of matching. Eve, not knowing the alphabet, disturbs the transmitted states, and her interference manifests as additional errors during verification.
quantum_key_distribution.description: Quantum key distribution is a cryptographic primitive that generates a key through the exchange of quantum states and guarantees unconditional security. Secrecy relies on the uncertainty principle: an eavesdropper cannot reliably distinguish non-orthogonal states, such as a photon with polarization 0° and 45°, without introducing errors when the basis is mismatched. In the BB84 protocol, Alice randomly selects a bit and a basis, sending one of four states; Bob measures in a random basis. After the sifting stage, bits with matching bases form the raw key. Estimating the quantum bit error rate (QBER) by sacrificing a portion of bits gives an upper bound on Eve’s information; if QBER is below the threshold, error correction and privacy amplification are performed to obtain the final key. Entanglement-based protocols (E91) verify the violation of Bell's inequality. The decoy state method protects against attacks on weakened laser pulses.
quantum_key_distribution.history: Quantum cryptography traces its origins to the concept of quantum money proposed by Stephen Wiesner in 1970 (published in 1983). In 1984, Charles Bennett and Gilles Brassard introduced the BB84 protocol, which became a turning point. In 1991, Artur Ekert proposed the E91 protocol based on entangled particles, opening up prospects for satellite communication. Today, QKD is a commercial technology with deployed networks.
quantum_key_distribution.how_it_works: The security of QKD stems from the disturbance of quantum states upon measurement (wavefunction collapse). If Eve guesses the basis, she introduces no errors, but she cannot guess correctly every time. For BB84, secure key extraction is possible when the quantum error rate e < 11%. Mathematically, after error correction and privacy amplification, the length of the final key is L = n·r, where n is the raw key size, r = 1 − h(e_x) − h(e_z) (asymptotic limit), h is binary entropy, and e_x, e_z are errors in the bases. Practical limitations include losses and detector noise.
key_problems: Range without quantum repeaters is limited by attenuation in optical fiber (≈100–200 km)
Vulnerabilities of real detectors (side-channel attacks, blinding)
Low key generation rate compared to classical methods
Integration into existing network infrastructures and high cost
quantum_key_distribution.fun_fact: The Micius satellite, launched in 2016, enabled the first intercontinental QKD between China and Europe using entangled photons from orbit.

formulas.description: Asymptotic fraction of the secret key in the BB84 protocol under collective attacks
formulas.meaning: r — fraction of bits that become secret key (dimensionless, 0 to 1); h(p) = -p \log_2 p - (1-p) \log_2 (1-p) — binary entropy; e_x, e_z — error probabilities in the X- and Z-bases, respectively (dimensionless, 0 to 0.5)

raw.mini: A secret key woven from light: any interference by an eavesdropper changes the photons, and the conversants immediately detect eavesdropping – the key grows from the inviolability of light.
raw.practical_application: Protection of interbank transfers and government communications; prepares infrastructure for the era of quantum computers capable of breaking classical ciphers.
raw.description_popular: Quantum key distribution (QKD) allows two parties to generate a shared secret key whose security is guaranteed by the laws of quantum mechanics rather than computational complexity. The BB84 protocol uses four photon states grouped into two non-orthogonal bases (e.g., rectilinear and diagonal). Since non-orthogonal states cannot be distinguished with certainty, any interception attempt inevitably introduces errors. After the quantum exchange, the parties perform reconciliation (bit alignment) and privacy amplification to minimize information leakage. Practical systems today operate over optical fiber at distances up to hundreds of kilometers, and satellite communication extends the range to thousands.
raw.fun_fact_popular: The first bank transfer protected by a quantum key took place in 2004 in Vienna, and today quantum networks connect entire cities.
raw.description_simple: Two people, Alice and Bob, want to agree on a secret password, but the malefactor Eve is eavesdropping. In the quantum world, light becomes the messenger – single photons. Alice encodes bits into different polarization states (wave direction) and sends them. Bob measures each photon, but does not know in advance which 'question' (basis) was used – he chooses randomly. Then, over an open phone line, they compare which questions matched and keep only the matching answers. If Eve tried to measure the photons, she would inevitably disturb the quantum state (since measurement in the quantum world is not just reading, but active intervention), and Alice and Bob would notice excess errors in a control sample. Thus a shared key is born, securely hidden from prying eyes.
raw.history_simple: Back in the 1970s, Stephen Wiesner conceived 'quantum money' that cannot be counterfeited thanks to quantum physics. But the idea gained fame in 1984 when Charles Bennett and Gilles Brassard published the BB84 protocol – a turning point for quantum cryptography.
raw.how_it_works_simple: Imagine two different alphabets: one uses dots and dashes (vertical/horizontal polarization), the other uses zigzags (diagonal directions). Alice writes a letter from either the first or the second alphabet at random. Bob reads, choosing an alphabet blindly. If he guessed the alphabet, the value is recorded correctly; if not, a random symbol results. Later, they tell each other which alphabet was used for each transmission (without revealing the symbols themselves!), and bits are kept only where the alphabets matched. Eve, not knowing the correct alphabet, inevitably spoils some symbols, and her presence is detected by unexpected mismatches during verification.
raw.description: Quantum key distribution is a cryptographic primitive in which a key is generated through the exchange of quantum states, invulnerable to attacks even in the presence of an arbitrarily powerful computational adversary (unconditional security). Secrecy derives from the uncertainty principle and the inviolability of non-orthogonal states: an eavesdropper cannot reliably distinguish, for example, a photon with polarization 0° and 45°, without introducing errors when the basis is mismatched. In the simplest realization of the BB84 protocol: Alice randomly selects a bit and a basis, sending one of four states. Bob measures in a random basis. After raw key sifting, the fraction of bits with matching bases serves as the future key. Estimating the quantum bit error rate (QBER) by sacrificing a portion of bits gives an upper bound on Eve's information; if QBER is below the threshold, error correction and hashing are applied to obtain the final secret key. Entanglement-based protocols (E91) use the verification of Bell's inequality to detect eavesdropping. Modern systems implement decoy state methods to protect against attacks on weakened laser pulses.
raw.history: Quantum cryptography was born from the work of Stephen Wiesner (around 1970), who proposed 'quantum money' that cannot be counterfeited. However, his paper was rejected and saw the light only in 1983. Inspired, Charles Bennett and Gilles Brassard developed the first key distribution protocol, known as BB84, presented at a conference in 1984. In 1991, Artur Ekert proposed an alternative protocol, E91, based on pairs of entangled particles, paving the way for communication via satellites. Since then, QKD has become a commercial technology with networks in China, Europe, and the USA.
raw.how_it_works: The security of QKD is based on the fact that any measurements performed by Eve inevitably disturb quantum states due to wavefunction collapse (a postulate of quantum mechanics). If Alice's and Bob's bases do not match, but Eve guessed the correct one, the attack introduces no errors, but she cannot guess correctly all the time. It has been established that a secret key can be safely extracted as long as the quantum error rate e < 11% for BB84. Mathematical model: after distribution, error correction, and privacy amplification, the final key length is L = n·r, where n is the number of raw bits, r = 1 − h(e_x) − h(e_z) in the asymptotically infinite limit, h is binary entropy, and e_x and e_z are errors in the two bases. For real systems, efficiency decreases due to channel loss and detector noise.
key_problems: Range without quantum repeaters is limited by attenuation in optical fiber (≈100–200 km)
Vulnerabilities of real detectors (side-channel attacks, blinding)
Low key generation rate compared to classical methods
Integration into existing network infrastructures and high cost
raw.fun_fact: The Micius satellite, launched by China in 2016, demonstrated intercontinental QKD between China and Europe using entangled photons transmitted from orbit.

void.name: void
void.mini: A cosmic void is a giant cavity in the distribution of galaxies, where matter is so sparse that light-years of space contain only a few stars.
void.practical_application: Studying voids reveals the mechanisms of large-scale structure formation in the Universe and helps understand how dark energy influences its expansion.
void.description_popular: Voids are extensive regions with a reduced density of galaxies, occupying about 80% of the Universe's volume but containing only a few percent of all galaxies. Their diameters range from 20 to 200 megaparsecs (65–650 million light-years). Due to their low density, voids expand faster than the surrounding space. They are not completely empty: inside, there are dwarf galaxies and clouds of cold gas, noticeable as absorption lines in the spectra of distant quasars (Lyman-alpha forest). The edges of voids are delineated by galactic filaments and clusters.
void.fun_fact_popular: The recently discovered KBC void is so large that its size is difficult to explain within the standard cosmological model — perhaps it points to unknown forces or special properties of dark energy.
void.description_simple: Galaxies in the Universe are not distributed evenly, but are gathered into thick filaments and walls, between which yawn almost empty regions — voids, resembling bubbles in cosmic foam. For example, the Boötes void is about 330 million light-years across, and it contains so few galaxies that there are only a few per billion cubic light-years.
void.history_simple: Voids were discovered in 1978 when compiling three-dimensional maps of galaxy distributions. It turned out that the large-scale structure of the Universe resembles a network with cells, rather than a homogeneous sea.
void.how_it_works_simple: After the Big Bang, dark matter under gravity began to clump together, dragging ordinary matter with it. Where dark matter was slightly less abundant, matter flowed away to denser regions, leaving behind rarefied bubbles — akin to water on a bumpy surface draining into hollows, leaving the high points dry.
void.description: A void is a large-scale region with below-average matter density (underdensity), identified by algorithms like VoidFinder from galaxy catalogs. Voids arise from primordial negative density fluctuations and evolve nonlinearly: they expand faster than the Hubble flow, and their boundaries become denser. The radial density profile is often described by a universal formula, close to a power law. Internal dynamics lead to a systematic blueshift of galaxies relative to the void center (void expansion effect). Voids serve as laboratories for testing gravitational theories: in modified models (f(R), MOND) different population and profiles are predicted. Voids are used to measure the integrated Sachs-Wolfe effect (delay in structure growth), which is sensitive to dark energy parameters and neutrino mass.
void.history: The idea of voids in the distribution of galaxies dates back to pioneering works on mapping the Universe in the 1970s (Chincarini, Gregory, Thompson). The term 'void' became established after analysis of the CfA Redshift Survey data in the 1980s, when the Great Wall and adjacent voids were discovered. In 1981, Kirshner and colleagues identified the Boötes void. Theoretical understanding was provided by the works of Sheth and van de Weygaert (2004), establishing statistical properties of voids in hierarchical models. Modern surveys SDSS (2000–) and DESI (2021–) have cataloged tens of thousands of voids.
void.how_it_works: Formation and dynamics of a void within the ΛCDM model. From linear theory, the growth of a density perturbation δ(t) ∝ D(t) depends on the cosmological growth factor. A void corresponds to δ < 0. Nonlinear evolution is described by the spherical model (analogous to collapse for overdensities). The equation of motion for a shell of radius R: \ddot{R}/R = -(4πG/3)(ρ_m + 3P_total/c²), where P_total includes the pressure of dark energy P_Λ = -ρ_Λ c². In the era of Λ dominance, the right-hand side becomes positive, which accelerates the void's expansion. Compensation: the excess density on the walls equals the deficit within some radius. Voids also allow testing the model through redshift-space distortions (Alcock-Paczynski test) and through weak lensing.
key_problems: The nature of anomalously large voids, such as KBC and Hyperion: consistency with ΛCDM.
Relation of galaxy populations in voids to neutrino mass and dark matter properties.
Using voids for independent measurement of the dark energy equation of state (w).
Testing modified gravity theories via density profiles and velocity fields in voids.
void.fun_fact: The largest known void, Hyperion, discovered in 2018, spans 1.5 billion light-years and is more of a system of several giant voids.

formulas.description: Evolution equation of a spherical shell in cosmology (for a void)
formulas.meaning: R — radius of the shell; G — gravitational constant (≈6.674×10⁻¹¹ m³/(kg·s²)); the sum is over all components of the Universe (matter, radiation, dark energy) with density ρ_i and pressure p_i; c — speed of light (≈3×10⁸ m/s). For Λ (dark energy) p_Λ = -ρ_Λ c², so the term in brackets becomes -2ρ_Λ, leading to accelerated expansion of the void.

raw.mini: A cosmic void is a bubble of near-absolute emptiness in the web of galaxies, where for hundreds of millions of light-years there are almost no stars. The most boring, yet also the most mysterious part of the Universe.
raw.practical_application: Studying voids helps understand how the large-scale structure of the cosmos formed and how dark energy drives the expansion of space between them.

They are not completely empty: they contain isolated dwarf galaxies and clouds of cold gas, visible as absorption lines in the spectra of distant quasars (the so-called Lyman-alpha forest). The boundaries of voids are outlined by galactic filaments and clusters.

They resemble holes in a cosmic sponge. The most famous such bubble — the Boötes void — has a diameter of about 330 million light-years. Inside it, there are extremely few galaxies — one can count only a few per billions of cubic light-years.
